All SECONDARY HOP per the standing caveat; the Union page (union-order-of-battle) verified serving the same B&M-type reproduction.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Henry J. Hunt's report (Chief of Artillery, AoP), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 228-243 (encyc.org full-text reproduction)
sources.author: Henry J. Hunt
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Full report text read at encyc.org (ehistory's serial 043 has a page gap over pp. ~228-244 - 'Page not found' - so the OR print pagination is via the civilwarcycling index). Dated Sept. 27, 1863. Key verbatim: the 10 a.m. July 3 instruction ('to fire deliberately, and to husband their ammunition as much as possible'); 'the enemy opened, at about 1 p. m.... from one hundred to one hundred and twenty' guns vs 'more than eighty to reply effectively'; 'About 2.30 p. m., finding our ammunition running low... I directed that the fire should be gradually stopped, which was done, and the enemy soon slackened his fire also'; 'About 3 p. m., and soon after the enemy's fire had ceased, he formed a column of attack'; II Corps batteries 'having nearly exhausted their supply of ammunition, except canister'; Rorty's/Arnold's/Cushing's batteries withdrawn 'toward the close'; army expenditure '32,781 rounds, averaging over 100 rounds per gun' of 270 carried; the army artillery casualty table (total 320 guns; 7+98 killed, 33+532 wounded, 67 missing, 881 horses) and the July 3 officer-loss list (Rorty, Cushing killed; Woodruff, Milne mortally wounded).

clockProfile.assessment: Three anchor statements: opening 'about 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07), cease order 'About 2.30 p. m.' (0 vs the adopted ~14:30 slackening HE caused), assault forming 'About 3 p. m.' (+5 vs the 15:05 step-off frame). Median +5, remarkably consistent - but the CA-J3A-4 comparison is partially circular (his order defines the event), so kind stays report-nominal and rule 4 applies.

sources.title: The Third Day at Gettysburg (Battles & Leaders vol. 3, pp. 369-385)
sources.author: Henry J. Hunt
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. TWO reproductions used, and the difference matters: the historycentral text is ABRIDGED (it omits the cease-fire ride); the full text was read from the archive.org B&L vol. 3 raw OCR (https://ia800502.us.archive.org/8/items/battlesleadersof03john/battlesleadersof03john_djvu.txt, pp. 374-375). Key verbatim: 'Between 10 and 11 A.M.... I crossed over to Cemetery Ridge'; the withhold order 'to withhold their fire for fifteen or twenty minutes after the cannonade commenced... I had just given these orders to the last battery on Little Round Top, when the signal-gun was fired'; the cease-fire ride (Meade sought, Howard concurring, 'I rode back along the ridge, ordering the fire to cease. This was followed by a cessation of that of the enemy, under the mistaken impression that he had silenced our guns'); Meade's aides independently carrying cease-firing orders (Bingham); the dozen exploded caissons on the Taneytown pike; the Hazard-sector indictment ('They had unfortunately exhausted their long range projectiles during the cannonade, under the orders of their corps commander' - Hancock unnamed); gun counts (77 on the II Corps mile; McGilvery 'forty-one guns'; II Corps batteries reduced to four guns each). 24-year retrospective, parti pris in the Hunt-Hancock dispute - flagged.

clockProfile.assessment: His B&L narrative hangs on the signal gun firing as he finished the Little Round Top order (~13:07 chain) with his ride starting 'between 10 and 11'; no independent clock reading beyond the OR report's. Offset inherited from the OR profile's +5~+7 with a retrospection-widened envelope.

sources.title: Maj. Thomas W. Osborn's report (Artillery Brigade, XI Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 747-751 (No. 264)
sources.author: Thomas W. Osborn
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Read page-by-page at live ehistory (pp. 747-751; ehistory 403s WebFetch but loaded in the browser pane - access route recorded; OCR slips reproduced: 'Artilelry', 'shoved', 'charage', 'whetever'). Dated July 29, 1863, to Hunt. Key verbatim: July 1 roster 'Total, 26 guns' (Wilkeson mw July 1, 'the command of the battery now devolved upon Lieutenant Bancroft'); July 2 morning 52 guns on the hill with the six Reserve batteries; the hill layout (Dilger right resting on the Baltimore road... Taft and Huntington perpendicular across the road); July 3 'About 2 p. m. they opened along our whole front with an unbroken line of artillery'; HIS OWN cease order with its contemporary motive: 'but as no satisfactory results were obtained, I ordered all our guns to cease firing, and the men to lie down to await developments. At the same time the artillery of our entire front ceased firing, and a few moments later the infantry of the enemy broke over the crest'; the repulse fire ('the havoc produced upon their ranks was truly surprising'); rounds expended: 'I am unable to give any definite estimate' (verified negative - the circulating ~1,600 figure is NOT in his OR); materiel table (98 horses; guns disabled/dismounted per battery). The postwar ruse framing (Weekly Times 1879) vs this report's no-satisfactory-results motive is a first-class conflict, carried in the dossier.

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement: opening 'About 2 p. m.' -> raw -53 vs the 13:07 chain. Joins the ~14:00 nominal-opening cluster (Longstreet's report, Smyth's report, Stannard's report) - four reports across both armies now share the ~2 p.m. opening, strengthening the 'camp clock' cluster note on or-27-2-longstreet. Rule 4: never anchor-defining.

sources.title: Osborn, 'The Artillery at Gettysburg' (Philadelphia Weekly Times, May 31, 1879; repr. Crumb ed., The Eleventh Corps Artillery at Gettysburg)
sources.author: Thomas W. Osborn
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. NOT directly accessible - carried AS-QUOTED in the GNMP Seminar 4 essay (URL above, read in full) and Seminar 16 essay 4 PDF (Barnett, fn 25). Verified-as-quoted verbatim: the hill 'the best target for artillery practice the enemy had during the war'; 'the shells must have reached us at the rate of one hundred and upwards a minute'; Meade on the hill mid-cannonade ('He said the result of the fight depended on our holding the hill'); the Howard/Wainwright/Osborn command split on the hill. His cease-to-lure-the-assault conversation with Hunt (the 'ruse') could NOT be verbatim-verified through any reachable channel (verified absence in the reachable reproductions) - it rests on secondary paraphrase plus Hunt's B&L parallel (Howard concurrence). 16-year retrospective; the Crumb reprint is print-only.

sources.title: Capt. John G. Hazard's report (Artillery Brigade, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 477-481 (No. 132)
sources.author: John G. Hazard
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Read page-by-page at live ehistory (pp. 477-478, 480-481; p. 479 is a map page) and cross-checked verbatim-identical against the Iron Brigader reproduction (https://ironbrigader.com/2021/06/26/captain-john-g-hazards-report-on-the-union-2nd-corps-artillery-brigade-at-gettysburg/, first verified 2026-06-13). Dated Aug. 1, 1863. Key verbatim: the July 2 line ('On the right, in a grove, Light Company I; 150 yards to the left, Battery A, First Rhode Island... Battery A, Fourth U. S....'); July 2 losses (Brown's B: 1 k, 7 w, 2 m, 24 horses killed, two guns to the rear, Brown 'severely wounded in the neck by a musket-shot'; Rorty's 1 k, 8 w, 13 horses); July 3 ~8 a.m. exchange ('exploding three limbers of Battery A, Fourth U. S.'; Woodruff's battery 'during the forenoon had eight separate engagements'); the cannonade: 'At 1 p. m. the artillery of the enemy opened along the whole line, and for an hour and a quarter we were subjected to a very warm artillery fire. The batteries did not at first reply, till the fire of the enemy becoming too terrible, they returned it till all their ammunition, excepting canister, had been expended'; the assault ('half the valley had been passed over by them before the guns dared expend a round of the precious ammunition remaining'; canister 'with terrible effect'); withdrawals and the battery consolidation to three; officer fates (Rorty k after one day in command; Cushing 'mortally wounded by a musket-shot' k; Woodruff mw d. July 4 'at his own request, was buried on the field'; Milne 'musket-shot through the lungs' d. July 10; Sheldon w; 'Every officer in this battery [A/4th US] was either killed or wounded'); Fuger's field promotion recommendation; brigade loss 'one-third'.

clockProfile.assessment: Two anchor statements: opening 'At 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07) and duration 'an hour and a quarter' of heavy fire -> slackening ~14:15 nominal, inside CA-J3A-4's 14:00-15:00 envelope. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. William Harrow's report (2nd Division + 1st Brigade, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 419-421
sources.author: William Harrow
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated July 16, 1863; written as division report (Harrow succeeded Gibbon July 3). Keys: 'At 1 p. m. the enemy opened a fierce cannonade upon the line from a hundred or more guns, which was continued until nearly 3 p. m.'; 'the enemy moved from the woods, 1,000 yards in front'; 'the crest of the hill occupied by the right of Colonel Hall and the left of General Webb seemed to be the point to which their main attack was directed'; 'the Third and First Brigades, of this division, inclined to the right, engaging the enemy as they moved'; 'the contest raged with almost unparalleled ferocity for nearly an hour'; division 'took into action 3,773 men, and lost 1,657'; 1st Brigade two-day loss 722 (148 k / 574 w); officer pins Col. Ward (15th MA) and Col. Huston (82nd NY) mw July 2, Colvill/Adams/Downie (1st MN) shot down July 3.

clockProfile.assessment: Opening 'At 1 p. m.' vs chain 13:07 (+7); cannonade 'continued until nearly 3 p. m.' vs CA-J3A-5 15:00 (consistent, +0..+7). Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Col. Thomas A. Smyth's report (2nd Brigade, 3rd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 464-466
sources.author: Thomas A. Smyth
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route; OCR renders the name 'Smith'). Keys: July 1 'order to march at 6.30 a. m.' from Uniontown; July 2 'At 4 a. m. ... placed in position on the hills overlooking the town', 'At 8 a. m. the First Delaware Volunteers were sent out as skirmishers, and the One hundred and eighth New York Volunteers were assigned to the support of Woodruff's battery', 'At 2 p. m. the enemy opened upon us with a severe fire of artillery, accompanied by an advance of infantry' (the Bliss farm axis); July 3 'Artillery firing from both sides began at 4 a. m.', 'Skirmishing ... until 10.30 a. m.', 'At 2 p. m. a most terrific cannonading was opened upon our front' 'continued without intermission until 5 p. m.', 'As the fire of the enemy's batteries slackened, their infantry moved upon our position in three lines'; 12th NJ held fire 'until they were within 20 yards'; prisoners 'estimated at from 1,200 to 1,500', 9 battle-flags; 108th NY casualties 'about half of the regiment in action'; Smyth wounded during the cannonading, 'surrendered the command to Lieutenant Colonel Francis E. Pierce'; Lt. William Smith (1st DE) killed 'with one of the enemy's captured flags in his hand'; brigade casualty recapitulation totaling 352.

clockProfile.assessment: Joins the ~14:00 nominal cluster (Longstreet, Osborn, Stannard): cannonade 'At 2 p. m.' vs chain 13:07 -> -53. His 'until 5 p. m.' end is 2h late against CA-J3A-5 and appears to fold the assault and after-fire into the cannonade window; envelope kept one-sided. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Lt. Col. James M. Bull's report (3rd Brigade [Willard's/Sherrill's], 3rd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 472-474
sources.author: James M. Bull
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: July 2 'first took its position, by direction of the division general, by battalion in mass near a barn a little south of ... Cemetery Hill'; 39th NY (Hildebrandt) skirmish, 'lost 26 enlisted men killed and wounded, and 2 commissioned officers wounded'; 'At about 3 p. m. ... the brigade moved from its position by the left flank about a quarter of a mile toward the left of the line' (July 2, then the Plum Run charge under Willard, 'pursuant to the order of Colonel Willard, given just before his death'); July 3 'About 1 p. m. the enemy opened from his batteries ... a terrific cannonade ensued, which continued about two hours'; 'The enemy, advancing in four lines across the flat, were subjected to a murderous fire of musketry and artillery, and were driven back in confusion, after an engagement of about an hour'; regiments 'so placed as supports to the artillery' on 'the slope of the hill occupied by us'; 'the brigade was commanded by Colonel Sherrill until his death, about 4 p. m., when the command devolved upon Lieutenant Colonel James M. Bull'; brigade loss 'severe ... the precise number of which I am unable to report' (attested-unreported at brigade grain).

clockProfile.assessment: Opening 'About 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07); 'continued about two hours' spans to ~15:00 nominal, consistent with CA-J3A-4/5. His 'Sherrill ... death, about 4 p. m.' sits ~30-40 min after the repulse window on the corrected clock - either a late-window casualty or clock drift; envelope widened, conflict carried on the dossier. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Capt. Andrew Cowan's report (1st New York Independent Battery), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 689-691
sources.author: Andrew Cowan
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated Aug. 15, 1863, Warrenton Va. Keys: left Manchester 21:00 July 1, arrived near the field '10 p. m. the 2d' (VI Corps march); July 3 'reported to General Newton ... about 10 a. m.'; heavy engagement 'about noon' (his opening clock - the corpus's second early pole after McGilvery's 12:30); parked 'less than 100 yards distant' behind the front line; moved at the crisis to the 'crest farther to my right, with General Webb's brigade' (the Brown-slot geometry); 'I commenced firing canister at 200 yards ... My last charge literally swept the enemy from my front, being fired at less than 20 yards'; counter-battery: 'concentrated my fire on a single battery, and exploded four of its limbers in rapid succession'; casualties '4 privates killed instantly, and 1 soon after died of wounds; 4 enlisted men and 2 officers wounded', 14 horses, 8 wheels disabled. No rounds-expended figure in the report (negative).

clockProfile.assessment: Single anchor statement: cannonade opened 'about noon' vs chain 13:07 -> +67 (his stated clock runs very early, the McGilvery pattern). One-source, one-statement; envelope reaches 0 because 'about noon' may loosely cover the pre-cannonade exchanges. Rule 4: display-only, never corrects.

sources.title: Lt. Gulian V. Weir's report (Battery C, 5th US), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 879-880
sources.author: Gulian V. Weir
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: July 2 'About 4 o'clock was ordered by Major-General Hancock to take up a position' forward; 'I opened with solid shot and spherical case'; 'Soon it was reported to me that we were out of canister' (the gun-loss fight; three guns temporarily lost, recaptured by the 13th VT per the battery tablet); July 3 'the enemy opened fire along the whole line, and it became so hot that we fell back'; 'I was conducted to General Webb's position, and came into battery under a heavy musketry fire'; ROUNDS EXPENDED VERBATIM: '50 rounds of solid shot, 160 rounds of spherical case, and 70 rounds of canister; total, 280 rounds'; Lt. Baldwin wounded by shell fragment, one caisson lost.

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon anchor clock stated (his only clock is July 2 'about 4 o'clock'). Profile recorded as unanchored nominal; timing of his July 3 movement is carried tier-D against the crisis sequence.

sources.title: Capt. Robert H. Fitzhugh's report (4th Volunteer Brigade + Battery K 1st NY), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 895-897
sources.author: Robert H. Fitzhugh
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: ordered by Tyler to post K/1st NY and A/1st NJ on the Baltimore turnpike near Slocum's line (July 2-3); 'At 1 p. m.' Friday July 3, by Hunt's direction, both batteries deployed 'near the stone fence in front of General Webb's division of the Second Corps'; enemy infantry reached the stone fence 'approximately 75 yards' from his position; Parsons delivered 'about 40 rounds of shrapnel into the flank of the rebel infantry charging the Second corps'; attack collapsed 'within thirty to forty-five minutes'; second assault (Wilcox/Lang axis) broken mid-field by 'an enfilading fire'; ROUNDS (K + attached 11th NY): '57 percussion shells, 15 shrapnel rounds, 17 time shells (89 total)'; casualties K: 7 wounded, 5 horses; A/1st NJ: 2 killed.

clockProfile.assessment: His 'At 1 p. m.' for the move to Webb's front is irreconcilable with the crisis sequence (the charge reached the wall ~15:20-25; Stone Sentinels' narrative has the batteries 'ordered into line at the gallop' at the height of the charge, and Parsons's own report says the order came 'about 3 p. m.'). Read as either the initial ORDER clock at the cannonade's opening (+7) or a plain misstatement; the huge negative envelope records the conflict. Rule 4: never moves anchors; the dossier carries the conflict. (Envelope floor clipped to the schema's -120 min bound; the raw conflict is larger and lives in the editionNote.)

sources.title: Lt. Augustin N. Parsons's report (Battery A, 1st New Jersey), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 899-900
sources.author: Augustin N. Parsons
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated July 17, 1863. Keys: 'On the 3rd instant, about 3 p. m., I received orders from General Hunt to move the battery'; placed 'about one-fourth of a mile south of Gettysburg Cemetery and near the Second Division, Second Corps'; 'At this time the enemy's infantry were advancing very rapidly. I at once opened fire upon them with case'; Fitzhugh's battery took position immediately on his right; then counter-battery: 'fired about 120 rounds with good effect' (shot) at one battery and 'used 80 rounds' (shell) at another before Hunt ordered cease; casualties 2 killed, 7 wounded; 3 horses killed + 2 mortally.

clockProfile.assessment: His 'about 3 p. m.' order-to-move with infantry 'advancing very rapidly' on arrival maps to ~15:10-15:20 canonical (between step-off and the wall) -> +10..+20. Consistent with the crisis sequence; rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Lt. William Wheeler's report (13th New York Independent Battery), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 752-753
sources.author: William Wheeler
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated July 29, 1863, Warrenton Junction. Keys: July 1 'orders to move forward at double-quick' through the town to Dilger's right, two caisson bodies broke down, 'very heavy fire ... both in front and upon the right flank'; July 3 'At approximately 4 p.m. ... orders to go assist the Second Corps, upon which a very heavy attack was being made'; Hancock assigned the position; found the enemy 'not more than 400 yards off, marching in heavy column by a flank'; 'enfilade their column with canister, which threw them into great disorder, and brought them to a halt three times'; ROUNDS EXPENDED: 850 total (whole battle); casualties 4 severely + 6 slightly wounded, 3 missing, 12 horses killed.

clockProfile.assessment: His 'about 4 p. m.' for the II Corps crisis move sits ~35-45 min after the canonical crisis (wall fighting 15:20-15:30); either his clock runs late or the battery arrived at the tail of the repulse (his canister enfilade on a column halting three times reads mid-fight, favoring clock error). Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Capt. James H. Cooper's report (Battery B, 1st Pennsylvania Light), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 364-365
sources.author: James H. Cooper
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: July 1 arrived ~noon, posted by Wainwright left of 3rd Div I Corps, 'fired about twenty-five shots at a battery in front', relocated near the Seminary, case/shell 'until canister range was obtained'; July 2 on Cemetery Hill crest 'fronting to the northeast', 16:00 duel at 1,400-2,000 yards, 'the enemy's guns were silenced in about two and a half hours' firing', relieved by Ricketts ~19:00, to the Artillery Reserve to refit; July 2 casualties 2 k (McCleary, Hoagland), 6 w, ~500 rounds; July 3 'Around 3 p. m., the battery moved to Second Corps front' engaging a damaged enemy battery then 'case shot until they reached canister range, when a few charges were fired into them, completely routing them'; July 3: 1 slightly wounded, 150 rounds. ROUNDS EXPENDED, PER DAY: ~400 (Jul 1, implied by total) / ~500 (Jul 2) / 150 (Jul 3); 'Total: 1,050 rounds fired across three days' (monument concurs at 1050).

clockProfile.assessment: July 3 'about 3 p. m.' move to the Second Corps front during the cannonade's last phase / opening of the assault (~15:00-15:10 canonical) -> ~+7; the War Dept tablet independently adjudicates the move 'at 3 P. M. ... during a heavy cannonade'. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Col. Charles S. Wainwright's report (Artillery Brigade, I Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 354-359
sources.author: Charles S. Wainwright
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route; pp. 355, 357-359 read; p. 356 not fetched this pass). Keys: July 1 Seminary Ridge fight and the retirement under Wadsworth's 'peremptory order'; East Cemetery Hill layout verbatim: 'Four guns of Battery B, Fourth U. S. Artillery, across the road so as to command the approaches from the town ... four guns of Battery I, First New York Artillery ... on the left; next Cooper's battery and then Reynolds'' (13 three-inch guns on the north front); 'The Fifth Maine battery was posted to the right and some 50 yards in front of this line, on a small knoll'; July 1 loss 'amounting in all to 83 officers and men and about 80 horses'; July 2 evening: CSA battery '(four 20-pounders and six 10-pounder Parrotts) in a wheat-field ... at about 1,300 yards' silenced in ~90 min leaving 'twenty-eight dead horses'; the dusk assault on the hill: 'fifteen guns in their front and six on their left flank', cannoneers of Wiedrich's and Ricketts' repelling attackers 'with fence-rails and stones'; his judgment 'this attack was almost entirely repelled by the artillery'; July 3: 'fired occasional shots at bodies of the enemy's troops' in the morning and participated in the afternoon's general engagement (no brigade rounds total in the report; component battery reports carry the figures: Cooper 1,050, Breck L/1st NY 1,290 itemized, Stevens 5th ME etc.); 1st Sgt Mitchell commanding B/4th US after Lt. Davison wounded. PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 356-359 fetched 2026-07-11): the p. 356 gap is CLOSED - it is July 1 narrative (the Seminary-crest twelve-gun concentration, guns 'hardly 5 yards apart'); the report contains NO brigade strength or rounds roll-up anywhere (now attested-absent, upgraded from unfetched): the only aggregate is July 1 'loss of the batteries ... amounting in all to 83 officers and men and about 80 horses'. July 2: the 4 p.m. Benner's Hill duel at 1,300 yards ('the most accurate fire I have ever yet seen from their artillery', an hour and a half to silence); the dusk East Cemetery Hill infantry attack repelled 'almost entirely ... by the artillery'. July 3: 'no serious attack upon the position ... The batteries fired occasional shots ... during the morning, and joined in the general artillery engagement in the afternoon. The fire of the enemy's batteries was noticed to be much less accurate than on the previous day.'

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon anchor clock in the fetched pages; July 1-2 clocks internally consistent with the provisional chains. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Robert O. Tyler's report (Artillery Reserve) with Lt. Gillett's ordnance annex, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 871-879
sources.author: Robert O. Tyler
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route; pp. 871-873, 878-879 read). Dated Aug. 30, 1863. Keys: June 29-30 Frederick -> Bruceville -> Taneytown; July 1 reported to Gibbon '10.30 p. m.' with eight batteries; July 2 parked 'about 1 1/2 miles from Gettysburg'; 'six batteries of thirty-four guns' sent to Sickles, 'under Major McGilvery ... doing terrible execution upon the successive columns attacking our left until about 6 p. m.'; B and M 1st CT Heavy ordered to Westminster (guns short two batteries); guns left on the field July 2 recovered after dark 'all but one gun' (Thompson's); Turnbull's battery July 3: '1 officer and 8 men killed, 14 men wounded, and 45 horses killed', four guns retaken; Ransom's regular brigade 'formed on hill crest near General Meade's headquarters'; 'No report has yet been received from Captain Ransom's ... or Captain Sterling's ... batteries'. GILLETT ORDNANCE ANNEX (2nd Lt. C. Gillett, ordnance officer, pp. 878-879) VERBATIM KEYS: ammunition ISSUED at Gettysburg '19,189' rounds across calibers; '4,694 rounds' remaining 'nearly all of which, though not in the best possible condition, could have been used'; 'Seventy wagons were unloaded, which were sent to the rear on the morning of the 3d'; caution that 'sending wagons loaded with ammunition to batteries in action often causes a loss of material'. This annex is the army-side ordnance-return class evidence behind Hunt's ~270 rounds/gun arithmetic. PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 874-878 fetched 2026-07-11): the report's own totals - '108 guns which were on the field'; 'seventy wagon-loads were issued on the night of the 2nd'; '10,090 rounds were issued to batteries outside of the reserve during the battle'; Fitzhugh and Parsons 'came up very opportunely to the support of the troops of Brigadier-General Webb' (ED-41 corroboration); Tyler's horse killed at the park as the cannonade opened ~12 o'clock (his clock). APPENDIX A (p. 876, July 4, 2 p.m.): 14 officers wounded, 216 privates k/w/m, 'Rounds of ammunition expended, about 11,653', 'Rounds of ammunition on hand 3,900' - the Reserve's own EXPENDITURE-side ledger, cleanly distinct from the issue-side figures (the ED-42 exemplar made explicit by the primary itself). Per-battery casualty table with officer pins at p. 878 (Ransom and Baldwin w; Livingston k; ...).

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon anchor clocks in the fetched pages (his July 2 'until about 6 p. m.' is a July 2 statement). Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Capt. A. P. Martin's report (Artillery Brigade, V Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 659-662
sources.author: Augustus P. Martin
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 661 read; brigade-level only). Rittenhouse's D/5th US filed NO separate report (verified negative this pass: p. 661 references Battery D only as a position datum for other units, 'to the right of Battery D, Fifth U. S. Artillery'). Brigade casualties 'Killed, 9; wounded, 33, and missing, 2. Total, 44.' PASS-8 EXTENSION (2026-07-11, pp. 660-661 read): THE WATSON/I-5TH US RECORD, brigade-grain (Watson filed no report - wounded at the opening; verified negative, the battery's story is told inside Martin's No. 214 quoting Lt. MacConnell): Battery I 'left in rear of the line of battle of the First Division... When positions had been selected and orders sent for the batteries to move to the front, they were not to be found' - 'taken' by 'an officer of General Sickles' staff, who had orders to take any batteries he could find, no matter where they belonged... thus depriving the Fifth Corps of its proper amount of artillery'; 'placed in position by some unknown officer of the Third Corps'; 'The battery was without support of any kind. The enemy appeared shortly - say twenty minutes - after taking position, nearly in front, at a distance of about 350 yards... the battery poured in canister, some twenty rounds, until men and horses were shot down or disabled to such an extent that the battery was abandoned'; THE RECAPTURE PRIMARY: 'soon recaptured by the bravery and determination of Second Lieutenant Samuel Peeples... who, having procured the services of the Garibaldi Guards {39th New York}, took a musket and led the charge himself, driving the enemy from the guns, and retaking everything that was lost, and conveyed it safely to the rear'; 'First Lieutenant M. F. Watson, commanding the battery, was wounded in the opening of the engagement'; battery loss '1 officer and 21 men... also 41 horses'; July 3 the battery 'reported unserviceable' and sent via Westminster to Washington for re-equipment; Peeples served with D/5th US on July 3 (p. 660). Also p. 660: Hazlett shot in the head after 'about an hour' engaged, 'died at 8 p. m.' (the D/5th command pin, cross-ref us-btty-rittenhouse).

sources.title: Maj. James Dearing's report (Dearing's Artillery Battalion, Pickett's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 387-391
sources.author: James Dearing
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route, id = 189683 + page; pp. 387-390 read). Dated Aug. 16, 1863. A REPORT PASS 1 DID NOT KNOW EXISTED (the battalion had been assumed Pendleton/tablet-only). Keys: arrived near the field July 2 (the Pendleton 'arrived since dusk' march chain with Eshleman); early July 3 'marched to the field of battle' into position 'on the crest of the hill immediately in front of the enemy's position'; THE ARC'S BEST UNIT-TO-UNIT SPACING STATEMENT: 'On my left and rear was Colonel Cabell's Artillery Battalion, and on my right and rear was the Washington Artillery Battalion'; daybreak sharpshooter fight, Stribling 'firing about a dozen rounds'; Maj. J. P. W. Read 'wounded in the head by a fragment of shell'; at the signal guns 'commencing firing slowly and deliberately' by battery 'at the batteries immediately in my front'; ammunition 'became completely exhausted, excepting a few round in my rifled guns'; batteries under 'a very heavy fire for upward of an hour without being able to fire a single shot'; 'about 6 p. m.' drove back an advancing infantry column (late-clock conflict, see profile); July 4 with McLaws, withdrew near sundown to Black Horse Tavern. Per-battery casualties: Stribling 3 w + 10 horses; Macon 3 k, 3 w + 8 horses; Blount 5 k&w + 12 horses; Caskie's figure NOT located in the fetched extraction (flagged, not attested-absent). Tablet conflict: the War Dept tablet has the battalion 'withdrawn from the field about 4 P. M.' vs his own 18:00 infantry-repulse statement - carried.

clockProfile.assessment: His signal-gun sequence is stated relative to the signal itself (tier A structurally, no clock given). The one stated clock, 'about 6 p. m.' for repulsing advancing infantry, sits ~2.5 h after the canonical repulse; either a genuinely later Union probe/skirmish advance or a very late clock - unresolved, envelope records it. Rule 4: display-only. (Envelope floor clipped to the schema's -120 min bound; the raw conflict is larger and lives in the editionNote.)

sources.title: Col. Henry C. Cabell's report (Cabell's Artillery Battalion, McLaws's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 374-378
sources.author: Henry C. Cabell
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 375-377 read). Keys: July 2 position 'on the edge of the wood, the right resting near the road leading from Gettysburg to Emmitsburg', dueling across ~600-700 yards at the Peach Orchard; Capt. Fraser 'dangerously wounded by the bursting of a shell' which killed two sergeants and one man; July 3 morning: McCarthy advanced 300-400 yards ahead of the skirmishers, fired twenty rounds; consolidation (Fraser's depleted command), six guns near a brick house on the Gettysburg-Emmitsburg road, Carlton + a McCarthy section 'slightly en echelon' before Pickett's division; cannonade 'about 1 p. m.' - 'For over two hours the cannonading on both sides was almost continuous and incessant, far, very far, exceeding any cannonading I have ever before witnessed'; Lt. Jennings w, Capt. Carlton w; post-assault forward move 'immediately on the road', one of Furlong's guns 'ordered to the rear' (ammunition exhausted); ~16:00 held ground 'about 700 yards from the Cemetery Hill' with orders 'to hold it till night'; withdrawn after dark. Casualty totals not in the fetched pages (tablet carries 12 k / 30 w / 4 m + 80 horses; the SS compilation page's 15 k / 57 w is the carried variant). Tablet ordnance figure: 'expended about 3300 rounds' July 2-3. PASS-3 UPGRADE: the unfetched casualty-table item is CLOSED via the compiled ANV return (or-27-2-anv-return p. 338), which prints the battalion PER-BATTERY: Carlton (Troup) 1/6=7, Fraser (Pulaski) 4/14=18, McCarthy (1st Richmond Howitzers) 2/3=5, Manly (Bty A 1st NC) 1/6=7; battalion total 8/29/-=37. Also fetched p. 385: Lt. C. W. Motes's company report (Troup Artillery) closing the battalion's July 4 posture ('the guns were not placed in position on the 4th' after Parrott ammunition exhaustion) with its own casualty line (1 k, 2 officers + 4 men w, 13 horses k).

clockProfile.assessment: Cannonade 'about 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07) with 'over two hours' duration reaching ~15:10, bracketing CA-J3A-5 cleanly. His '4 p. m.' forward-hold statement is post-assault, consistent at +7. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Lt. Col. John J. Garnett's report (Garnett's Artillery Battalion, Heth's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 652-654
sources.author: John J. Garnett
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 652-653 read). Keys: July 1 'About 11 a. m. ... orders to bring up my command to within supporting distance on the Gettysburg pike'; one battery's ammunition exhausted July 1, relieved by his rifles; July 2 'dispatched Major Richardson with the nine rifled pieces of the battalion to the hill ... opposite Cemetery Hill'; 15:00 July 2 'these pieces opened fire upon the enemy's batteries opposite, which they kept up without cessation until about thirty minutes before sunset'; JULY 3, THE ATTESTED SILENCE VERBATIM: Richardson's rifles ordered to Anderson's front 'to the right of Major Pegram's battalion' and 'placed his guns in position under fire at this point, but did not fire a single shot, having received orders to that effect'; the other six guns (four Napoleons + two howitzers) 'bore no part in these actions, although they were upon the field in readiness whenever they should be called upon'; July 4 morning reoccupied the July 2 rifle positions until night; Richardson + five rifled pieces detached to Imboden (the train guard). Tablet concurs: 'The Napoleons and Howitzers were in reserve and not actively engaged at any time.' PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 652-654 re-fetched in full 2026-07-11): THE CASUALTY GAP IS CLOSED - p. 654: 'Severely wounded, 2 enlisted men; slightly wounded, 3 enlisted men; missing (supposed to be in the hands of the enemy), 14 enlisted men', with the OR editors' footnote '*But see p. 344' pointing at the compiled return (-/5/17=22) - a report-vs-return delta of 3, both carried. The July 3 silence now has its order chain: Richardson's nine rifles were placed 'in obedience to orders from General Longstreet ... under fire at this point, but did not fire a single shot, having received orders to that effect' at 'the position held by Major-General Anderson's division, and to the right of Major Pegram's battalion'; Alexander (Military Memoirs p. 419) identifies these as the nine short-range howitzers Pendleton offered him, which he 'placed under cover close in rear of the forming column with orders to remain until sent for, intending to take them with the column when it advanced' - the silent battalion was the assault column's intended accompanying artillery. Also: July 1 Maurin detachment relieving one of Pegram's exhausted batteries; July 2 Richardson's nine rifles engaged 3 p.m. to ~30 min before sunset opposite Cemetery Hill.

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon clock statements (the day's record is a sequence of orders and an attested silence). Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Maj. D. G. McIntosh's report (McIntosh's Artillery Battalion, Third Corps reserve), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 674-677
sources.author: David Gregg McIntosh
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 675-676 read). Keys: July 1 flank fire near the Cashtown pike, guns 'enfiladed a heavy column of the enemy's infantry, formed in the railroad cut'; July 2-3 'behind a stone wall, on the range of hills to the left of the town of Gettysburg' (Seminary Ridge); July 3 sustained firing, 'not a single case' of flinching observed; July 4 'the same position was maintained with but little firing', withdrew to the stone bridge under Hill's orders that afternoon. ROUNDS EXPENDED, ITEMIZED VERBATIM: Napoleon 213 + 3-inch rifle 1,049 + Whitworth 133 = 1,395 total; casualties 24 killed and wounded, 16 captured, 38 horses disabled/killed (tablet variant: 7 k, 25 w 'of whom 16 were captured' - two accountings carried); horses 'received no corn, subsisting entirely upon grass' on the retreat.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks in the fetched pages. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Capt. Ervin B. Brunson's report (Pegram's Artillery Battalion, Third Corps reserve), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 677-679
sources.author: Ervin B. Brunson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 677-679 read). Keys: July 1 approach - 'When within 2 miles of Gettysburg, Captain Marye's section of rifled guns was run forward' and 'opened on a piece of woods to the left of the pike', 'After firing some eight or ten rounds, the pieces were limbered up' (the arc's opening-shots record); July 1 line 'ten Napoleons and seven rifled guns' forcing Union artillery 'to limber up and retire their pieces three distinct times'; July 2 repositioned 'approximately one mile right of the pike, roughly 1,400 yards from' the Union artillery, 'enfilading their batteries whenever they opened upon the batteries on our right'; July 3 'never did men behave more heroically', 'one of the most terrific' artillery fights; 'The infantry failed to dislodge the enemy from his position.' ROUNDS EXPENDED VERBATIM: 'The battalion expended in the three days' fight 3,800 rounds of ammunition' (tablet concurs). PER-DAY CASUALTIES (EC6 episode split, primary): July 1: 2 k, 8 w, 6 horses; July 2: 2 k, 7 w, 25 horses; July 3: 10 k, 37 w, 38 horses; ordnance officer mw July 1 by solid shot; retreat: ~6-8 men taken prisoner, one howitzer caisson abandoned, ~20 horses abandoned for want of shoes. Tablet casualty line (10 k / 37 w = 47) matches only the July 3 row - the tablet-vs-report accounting conflict is carried.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks in the fetched pages. Unanchored nominal.

sources.author: R. Lindsay Walker
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; pp. 610-611 read). sources.title: Col. J. Thompson Brown's report (Chief of Artillery, Second Corps ANV), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 455-457
sources.author: J. Thompson Brown
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route; p. 456 read). THE EWELL-WING JULY 3 PARTICIPATION RECORD: 'The First Virginia Artillery [Dance's battalion] and a portion of Carter's and Nelson's battalions engaged the enemy's batteries, in order to divert their fire from our infantry' - fire 'was well directed, and its fine effect was very noticeable', temporarily silencing Cemetery Hill artillery. July 2: Latimer's (Andrews's) battalion fire from the extreme left (Benner's Hill) 'aided by Graham's battery ... and from Dance's Watson's and Smith's jr., batteries ... on the right of our line'; Latimer withdrew three batteries against superior positioning and was mortally wounded - 'no heavier loss could have befallen the artillery of this corps.' Jones's battalion operated with the cavalry; Tanner's battery sent with the wagon train (Williamsport). This report converts the pass-1 'Ewell-wing near-silence' framing into a documented PARTIAL, diversionary participation: Dance engaged, parts of Carter/Nelson engaged, Jones detached, Latimer's battalion wrecked the evening before.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks in the fetched page. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Cadmus M. Wilcox's report (Wilcox's brigade, Anderson's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 616-621 (No. 537)
sources.author: Cadmus M. Wilcox
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 616-621 read in full). Dated July 17, 1863, Bunker Hill. July 3 keys: 'early in the morning, before sunrise, the brigade was ordered out to support artillery under the command of Colonel Alexander ... formed in line parallel to the Emmitsburg turnpike and about 200 yards from it, artillery being in front, much of it on the road'; men had not eaten since morning of the 2d; held 'till about 3.20 p.m.'; cannonade 'about one hour' each side, 'my men ... suffered comparatively little, probably less than a dozen men killed and wounded. The brigade lying on my right (Kemper's) suffered severely'; 'Our artillery ceased to fire after about one hour. The enemy continued to fire for awhile after ours had ceased'; 'I do not believe a single battery of the enemy had been disabled'; THE ECHELON TRIGGER: 'The advance had not been made more than twenty or thirty minutes, before three staff officers in quick succession (one from the major-general commanding division) gave me orders to advance to the support of Pickett's division'; 'My brigade, about 1,200 in number' (July 3 engaged-strength primary), order R-to-L 9th-10th-11th-8th-14th Ala; 'Not a man of the division that I was ordered to support could I see'; withdrew on his own judgment, retired under artillery fire, reformed on the original line. July 3 loss 204; July 2 loss 577 (four of five regimental commanders wounded July 2: Forney, Pinckard, Sanders w + Fletcher w); campaign total 777 k/w/m of which 257 missing, 'nearly all ... supposed to be killed or wounded ... but few, if any, not wounded, were taken prisoners.' July 2 arc: 7 a.m. into line; 10th/11th Ala wood fight; McLaws formed at right angles ~2 p.m.; advanced when McLaws' left reached him (his 6.20 p.m. clock); two guns + six guns captured; withdrew unsupported.

clockProfile.assessment: His one July 3 clock, held position 'till about 3.20 p.m.', reads against his own +20-30-min-after-step-off trigger statement (canonical ~15:25-15:35 for his advance under CA-J3A-6=15:05): roughly agreeing to slightly early. His July 2 clocks (2 p.m. McLaws artillery duel opening; 6.20 p.m. advance) ride the July 2 chain and are not used here. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Col. David Lang's report (Perry's Florida Brigade, Anderson's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 631-633 (No. 545)
sources.author: David Lang
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 631-633 read in full). Dated July 29, 1863. July 3 keys: at daylight ordered by Anderson to connect his right with Wilcox's left and 'conform my movements during the day to those of his brigade ... I would receive no further orders' (the standing-conformity trigger, EC4); ~7 a.m. moved up with Wilcox's left 'in front and at the foot of the hill upon which the batteries were in position', skirmishers advanced to the next crest; 'remained quietly until nearly 2 p.m., when the batteries opened a furious bombardment ... which lasted till nearly 4 p.m., when Pickett's division ... charged'; the echelon: 'Soon after General Pickett's troops retired behind our position, General Wilcox began to advance, and, in accordance with previous orders to conform to his movements, I moved forward also' (sequence: the supporting advance follows the repulse); advanced under artillery fire without meeting infantry until the woods at the foot of the heights; left flanked, 'certain annihilation', ordered retreat; 2nd Florida colors + a large number cut off and captured (the 16th Vermont's flank action object, cross: us-i-3-3-stannard.md); Capt. McCaslan (AAAG) killed during the retreat; Maj. Moore (2d Fla) wounded and captured, Capt. Gardner (5th Fla) wounded. Strength/loss primary: 'The brigade went into action near 700 strong, and lost ... 455 killed, wounded, and missing' (matches the p. 343 return 33/217/205=455); July 2 charge lost 'about 300' (=> July 3 share ~155 by subtraction, [B]-grade). July 2 arc: 6 p.m. advance on Wilcox's left, 8th Fla colors lost. PASS-7 ADDITION (2026-07-11, p. 631 read - the July 2 portion): morning move ~1.5 miles, formed behind a mile-wide open field; 'At 5 p.m.' the Longstreet-is-driving notice, 'at 6 p.m.' the advance on Wilcox's movement (stated clocks ~+45..+60 early vs the ~18:45-19:00 canonical - the brigade's July 2 clocks join the ED-58 field-wide class even though its July 3 clocks sit in the -53 LATE cluster: the same house's two reports of two days skew in OPPOSITE directions, the audit's sharpest single-source clock heterogeneity); 'a murderous fire of grape, canister, and musketry' at the first crest; charged at the double-quick past the guns, 'four or five pieces of cannon' taken but horses/limbers retained by the enemy (contrast Wright's 'over twenty'); halted at the base of the heights to reform; the aide's report that Wilcox was being forced back triggered the withdrawal; July 2 loss 'about 300'; THE 8th FLORIDA COLORS (both-sided): the Perry advanced tablet has 'the color bearer of the 8th Florida fell and its flag was lost' = Brewster's report names Sgt. Thomas Hogan (3rd Excelsior) taking 'the colors of the Eighth Florida Regiment' in the evening recharge.

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated July 3 clocks, both consistently LATE against the chain: cannonade opens 'nearly 2 p.m.' (canonical 13:07, offset -53) and Pickett charges 'nearly 4 p.m.' (canonical 15:05, offset -55). Joins the -53 late-stated cluster (Longstreet, Smyth) as its sixth member and first Florida witness. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Maj. John T. Jones's report (Pettigrew's brigade, Heth's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 642-644 (No. 550)
sources.author: John T. Jones
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 642-644 read in full). Dated August 9, 1863; Jones (26th NC) commanded the brigade after Marshall fell. July 1: regimental order R-to-L 52d-47th-11th-26th NC; the branch/wheat-field fight; 26th NC 'lost more than half its men killed and wounded', Col. Burgwyn k and Lt. Col. Lane w 'both with the colors'; Col. Leventhorpe (11th) w, Maj. Ross k. July 2 evening: 'moved about a mile to our right, and took position in rear of our batteries, facing the works of the enemy on Cemetery Hill.' July 3 keys: 'remained here until about 12 o'clock on the 3d, when our batteries opened'; 'About 2 o'clock we were ordered to advance'; step-off friction: 'some confusion in the line, owing to the fact that it had been ordered to close in on the right on Pickett's division, while that command gave way to the left. This was soon corrected'; 'When about half across the intervening space, the enemy opened on us a most destructive fire of grape and canister. When within about 250 or 300 yards of the stone wall ... a perfect hail-storm of lead from their small-arms'; 'many had reached the wall, when we received a deadly volley from the left. The whole line on the left had given way'; fell back to the original position in rear of the batteries; 'After this day's fight, but one field officer was left in the brigade. Regiments that went in with colonels came out commanded by lieutenants.' July 14 Falling Waters: Pettigrew mw ('He had received a severe contusion on the hand on the 3d, but would not report off duty').

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated July 3 clocks, both consistently EARLY against the chain: batteries open 'about 12 o'clock' (canonical 13:07, offset +67) and advance 'about 2 o'clock' (canonical 15:05, offset +65 - his brigade stepped off with the division at the cannonade's end). Joins Cowan (+67) at the early-stated pole from the opposite side of the field. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Capt. J. J. Young (QM, 26th North Carolina) to Gov. Zebulon B. Vance, July 4, 1863, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 645-646 (No. 551)
sources.author: J. J. Young
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11. Written NEAR GETTYSBURG on July 4 - the freshest strength-decay primary in the batch (next-day document, not a retrospective report). Keys: 26th NC 'went in with over 800 men ... There came out but 216, all told, unhurt' (July 1); 'Yesterday they were again engaged, and now have only about 80 men for duty' (after July 3); 'Eleven men were shot down the first day with our colors; yesterday they were lost'; division grain: 'Our whole division numbers but only 1,500 or 1,600 effective men, as officially reported ... The division at the beginning numbered about 8,000 effective men' (Heth's division decay, July 4 official-report basis); officer catalogue incl. Burgwyn k (through both lungs), Lane w (neck/jaw/mouth), 'Colonel Marshall and Major Richardson, of the Fifty-second, supposed to be killed'; Capt. Wagg k by grape July 3; Capt. Albright the only unhurt captain, commanding the regiment. Caution: casualty attributions are day-after supposition in places ('supposed to be killed') - document pin for strengths, conflict-carrying for individual fates.

sources.title: Lt. Col. S. G. Shepard's report (Archer's brigade, Heth's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 646-648 (No. 552)
sources.author: Samuel G. Shepard
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 646-648 read in full). Dated August 10, 1863; written from his own observation plus regimental officers' testimony (he states the method). July 1: Archer captured ('very much exhausted with fatigue'), ~75 lost at the creek obstruction; Fry took command. July 2: 'We were not in the engagement of July 2' (first-class negative evidence); night of the 2d moved 'in front of the enemy's works'. July 3 keys: regimental order R-to-L 1st Tenn - 13th Ala - 14th Tenn - 7th Tenn - 5th Ala Bn; THE CONVERGENCE EXECUTED: 'a space of a few hundred yards between the right of Archer's brigade and the left of General Pickett's division when we advanced ... by the time we had advanced a little over half of the way, the right of Archer's touched and connected with Pickett's left. The command was then passed down the line by the officers, "Guide right"' (claim-fry-guide from the executing side); 'Within 180 or 200 yards of his works, we came to a lane inclosed by two stout post and plank fences. This was a very great obstruction' (the Emmitsburg road on the Fry front); the color record: every flag in the brigade except the 7th Tennessee's captured at or within the works, color-bearer sequences 3/3/4/3 shot down, the 7th's flag saved by Capt. Norris tearing it from the staff; '7 field officers ... went into the charge, only 2 of whom came out'; strength/loss primary: 'We went into the fight on the 1st with 1,048 men, 677 of whom were killed, wounded, and captured during these engagements' (matches the p. 344 return exactly). Falling Waters clubbed-guns fight; Pettigrew mw while with the brigade.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks anywhere in the report; all July 3 facts are sequence-grade (tier D) against CA-J3A-6..9. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Joseph R. Davis's reports (Davis's brigade July 1; Heth's Division July 3), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 648-651 (No. 553)
sources.author: Joseph R. Davis
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 648-651, both reports read in full). Brigade report (Aug 26): July 1 with three regiments (11th Miss LEFT AS DIVISION TRAIN GUARD - EC2 detachment primary), 42d Miss right / 2d Miss center / 55th NC left, the railroad-cut fight, retired ~1 p.m., renewed ~3 p.m. with Ewell's appearance; 'of 9 field officers present, but 2 escaped unhurt.' DIVISION report (Aug 22, written by Davis because Pettigrew was mw at Falling Waters before reporting): evening July 2 line Archer(Fry) right - Pettigrew(Marshall) right center - Davis left center - Brockenbrough left, 'immediately in the rear of our artillery ... the enemy's line ... nearly 1 mile distant, the intervening space ... fields, intersected by strong post and rail fences'; early July 3 morning shells caused 'a few casualties ... in one of the brigades'; 'About 9 a.m. the division was moved to the left about a quarter of a mile, and in the same order of battle was formed in the rear of Major Pegram's battalion' (the morning leg + the Pegram frame anchor); 'About 1 p.m. the artillery along our entire line opened ... For two hours the fire was heavy and incessant ... In Davis' brigade 2 men were killed and 21 wounded' (bombardment apportionment primary); the standing order ('when the artillery in our front ceased firing, the division would attack ... keeping dressed to the right ... in line with ... Pickett's division ... march obliquely to the left'); 'The artillery ceased firing at 3 o'clock, and the order to move forward was given' (the CSA-side twin of Hall's 'At 3 o'clock exactly'); 'Not a gun was fired at us until we reached a strong post and rail fence about three-quarters of a mile from the enemy's position, when we were met by a heavy fire of grape, canister, and shell' (EC5 range relation); 'About 4 p.m. the division reached the line held in the morning'; officer record: 'Colonel B. D. Fry ... and Colonel James K. Marshall ... were wounded and taken prisoners while gallantly leading their brigades' (CONFLICT: the register and Young's July 4 letter carry Marshall killed - both readings recorded); 'in Archer's brigade but two field officers escaped, in Pettigrew's but one, and in Davis' all were killed or wounded'; Pettigrew's horse killed, slight hand wound. PASS-10 VERBATIM UPGRADE (2026-07-12, p. 649 re-read): 'About 10.30 o'clock a line of battle was formed - with the Forty-second Mississippi... on the right; Fifty-fifth North Carolina... on the left, and Second Mississippi... in the center - skirmishers thrown forward, and the brigade moved forward to the attack' - THE CSA ATTACK-POLE CLOCK for the Heth deploy-vs-attack split (ED-66 candidate's keystone); the railroad rally ('rallied near the railroad, where he again made a stand, and, after desperate fighting, with heavy loss on both sides, he fled in great disorder'); the withdrawal 'This was about 1 p.m.'; 'About 3 p.m. a division of Lieutenant-General Ewell's corps came up on our left, moving in line perpendicular to ours, and the brigade was again moved forward... reached the suburbs of the town'; 'of 9 field officers present, but 2 escaped unhurt.'

clockProfile.assessment: Three stated July 3 clocks, all agreeing with the chain: cannonade opens 'about 1 p.m.' (canonical 13:07, +7), 'ceased firing at 3 o'clock' (CA-J3A-5 adopted 15:00, +0) with the step-off order immediately following (CA-J3A-6 15:05), retreat complete 'about 4 p.m.'. The best CSA-side chain-agreeing report clock found to date - the Confederate twin of Hall's +5 profile.

sources.title: Maj. Joseph A. Engelhard's report (operations of Pender's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 656-660 (No. 556)
sources.author: Joseph A. Engelhard (AAG, the division report)
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 656-660 read in full). Dated November 4, 1863, written by the AAG because both division commanders (Pender, then Trimble) were lost. July 1: Perrin/Scales assault of Seminary Ridge ('Every field officer, with one exception, was either killed or wounded' in Scales's at the 75-yard bottom; Scales and Riddick w; Pender personally rallied the brigade). July 2: Pender mw late afternoon by shell fragment on his extreme right; command to Lane ~sunset; the Ewell-coordinated night attack prepared then abandoned. July 3 keys - THE TRIMBLE COMMAND RECORD: morning, Lane ordered by Hill to report with his two second-line brigades to Longstreet 'as a support to Pettigrew'; Longstreet ordered them 'in rear of the right of Heth's division'; 'General Lane was relieved of the command by Major General I. R. Trimble, who acted under the same orders' (Trimble assumes command on the field, morning July 3); 'with Lowrance on the right'; 'suffering no little from the two hours' exposure to the heavy artillery fire which preceded the attack'; the advance '~1 mile' with Trimble 'taking immediate command of the movement'; Lane's left uncovered by the front division gaining ground right, its faster advance 'checked by an order from General Trimble'; the division's right 'had reached the works'; the two left regiments of Lane's (Avery, Barry) 'advanced some minutes after the whole line had given way, and fell back, under direct orders'; TRIMBLE PIN: 'severely wounded near the enemy's works, in the leg, which necessitated its amputation, and in the retreat to the Potomac ... fell into the hands of the enemy'; one of his staff k, 2 w; the division reformed by Lane per Trimble's orders 'just in rear of the artillery, and upon the same ground where it had rested.' Scales's brigade context: 'one-half of its remaining numbers killed and wounded in the attack on the 1st'.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks ('two hours' exposure' is a duration, consumed as tier B input); all ordering facts are sequence-grade. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope. Four-month retrospective by a staff officer consolidating brigade reports.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. James H. Lane's report (Lane's brigade, Pender's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 664-667 (No. 558)
sources.author: James H. Lane
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 664-667 read in full). Dated August 13, 1863. July 1: order 7th-37th-28th-18th-33d NC; advanced past the woods 'subsequently occupied by Pegram's battalion'; halted at the McMillan peach orchard/stone fence by Pender's order. July 2: 33d and 18th moved 'to the left of Lieutenant-Colonel Garnett's battalion of artillery' for shelter (frame cross: csa-3c-bn-garnett); skirmishers took the road in front; ~sunset informed of Pender's fall and took division command; the abandoned night attack (his dispositions given in detail). July 3 keys: ordered by Hill 'to move in person to the right, with the two brigades forming my second line, and to report to General Longstreet as a support to Pettigrew. General Longstreet ordered me to form in rear of the right of Heth's division'; 'putting Lowrance on the right'; relieved by Trimble; STRUCTURAL STATEMENT: 'Heth's division was much larger than Lowrance's brigade and my own, which were its only support, and there was consequently no second line in rear of its left'; the advance 'through the woods in which our batteries were planted, and through an open field about a mile'; 'As soon as Pettigrew's command gave back, Lowrance's brigade and my own, without ever having halted, took position on the left of the troops which were still contesting the ground'; 'repeatedly driving the cannoneers from their pieces, completely silencing the guns in our immediate front'; 'We advanced to within a few yards of the stone wall, exposed all the while to a heavy raking artillery fire from the right. My left was here very much exposed, and a column of the enemy's infantry was thrown forward in that direction, which enfiladed my whole line' (the 8th Ohio object, cross: Smyth/Woodruff frames); withdrew, 'reformed immediately in rear of the artillery, as directed by General Trimble'; STRENGTH/LOSS PRIMARY: '660 out of an effective total of 1,355, including ambulance corps and rear guard, our loss on the 1st and 2nd being but slight' (vs the p. 344 return's 389 k+w with the OR's own footnote carrying his 660 - the missing difference, ED-47 candidate); Trimble wounded -> Lane again commanded the division from July 3 evening.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks; sequence-grade throughout. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Alfred M. Scales's report (Scales's brigade, July 1), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 669-670 (No. 560)
sources.author: Alfred M. Scales
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11. Dated August 14, 1863; covers July 1 only (Scales w that evening, refers the rest to Lowrance). Keys: the brigade's July 1 charge past the halted exhausted front line ('without ammunition, and would not advance farther'), the descent opposite the seminary, 'a most terrific fire of grape and shell on our flank, and grape and musketry in our front' to the bottom ~75 yards out; 'Here I received a painful wound from a piece of shell'; 'Every field officer of the brigade save one had been disabled'; HIS CASUALTY TABLE (printed in the report): officers 9k/45w/1m=55, men 39/336/115=490, TOTAL 48/381/116=545 - CONFLICT with the p. 345 return's brigade total 102/323/110=535 for July 1-3 (a July-1-heavy report table larger than the compiled three-day total; both readings carried, never averaged; the return itself warns its totals are approximative).

sources.title: Col. William L. J. Lowrance's report (Scales's brigade from July 1 evening), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 671-673 (No. 561)
sources.author: William Lee J. Lowrance
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 671-672 read in full). Dated August 12, 1863. Keys: took command evening July 1, brigade 'numbering in all about 500 men, without any field officers, excepting Lieutenant-Colonel Gordon and myself ... In this depressed, dilapidated, and almost unorganized condition' (EC2 primary for the July 3 basis); moved at 1 a.m., July 2 dawn to the extreme right 'on line with the artillery ... its only guard' with a half-mile skirmish line, relieved 1 p.m. by Anderson's division, rejoined the division second line right of Lane; July 3 morning with Lane to the right, 'placed under command of General Trimble, and were formed on the second line, in rear of Major Poague's battalion of artillery' (EC3 via the Poague frame); 'here we remained at least one hour, under a most galling fire of artillery'; the advance: 'a wide, hot, and already crimson plain ... at a distance of 1 mile'; 'ere we had advanced over two-thirds of the way, troops from the front came tearing through our ranks, which caused many of our men to break, but with the remaining few we went forward until the right of the brigade touched the enemy's line of breastworks'; THE CLIMAX STRENGTH PIN: 'The two brigades (now reduced to mere squads, not numbering in all 800 guns) were the only line to be seen upon that vast field, and no support in view ... without orders, the brigade retreated'; rallied on the original line; wounded himself July 3 (register: w July 3) - his report does not narrate his own wound.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated July 3 clocks ('at least one hour' under fire is a duration input, tier B); sequence-grade. Unanchored nominal at the kind default envelope.

sources.title: Maj. Gen. Richard H. Anderson's report (Anderson's Division, Third Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 613-616 (No. 536)
sources.author: Richard H. Anderson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 614-616 read). Division line July 2 (R-to-L Wilcox-Perry-Wright-Posey-Mahone), enemy line 'about 1,200 yards in our front'; July 2 en-echelon commitment and withdrawal 'in the same succession in which they had advanced'. July 3 keys: 'at 3.30 o'clock ... troops of General Longstreet's corps were advanced to the assault of the enemy's center'; 'I received orders to hold my division in readiness to move up in support'; 'Wilcox's and Perry's brigades had been moved forward, so as to be in position to render assistance'; THE STOP ORDER: 'I was about to move forward Wright's and Posey's brigades, when Lieutenant-General Longstreet directed me to stop the movement, adding that it was useless, and would only involve unnecessary loss, the assault having failed' (CA-J3A-10 layer, the counter-echelon that never launched); EC6 attribution: 'Some loss was sustained by each of the brigades of the division from the cannonading. Wilcox's, which was supporting Alexander's artillery, suffering the most seriously.' Total division campaign loss 2,266 (p. 616, incl. Manassas Gap).

clockProfile.assessment: One stated July 3 clock: Longstreet's corps advanced 'at 3.30 o'clock' (canonical CA-J3A-6 15:05, offset -25) - a moderately late-stated clock between the agreeing cluster and the -53 pole. His July 2 clocks (2 p.m. artillery, 5.30 McLaws) ride the July 2 chain. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Gen. R. E. Lee to Maj. Gen. George E. Pickett [no date]: destroy the division report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 3 p. 1075
sources.author: Robert E. Lee
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-iii route, id = 190731 + page). Verbatim: 'You and your men have crowned yourselves with glory; but we have the enemy to fight, and must carefully, at this critical moment, guard against dissensions which the reflections in your report would create. I will, therefore, suggest that you destroy both copy and original, substituting one confined to casualties merely. I hope all will yet be well.' Printed undated among August 1863 correspondence. THE negative-evidence primary for why no Pickett division report exists in the OR (No. 441 Peyton and No. 442 Dearing are the division's only surviving reports); the suppressed report itself has never been produced (LaSalle Corbell Pickett's claims never verified).

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Alexander S. Webb's report (2nd Brigade, 2nd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 427-428 (No. 104)
sources.author: Alexander S. Webb
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, id = 188622 + page; pp. 427-428). Dated July 12, 1863, Jones' Cross-Roads, Md. July 2 keys: 'this brigade was put in position at 6.30 a.m. on the 2d, on Granite Ridge, on the right of the division'; 'The Sixty-ninth Regiment was placed behind a fence a little in advance of the ridge; the remaining three regiments ... under cover of the hill in rear'; right on Cushing's battery, left connecting Brown's B/1st RI; ~6.30 p.m. assault (Wright): line 'advanced beyond one gun of Brown's battery', met the 69th + 106th + 72nd fire, 'halted, wavered, and fell back'; ~250 prisoners (1 col, 5 capts, 15 lts); 'Colonel Baxter, Seventy-second Pennsylvania Volunteers, at this time was wounded'; 'ThE One hundred and sixth Pennsylvania Volunteers were ordered back from the Emmitsburg road a little before dark, and ordered to report to Major-General Howard ... near the cemetery' (the brigade fights July 3 without it). July 3 keys: 'About 1 p.m. the enemy opened with more than twenty batteries upon our line. By 2.45 o'clock had silenced the Rhode Island battery' (the Brown withdrawal, ED-33, from the brigade commander); 'The Seventy-first ... advanced to the wall on the right of the Sixty-ninth'; 'The Seventy-second ... held in reserve under the crest of the hill'; 'Three of Cushing's guns were run down to the fence, carrying with them their canister' (three-gun reading, vs the V2 two-serviceable-guns claim - conflict carried on claim-cushing-guns-to-wall); 'The enemy advanced steadily to the fence, driving out a portion of the Seventy-first'; 'General Armistead passed over the fence with probably over 100 of his command and with several battle-flags'; 'The Seventy-second ... ordered up to hold the crest, and advanced to within 40 paces of the enemy's line' (ED-4's crest reading from Webb himself); captures 'nearly 1,000 prisoners, 6 battle-flags', 1,400 stand of arms, 903 sets of accouterments; loss July 2-3 '43 commissioned officers and 482 enlisted men' = 525 (vs the official return's 491 - conflict recorded, dossier EC6). Public domain.

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated clocks, both near-chain: 'About 1 p.m.' at the opening (chain 13:07 -> +7) and 'By 2.45 o'clock' for the silencing of Brown's battery (the ~14:40 ED-33 withdrawal -> ~-5). His report clock sits within ~7 min of the chain on both; adopted +6 as the midpoint reading. Report-nominal kind (ED-25 rule 4): corroborates, never defines.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. John Gibbon's report (2nd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 415-418 (No. 98)
sources.author: John Gibbon
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 416-418). Dated August 7, 1863 (written convalescent). July 2 keys: III Corps 'At 4 o'clock ... advanced, and, swinging round its left flank, took up a position along the Emmitsburg road'; 'Webb's brigade on the right, partially behind a low stone wall, and protecting Cushing's battery'; the evening penetration 'came quite through a vacancy in our line' before dark ended the contest. July 3 keys: command shuffle stated (Hancock resumed corps command, Gibbon returned to division); 'At 1 o'clock ... the enemy opened with his artillery all along his line, and for two hours the most terrific shower of shot and shell continued, ably responded to by our batteries' (a TWO-HOUR duration primary, 13:00-15:00 on his clock - chain-agreeing with Haskell/Hancock); the assault: 'As the front line came up, it was met with such a withering fire of canister and musketry as soon melted it away, but still on they came from behind, pressing forward to the wall'; 'turned again, drove him back over the wall, capturing a large number of prisoners and many colors'; HIS WOUNDING: 'I was wounded and left the field' (position context: the left of his division, where 'the right of the enemy's line did not extend'); DIVISION EC2+EC6 PRIMARY: 'lost in killed and wounded over 1,600' of ~3,800 engaged, 'captured more prisoners than it had men on the ground at the end of the conflict'; eight field officers killed incl. Col. Ward (15th Mass) and Col. O'Kane (69th Pa); Haskell commendation ('slightly wounded, but refused to quit the field'). Public domain.

clockProfile.assessment: 'At 1 o'clock' at the opening (chain 13:07 -> +7) with 'for two hours' carrying the cannonade to 15:00 stated = 15:07 corrected, inside CA-J3A-5's envelope - the report twin of Haskell's watch arithmetic (Gibbon was his general). Report-nominal (rule 4).

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Alexander Hays's report (3rd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 453-455 (No. 115)
sources.author: Alexander Hays
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 453-455; p. 454 transcribed in full). Dated July 8, 1863. July 2 position: division 'arrived within about a mile of the town ... assigned a position on a ridge nearly parallel with the road, facing westward'; 'A stone wall just below the crest of the hill gave much strength to the position, and an open space of half a mile in our front afforded the artillery ... a fair field'; the day: 'desultory fire from their artillery ... frequently shifting'; Willard's brigade detached to the left (Barksdale repulse; 'The loss of this brigade amounts to one-half the casualties in the division'). July 3 keys, verbatim: 'About 11 a.m. an entire lull occurred, which was continued until nearly 2 p.m. Anticipating the movement of the enemy, I caused the house and barn in our front, which interrupted the fire of our artillery, to be burned' (the Bliss buildings, EC4); 'At the hour last named, they opened upon our front the most terrific and uninterrupted fire from artillery. I cannot believe there were less than eighty pieces bearing on us within good range. It was continued uninterruptedly until 4.30 o'clock, when a heavy column of the enemy moved forward in three lines, preceded by a strong line of skirmishers'; 'Their march was as steady as if impelled by machinery'; 'When within 100 yards of our line of infantry, the fire of our men could no longer be restrained. FOUR LINES ROSE FROM BEHIND OUR STONE WALL, and before the smoke of our first volley had cleared away, the enemy, in dismay and consternation, were seeking safety in flight'; 'The angel of death alone can produce such a field as was presented'; captures: 'fifteen battle-flags or banners' turned in (others 'surreptitiously disposed of'), Lt. Potter's collection of 2,500 stand of arms + ~1,000 left on the ground, prisoners 'cannot be less than 1,500'; Col. Smyth [printed 'Smith'] w head/face by shell, returned next day; division loss 'in the two days' action ... 1,285 killed, wounded, and missing' (p. 455). Public domain.

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated clocks, both late-stated: 'nearly 2 p.m.' at the opening (chain 13:07 -> ~-50) and '4.30 o'clock' at the step-off (chain 15:05 -> ~-85). Joins the late-stated pole (Longstreet -53, Lang -53, Smyth, Sawyer) - plausibly the same right-wing/II-Corps-sector timepiece culture as his brigade commander Smyth. Report-nominal (rule 4): never defines an anchor; the envelope spans the two derivations.

sources.title: Col. Samuel S. Carroll's report (1st Brigade, 3rd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 456-458 (No. 116)
sources.author: Samuel S. Carroll
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 456-458). Dated July 5, 1863, Two Taverns (written DURING the campaign - the earliest-dated brigade report in the Union set fetched so far). July 2 keys: 'formed in line of regiments, right in front, between Woodruff's battery on the left and the Taneytown road on the right, at about 8 a.m.'; four companies 4th Ohio out as skirmishers; the EVENING MOVE: ordered 'immediately to the assistance of part of the Eleventh Corps supporting batteries on Cemetery Hill, as they were being driven back', moved with THREE regiments (14th Ind leading; 8th Ohio left out on the skirmish line), 'guided by an aide of General Howard's'; the 7th WV changing front in the fight. July 3 keys: the three regiments HELD EAST CEMETERY HILL 'until the 5th', 'exposed to a great deal of cross-firing during the heavy cannonading of the 3d', Ames's division connecting - i.e. Carroll's brigade (minus the 8th Ohio) was NOT on the II Corps front for the repulse; the 8th Ohio 'retained their position in front of the extreme right of the corps until after the severe fighting of the 3d' (24+ hours out) and 'changed front, and opened fire on their flank'. Ledger: 252 prisoners taken, 113 enemy wounded cared for, 37 enemy dead buried, 349 stand of arms collected. No stated afternoon clocks (sequence-only source; no clockProfile). Public domain.

sources.title: Col. John Coons's report (14th Indiana Infantry, Carroll's brigade), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 458-459 (No. 117)
sources.author: John Coons
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 458 tail). Dated July 5, 1863, near Gettysburg. Keys: July 2 'At 4.30 p.m. the enemy opened upon our position with artillery; their fire was heavy and continuous'; 'At 6 o'clock I was ordered to support the Eighth Ohio' (the picket-line support relation on the Emmitsburg-road front, before the evening East Cemetery Hill move); regiment led Carroll's night column to the hill. Fetched as the Carroll-brigade regimental cross-check; not read in full. Public domain.

sources.title: Lt. Col. Franklin Sawyer's report (8th Ohio Infantry), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 461-462
sources.author: Franklin Sawyer
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 461-462). THE 8th Ohio flank-action primary (the documented counterpart of Brockenbrough's break, csa-3c-het-2). Keys: July 2 ordered to 'move my regiment forward to the picket line in front of our position and on the left of the pickets of the Eleventh Corps' (the advanced position held 24+ hours); July 3: 'Soon after 2 p.m. the enemy opened a terrific fire from sixty-four pieces of artillery, in a semicircle which inclosed my position' (a gun-count + geometry statement from INSIDE the pocket); picket-line loss to noon July 3: 4 k + 1 capt, 1 lt, sgt-maj, 38 men w; 'The enemy did not advance upon us in force until about 4 p.m. of the 3d'; THE FLANK ACTION: 'I changed front forward on the left company, thus presenting our front to the left flank of the advancing rebel column'; 'Our fire was poured into their flank with terrible effect for a few minutes before the Second Brigade at the battery opened, but almost instantly on the fire from the front, together with the concentrated fire from our batteries, the whole mass gave way' (sequencing: HIS fire preceded Smyth's front volley - tier D bracket); ~200 prisoners, three battle-flags (34th NC [Lowrance's] and 38th Va [Armistead's] identified - colors collected in the general collapse, not only from Brockenbrough's front); total loss both days 101 k+w + 1 m = 102 (matches the official return's 102 exactly). Public domain.

clockProfile.assessment: 'Soon after 2 p.m.' at the opening (chain 13:07 -> ~-55) and 'about 4 p.m.' for the column reaching his front (chain ~15:10-15:15 -> ~-50). A consistent late-stated regimental clock; joins the Longstreet/Lang/Hays-division cluster. Report-nominal (rule 4).

sources.title: Maj. Gen. Abner Doubleday's report (I Corps July 1; 3rd Division July 2-3), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 243-259 (July 3 narrative pp. 258-259)
sources.author: Abner Doubleday
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 252-259 sampled; the July 3 narrative is pp. 258-259; correspondence about the report's revision dated January 1864 rides p. 257 - a LATE, litigious document, written after his I Corps command grievance). July 3 keys: 'About 2 p.m. a terrific artillery fire opened on us from more than 100 guns. The firing was accurate and incessant, and lasted for several hours'; 'Toward 5 o'clock I received notice from General Hancock and others that the final charge of the enemy had commenced'; DIVISION STRUCTURE STATEMENT (the pass-4 frame for Biddle/Stone): 'The Vermonters, with the Twentieth New York and One hundred and fifty-first Pennsylvania Volunteers, held the front line during the remainder of the action, and the troops of Rowley's and Dana's brigades ... held the second and third lines'; p. 259 gives his own narrative of the 13th/16th Vt flank maneuvers ('changed front forward on the first company ... at right angles to the main line'; the second column 'scooped almost en masse into our lines'; 16th Vt taking 'the regimental colors of the Second Florida and Eighth Virginia Regiments, and the battle-flag of another rebel regiment') executed 'with the promptness and precision of battalion drill' - Doubleday's flank-attack account agrees with Stannard/Veazey/Benedict against Hancock's I-directed-it reading only in emphasis; both carried on the Hancock conflict record (us-ii-hq-hancock.md). Public domain.
