PASS-10 JULY 1 NARRATIVE READ (2026-07-12, IA full text pp. 244-252 in full; civilwar.com lacks pp. 244-246): the 7-8 o'clock Reynolds telegram briefing at Marsh Creek; the hour-and-a-half-to-two-hours corps stagger ('Wadsworth's division was, therefore, obliged to sustain the brunt of the action alone for this length of time'); Reynolds superintending Cutler's placement in person; the Iron Brigade deployment order R-to-L (2d Wis, 7th Wis, 19th Ind, 24th Mich; 6th Wis + brigade guard detached as HIS reserve); 'If we can't hold it, where will you find men who can?'; the charge ('hurled the enemy back into the run, captured... nearly 1,000 prisoners - all from Archer's brigade'); Archer taken by Private Patrick Maloney, 2d Wis (k later that day); 'this melancholy event occurred in the beginning of the attack referred to, about 10.15 a.m.' - THE 10.15 PIN'S THIRD PRIMARY (tablet + Wadsworth + Doubleday); the railroad-cut maneuver 'accomplished in less than half an hour, and before General Howard had arrived on the field or assumed command' (his side of the feud); 'Ewell's forces advanced about 1.30 p.m. in two deployed lines, supported by a third line of battalions en masse' (his Rodes-attack clock, 30 min EARLY of the tablet 14:00 - recorded, not adopted); the Stone/Baxter/Paul rotations with Robinson's noon-to-nearly-5 quote; 'About 4 p.m. the enemy, having been strongly re-enforced, advanced in large numbers, everywhere deploying into double and triple lines' - the collapse clock agreeing with the tablet's 'At 4 P.M. the Corps retired'; the seminary barricade stand (Glenn's HQ-guard twenty minutes); 'The First Corps only consisted of about 8,200 men when it entered the battle. It was reduced at the close of the engagement to about 2,450' (his litigious EC2/EC6 frame - the enemy-strength estimates in the same passage are wild overcounts, carried with caveat); the cemetery scene: 'Major-General Hancock now rode up, and informed me he had been placed in command of both corps... Immediately afterward orders came from Major-General Howard, who ranked Hancock, to send the troops in another direction' - THE DUAL-COMMAND FRICTION WITNESSED BY A THIRD PARTY (CA-J1P-7 conflict record evidence).

clockProfile.assessment: 'About 2 p.m.' at the opening (chain 13:07 -> -53, the Longstreet value exactly) and 'Toward 5 o'clock' for notice of the charge (chain 15:05-15:15 -> ~-105/-115, but 'received notice' may lag the event and may fold in the Wilcox echelon). A late-stated, six-months-retrospective report clock; the envelope spans the two derivations. Report-nominal (rule 4); never defines.

sources.title: Col. Theodore B. Gates's report (20th N.Y. State Militia / 80th New York, with the 151st Pennsylvania at the line July 3), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 321-323
sources.author: Theodore B. Gates
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 321-323; signature 'THEODORE B. GATES, Colonel, Commanding' addressed to Doubleday). The Biddle-brigade July 3 executor primary (Gates commanded the two-regiment demi-brigade at the front line). Keys: July 1 night 'posted in line along the Taneytown road'; 'About 5 p.m. on July 2, the brigade was ordered to the left center, to support the Second Corps'; position 'halted on the last and lowest of the ridges running nearly north and south between the Taneytown and Emmitsburg roads'; July 3 dawn: 'About 5 a.m. on the 3d, the enemy opened with artillery, and for some time kept up a brisk fire' (the Culp's Hill morning fight heard from the center); the charge: enemy 'debouched from the orchard and woods on his right center, and moved in two lines of battle across the fields' after a near-three-hour cannonade of 100+ guns; THE MOVEMENT: 'by the right flank up to the foot of the bluff, delivering our fire as we marched, and keeping between the enemy and the object of his enterprise' (the lateral slide north toward the Copse, EC4); fire opened at ~200 feet; 'Two regiments advanced to the fence and poured a volley into the enemy at very short range, who now completely broke'; 'took a large number of prisoners'; 'Two colors were left upon the ground by the enemy'; loss stated 170 (3 off k, 15 off w, 1 off m; 32 men k, 96 w, 23 m) = the 80th NY's own return row exactly (or-27-1-union-return p. 174). Public domain.

sources.title: Return of Casualties in the Union forces at Gettysburg, July 1-3, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 155-187 (regiment grain; revised statement p. 187)
sources.author: U.S. War Department compilation
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 174-176 read at regiment grain - the Union counterpart of the ANV return, THREE-DAY battle totals with a full k/w/captured-missing column set (unlike the ANV return's pooled/absent missing, ED-49's asymmetry runs the other way here). Key rows this pass: I Corps 3rd Div - Biddle's brigade 898 (80th NY 170, 121st Pa 179, 142nd Pa 211, 151st Pa 337), Stone's brigade 853 (143rd Pa 253, 149th Pa 336, 150th Pa 264), Stannard's 351 (13th Vt 123, 14th Vt 107, 16th Vt 119; the pass-1 figure re-verified), division aggregate 2,103. II Corps 2nd Div - Harrow 768 (19th Me 202?, 15th Ma 148, 1st Minn 224, 82nd NY 192 - brigade sums verbatim '10 off 137 men k; 46 off 527 men w; 1 off 47 men c/m'), WEBB 491 (69th Pa 137, 71st Pa 98, 72nd Pa 192, 106th Pa 64), Hall 377. II Corps 3rd Div - CARROLL 211 (14th Ind 31, 4th Oh 31, 8TH OHIO 102, 7th WV 47), Smyth 366. Scope note: three-day totals; day/episode splits require the unit reports (e.g. Webb's report 525 vs the return's 491 - conflict carried; 8th Ohio report 102 = return 102 exact). Public domain. PASS-5 EXTRACTION (pp. 179-180, V Corps rows): Vincent's brigade - 20th ME 29/91/5=125, 16th MI 23/34/3=60, 44th NY 26/82/3=111, 83rd PA 10/45/0=55, brigade 352; Weed's brigade - 140th NY 26/89/18=133 (aggregate reconciles exactly), 91st PA/146th NY/155th PA rows read with column-alignment noise (aggregates ~19/28/19 class; brigade total 200, 2nd Division total 1,029 - both canonical); Martin's artillery brigade - Battery D 5th US 7/6/0=13 (vs the tablet's 14: a return-vs-tablet conflict, ED-52 adopts the return). PASS-6 EXTRACTION (pp. 175, 177-180): II Corps 1st Div (Caldwell) blocks - Cross 330 (5th NH 80, 61st NY 62, 81st PA 62, 148th PA 125; report=return EXACT), Kelly 198 (28th MA 100, 63rd NY 23, 69th NY 25, 88th NY 28, 116th PA 22; report 202 carried), Zook/Fraser 358 (52nd NY 38, 57th NY 34, 66th NY 44, 140th PA 241), Brooke 389 (27th CT 37, 2nd DE 84, 64th NY 98, 53rd PA 80, 145th PA 90), division 1,275. III Corps 1st Div - Ward 781 (20th IN 156, 3rd ME 122, 4th ME 144-class [row read with column noise: k+w 70 + pooled captured ~74 - re-read flagged], 86th NY 66, 124th NY 90, 99th PA 110, 1st USSS 49, 2nd USSS 43), de Trobriand 490 (17th ME 133, 3rd MI 45, 5th MI 109, 40th NY 150, 110th PA 53; report=return EXACT), Burling 513; III Corps artillery - Clark 20, Winslow 18, SMITH'S 4TH NY 13 (2/10/1; report states 12 + one pooled missing), Bucklyn 30, Seeley 25 = 106. V Corps 1st Div - Tilton 125 (18th MA 54, 22nd MA 31, 1st MI 42, 118th PA 25; report 109 carried), Sweitzer 427 (9th MA 7, 32nd MA 80, 4th MI 165, 62nd PA 175), Vincent 352 + staff 1, division 904. WEED DIGIT-NOISE RE-READ (standing pass-5 item, CLOSED): p. 180 re-read gives clean aggregates 140th NY 133 / 146th NY 28 / 91st PA 19 / 155th PA 19 (sum 199 + staff 1 = brigade 200 canonical); the 146th/91st/155th COLUMN split still carries alignment noise (e.g. 146th read 4k/2w/22m vs the published 4k/24w shape) - aggregates firm, splits flagged. PASS-7 ADDITION (2026-07-11, pp. 177-180 re-read at brigade grain): III Corps - Graham 740 (6+61k/45+463w/6+159m; 141st PA row 149 = Birney's 149-of-200 EXACT; 114th PA k-column '9 officers/1 man' carries alignment noise, flagged), Ward 781, Carr 790, Brewster 778, Burling 513, division (Humphreys) 2,092 vs his stated 2,088 (ED-52: return adopted, report carried); V Corps - Day 382, Burbank 447, McCandless 155, Fisher 55 (sum 210 = Crawford's stated '20 officers and 190 men'); VI Corps 3rd Div 3rd Bde row beyond p. 180 (unfetched; the tablet's 53 stands as the working reading). PASS-8 ROWS (2026-07-11, pp. 177-187 re-read): 4th Maine CLEAN RE-READ (the pass-5 garble closed): 2+9 k / 3+56 w / 4+70 m = 144 (the heavy missing column = the Devil's Den pocket); Winslow's D/1st NY 10 w + 8 m = 18 (CONFLICT vs his report's '1 killed, 16 wounded, 3 missing' = 20 - both carried); Smith's 4th NY 2/10/1 = 13; Watson's I/5th US 1 k + 1+18 w + 2 m = 22 (= Martin's '1 officer and 21 men' EXACT); p. 182: Wheaton's/Nevin's 3rd Bde 3rd Div VI Corps 2 k + 7+44 w = 53 (= the tablet's 53 EXACT - the pass-7 open EC6 row CLOSED); von Gilsa 527; Ames's/Harris's 2nd Bde 1st Div XI 5+63 k / 24+342 w / 9+335 m = 778 (17th CT 197); p. 183: XI Corps arty - Wiedrich's I/1st NY 3 k + 2+8 w = 13 (vs his report's enumerated 3 k + 11 w = 14, one-man scope conflict flagged); corps total 3,801; pp. 184-185: XII Corps - Greene's brigade 60th NY 52 / 78th NY 30 / 102d NY 29 / 137th NY 137 (4+36 k, 3+84 w, 10 m) / 149th NY 55 = 6+61 k / 10+202 w / 1+23 m = 303 (CONFLICT vs Greene's own report table 62 k/213 w/32 m = 307, both carried); McDougall 80, Lockwood 174, Colgrove 279, Candy 139, Kane/Cobham 98, corps total 1,082; p. 187: Artillery Reserve - 9th Massachusetts Battery 1+7 k / 2+16 w / 2 m = 28 (= Milton's itemized July 2 loss 8 k + 17 w + 2 prisoners = 27 PLUS his 1 w July 3 = 28, arithmetic EXACT); Ricketts's F&G/1st PA 6 k / 1+13 w / 3 m = 23 (= his report's 'Killed, 6; wounded, 14; missing, 3' EXACT); AR total 242; army total 23,049. PASS-9 EXTENSION (p. 184, XII Corps rows): McDougall 1+11 k / 4+56 w / 1+7 m = 80 (20th CT 28 the largest row); Lockwood 3+32 k / 3+118 w / 0+18 m = 174 (1st MD PHB 104, 1st MD ES 25, 150th NY 45); Colgrove 2+47 k / 20+205 w / 0+5 m = 279 (27th IN 110, 2nd MA 136, 13th NJ 21, 107th NY 2, 3rd WI 10); p. 185 (re-read): Greene 303, 2nd Div total 540, Arty Bde 9 w (Knap's 3, F 4th US 1, K 5th US 5), corps total 1,082. PASS-10 ROWS (2026-07-12, pp. 173/182/183/185 read): I Corps 1st Div - Meredith 1,153 (19th Ind 210, 24th Mich 363, 2d Wis 233, 6th Wis 168, 7th Wis 178), Cutler 1,002 (76th NY 234, 84th NY/14th Bkln 217, 95th NY 115, 147th NY 296, 56th Pa 130, 7th Ind ~20 [row thin - on rear duty July 1]), division 2,155. XI Corps 1st Div (p. 182): von Gilsa 527 (41st NY 75, 54th NY 102, 68th NY 138, 153d Pa 211), Ames 778 (17th Conn 197, 25th Ohio 184, 75th Ohio 186, 107th Ohio 211), division 1,306. XI Corps total 3,801; 3d Div 1,476 (p. 183). Cavalry (p. 185): Gamble 99, Devin 28, Reserve Bde 291 (6th US 242, 'notably incurred at Fairfield'), 1st Cav Div 418 - the Gamble/Devin rows are CAMPAIGN totals (their July 1 share dominant for Gamble; Devin's 28 spread across July 1 + the retreat screening).

sources.title: Maj. Gen. George G. Meade's report (Army of the Potomac), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 114-119 (results/burials pages fetched)
sources.author: George G. Meade
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 118-119 only - the results statements, fetched for the pass-4 prisoner cross-check). Keys: campaign results '13,621 prisoners', '3 guns', '41 standards'; army casualties '2,834 killed, 13,709 [13,713] wounded, and 6,643 missing; in all, 23,186 [23,190]' (OR editors' brackets; revised statement cross-referenced at p. 187); BURIAL LEDGER: '126 Confederate officers and 2,764 men were buried by our troops at Gettysburg' (p. 119) - a Union-side floor on CSA killed left on the field, and the second leg of the missing-column cross-check (ED-49). Public domain.

sources.title: Maj. William T. Poague's report (Artillery Battalion, Pender's Division), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 pp. 673-674 (No. 562)
sources.author: William T. Poague
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 673-674 transcribed in full). Dated July 30, 1863, Culpeper County. Keys: battalion = 3 batteries + Graham's (NC) joined June 21; with Pender until July 1 morning, then 'detached, and directed to remain at Cashtown until further orders. About 11 o'clock I was ordered to the front, but THE BATTALION TOOK NO PART IN THE ENGAGEMENT OF JULY 1 AND 2' (attested non-participation, ED-44 class); 'Late in the evening of the 2d, by your order [R.L. Walker], I reported to Major-General Anderson ... and at last succeeded in getting ten of my guns into position. The balance (six howitzers) were kept a short distance in rear, as no place could be found from which they could be used with advantage'; GEOMETRY: 'three rifles and two Napoleons were posted on the left of Anderson's division, and not far from Pegram's battalion, and on the right of these and in front of Anderson's left, at the distance of 400 yards, five Napoleons'; 'about 1,400 yards from the enemy's batteries'; 'Immediately on my right were the batteries of the First Corps'; command split: Wyatt over 3 Wyatt + 2 Graham guns (the north group), Ward over Ward + Brooke (the Napoleon group); July 3 ~07:00: Wyatt's five guns opened (Hill's order, Poague not knowing its origin, ceased it - 'the contest was a very unequal one'), Wyatt lost 8 horses, an enemy caisson exploded; 'In the general engagement that occurred about the middle of the day, the battalion participated'; post-repulse: 'anticipating an advance of the enemy, I ordered up the howitzers. The enemy, however, failed to follow up his advantage, and I got no service out of those useless guns'; July 4 dusk: march toward Hagerstown. No stated afternoon clocks (sequence-only; no clockProfile). Public domain.

sources.title: Isaac R. Trimble, 'The Battle and Campaign of Gettysburg', Southern Historical Society Papers vol. 26 (1898), pp. 116-128
sources.author: Isaac R. Trimble
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 - the pass-3 standing-fetch item CLOSED (the previously recorded item id southernhistoric26sout 503s; the working mirror is southernhistoricv26sout, server ia801603). 'From the original MS. Furnished by Major Graham Daves ... originally written for one of the Veteran Associations and has never been in print' (SHSP editorial headnote). July 3 keys: Lee's order of battle READ BY TRIMBLE, quoted: 'General Longstreet will make a vigorous attack on his front; General Ewell will threaten the enemy on the left, or make a vigorous attack, should circumstances justify it: General Hill will hold the centre at all hazards'; 'preparations were ready by one o'clock'; 'After that tremendous cannonade of one and a half hours' (a ~1.5-h duration statement, McGilvery's value); 'I think this charge was made about three o'clock, and by four it was over'; geometry: 'Heth's Division marched in fine order, in a line with Picket's, about 200 yards in advance of Pender's two brigades'; THE FENCE CLIMAX + HIS WOUNDING PIN: 'drove the enemy from the fence at the road, where our men stopped and began firing, instead of mounting the fence; while making efforts to get them over the fence I was wounded. While at the fence the exposure was dreadful. The incessant discharge of canister, shell and musketry was more than any troops could endure'; the retreat: 'The brigades of Pender yielding ground, began to move back slowly and in good order, not breaking ranks even' and 'these brigades were the last troops to leave the field'; his no-renewal decision quoted ('No; the best thing the men can do, is to get out of this'); 'The battle of Gettysburg was fought on 3rd in reality by three divisions - Pickett's, Heth's, under Pettigrew, Pender's, under Trimble'. CONFLICT: his men-stopped-AT-THE-ROAD-FENCE reading vs Lane's 'advanced to within a few yards of the stone wall' (or-27-2-lane-jh) - carried on csa-3c-pen-hq-trimble.md/csa-3c-pen-2-lane.md. Public domain.

clockProfile.assessment: Three stated clocks, all near-chain: 'ready by one o'clock' (signal 13:07 -> +7), charge 'about three o'clock' (chain 15:05 -> +5), 'by four it was over' (~chain). His 1.5-h cannonade duration statement rides CA-J3A-4/5 as chain evidence for the short-duration school (with Peyton, McGilvery), not as a clock. Decades-later MS caps confidence; a civil engineer's stated care with chronology ('put down at a wrong hour' complaint) noted.

sources.title: Oliver W. Norton, The Attack and Defense of Little Round Top (1913) - compiled primary accounts incl. the 146th NY Garrard-staff narrative
sources.author: Oliver W. Norton (compiler)
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (full text). Fetched as the Rittenhouse-paper substitute route (the MOLLUS paper itself is STILL unfetched - see rittenhouse-mollus-1887); Norton does not reprint it, but the compiled 146th NY account (a Garrard staff officer, pp. ~300-302) is a same-hill witness to the battery's July 3 fire: 'During Lee's great charge on the afternoon of July 3, Hazlett's battery (commanded by Lieutenant Rittenhouse after Hazlett was killed) had a terrible raking fire on the rebel line, which was made with great effect. This caused a concentration of the fire of many rebel batteries upon Hazlett's position, their shell and shot crashing among the rocks as if the world was coming to an end' - the receiving-and-return record for us-btty-rittenhouse.md EC5 (pairs with Peyton's 10-men-to-a-shell enfilade from the target side). Also: Weed's brigade loss table (staff 1, 140th NY 133, 146th NY 28, 91st Pa 19, 155th Pa 19 = 200); July 3 V Corps hospital displaced from the Weikert house by overshooting shells; Meade/Warren on the summit July 3. Public domain. PASS-5 PROMOTION: from Rittenhouse-substitute to the CA-J2P compiled-primary base (the ED-28 precondition source, now read in full): ch. VII the WARREN LETTERS (July 13 + 24, 1872, to Farley, from the original MSS) - Meade sent Warren to the left from Sickles's position; the signal-station shot into the woods; Hazlett up (Warren + stragglers lifting a gun over the rocks); O'Rorke detached personally by Warren; Warren w by musket ball while talking with Hazlett; the second letter's honest hedges ('I will not be sure that Hazlett's battery got up before I went down to Colonel O'Rorke'; 'If I detached Vincent's brigade I don't recollect it'); ch. IV Norton's compiled ruling - Meade rode left ~15:00, Warren on the hill 'a little before half past three', cannonade opened ~15:30, Hood's infantry after ~30 min, 'The attack on Vincent's brigade must have commenced at some time between 4:30 and 5 P.M.', with Woodward's (83rd PA) 1912 letter ADMITTING his published time error - veteran-clock fallibility documented by the veteran; Farley's 140th NY account (Weed's dying requests, 'I'm as dead a man as Julius Caesar', died 'at about nine o'clock that evening'); Law's B&L reprint incl. the 16:00 signal dispatch; Stine's Law-arrival 'a little before twelve'; Rice sent Chamberlain up Great Round Top 'about nine o'clock'.

sources.title: D. Scott Hartwig (GNMP), 'Prisoners of War' series, Blog of Gettysburg National Military Park (Nov-Dec 2012)
sources.author: D. Scott Hartwig
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (parts 1-3 sampled). The institutional-secondary compilation for the pass-4 prisoner cross-check (ED-49's missing-column problem): 'Of the estimated 51,000 casualties ... approximately 17,500 were prisoners of war. Of this number 5,365 were Union and 12,227 (6,802 wounded and 5,425 unwounded) were Confederates' (part 1; NO page-cited source given for the split - carried as attributed-institutional, not return-grade). Part 3 documents the processing pipeline at unit grain for one case (20th NC: ~200 reported captured, 123 in the prisoner records; Fort Delaware/Point Lookout destinations) - the record-vs-report attrition pattern that cautions any per-unit reconciliation. Per-regiment prisoner-record compilations (Busey, Nothing But Glory) remain print-only: acquisition item.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. J. B. Kershaw, CSA, commanding brigade, McLaws's division (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 2, pp. 366-371)
sources.author: Joseph B. Kershaw
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route, id = 189683 + page; pp. 366-370 read). Dated October 1, 1863, near Chattanooga (3-month retrospective report). July 2 keys: 'About 3 p.m. the head of my column came into the open field in front of a stone wall'; filed right along the wall and formed under skirmish cover; the tactical-exposure worry (attacking the Peach Orchard would expose his right); 'about 4 o'clock' received the order to advance (the McLaws-wing EARLY clock in the commander's own report - the tablet's 4.30 is a partial correction, modern ~17:30); the 3-gun Cabell signal is in his B&L account (via alexander-1907 quote); obstacle catalogue for the advance (fences, stone building and barn, morass, raking grape/canister); 2nd SC ordered to charge the battery; 7th+3rd to the stony hill; 'Barksdale's brigade subsequently cleared the orchard'; the Wheatfield two-line counterattack lapping his line at 30 paces; Semmes brought up; 'Wofford coming in in splendid style' swept the field 'up to the foot of the mountain'; withdrawal 'near nightfall'; officer losses (De Saussure k, McLeod mw, Kennedy w, Bland w); July 3: after dark July 2 to the Peach Orchard, held to noon July 3, then the stone wall, right connecting Hood; casualty statement 115 k / 483 w / 32 m = 630 (EXACT match to both the ANV return brigade row and the tablet - the wing's clean agreement case).

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated clocks: column into the open field 'about 3 p.m.' and the advance order 'about 4 o'clock'. Against the ED-28 provisional Kershaw step-off ~17:30 (CA-J2A-7) the stated 16:00 gives a raw offset of ~+90; the tablet's '4.30' (tablet-adjudicated, McLaws-wing -60 class per ED-25/ED-28) sits between them. Pass-5 finding: the McLaws-wing early skew ORIGINATES in the reports - the tablets partially corrected it. Per ED-25 rule 4 the report clock never defines or moves an anchor.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. J. B. Robertson, CSA, commanding Texas Brigade, Hood's division (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 2, pp. 404-406)
sources.author: Jerome B. Robertson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 404-406 read). Dated July 17, 1863, near Bunker Hill, Va. July 2 keys: the division arrived 'but a few minutes before we were ordered to advance' (no reconnaissance time - a first-person negative on the arrival-to-step-off interval); ordered to keep right on Law and left on the Emmitsburg Pike, found the line too narrow and ABANDONED THE PIKE to close on Law (the order-conflict decision that shaped the brigade's split); half a mile of open ground under 'a heavy and destructive fire of canister, grape, and shell from six pieces' (Smith's 4th NY); 1st TX took the battery hill (3 of 6 guns); 4th/5th TX drifted right to Law against Little Round Top; 'one of the hottest contests, against five or six times their number' lasting over an hour with positions retaken repeatedly; courier to Hood for left support brought Anderson and Benning; officer casualties (Key w, Powell w, Manning w, Carter w, Bryan w, Woodward w); Robertson himself struck above the knee 'late in the evening', retired ~200 yards, command to Lt. Col. Work; July 3 continuous sharpshooter skirmishing, 'a number of our men severely wounded'; Capt. F. L. Price AAG lost the morning of July 3 examining positions ('never seen again'); withdrew about dark July 3 to a new line. No stated clocks anywhere (kind none - sequence evidence throughout).

sources.title: Report of Col. William C. Oates, Fifteenth Alabama Infantry, Law's brigade (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 2, pp. 392-393)
sources.author: William C. Oates
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 392-393 read). Dated August 8, 1863. The Little Round Top attacker's contemporary report (his 1905 book is the retrospective pair, oates-1905): the left-wheel order he declined to obey (justified by 'tactical confusion and enemy positioning'); Lt. Col. Feagin w (lost a leg); the enemy behind rocks with a 300-yard depression between; the very-large-rock left-wheel to face Chamberlain's flank fire ('in less than five minutes caused him to change front'); 'Receiving re-enforcements, he charged me five times, and was as often repulsed' (the five charges FROM THE ATTACKER'S SIDE - Chamberlain's report mirrors it); the withdrawal trigger ('flanked me on the right, and two regiments were moving rapidly upon my rear and not 200 yards distant'); command passed to Capt. B. A. Hill for the withdrawal; casualty statement 17 k / 54 w / 90 m = 161. ED-49 rule-4 exemplar: the ANV return's 15th AL row prints 17/66 = 83 k+w only (missing pooled at brigade grain as 146) - the report total is the battle-total basis, the return row the component measure. No stated clocks.

sources.title: Report of Lieut. Col. L. H. Scruggs, Fourth Alabama Infantry, Law's brigade (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 2, pp. 391-392)
sources.author: L. H. Scruggs
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 391-392 read). Dated August 8, 1863, addressed to Col. James L. Sheffield commanding Law's brigade (the address line is the verified-negative that LAW FILED NO BRIGADE REPORT - the brigade's OR base is its five regimental reports). Keys: marched ~24 miles from New Guilford, 'arriving at 3:30 p.m.' July 2 (a stated regimental arrival clock - reads as arrival at the deployment area after the approach, consistent with the Kershaw-tablet 3:30 arrival and Alexander's 3:45 guns-in-action); advanced across a plowed field, overran a stone-fence position, up the mountainside; could not carry the second position ('exhausted troops and difficult terrain'); July 3 breastworks of rock, defensive posture; 87 total casualties stated vs the return row 17/49 = 66 k+w (missing hidden in the brigade pool - ED-49 rule-4 case).

sources.title: Report of Col. James C. Rice, 44th New York, commanding 3rd Brigade, 1st Division, V Corps after Vincent fell (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 1, pp. 616-619)
sources.author: James C. Rice
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 616-618 read; p. 619 fetch returned menu-only, retry open). Dated July 31, 1863. THE Vincent-brigade primary: occupied 'the extreme left of our line of battle' at about 4 p.m. July 2 (report-nominal; modern ~16:45); order of the line 20th ME left / 83rd PA + 44th NY center / 16th MI right; 'about 1,000 muskets' engaged (the brigade's own strength statement - primary); the ledge-of-rocks + smooth-ravine ground description; Hood's division attacking 'along the entire line' in three columns without skirmishers; the 16th MI right breaking and the 140th NY's supporting arrival; Vincent mw in that crisis; Chamberlain's half-hour struggle and the right-angle refuse + charge (brigade-report corroboration of the 20th ME record); the ammunition crisis (multiple commanders out, every rear officer/man pressed into cartridge supply); Fisher's five 3rd-Division regiments arriving; Chamberlain ordered to take the mountain - 'It was now 8 o clock in the evening' (the LRT repulse-complete evening clock, primary); complete possession before 9 p.m.; 500+ prisoners incl. 2 colonels + 15 officers, 1,000+ muskets collected; July 3 relieved during the forenoon by the 1st Brigade and moved to the center as reserve under 'severe cannonading' without loss.

clockProfile.assessment: One stated clock pair: in position 'about 4 p.m.' (vs Norton's compiled ruling that first contact on Vincent came 16:30-17:00 and the diversion ~16:00-16:30; raw +45) and 'now 8 o'clock in the evening' for the repulse complete (inside CA-J2A-6's fight 16:45-19:00 window - roughly chain-agreeing at the evening end). Per ED-25 rule 4 the report clock never defines an anchor; the 20:00 statement is carried as the best Union primary clock for the LRT fight's end.

sources.title: Report of Col. Joshua L. Chamberlain, Twentieth Maine Infantry (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 1, pp. 622-626)
sources.author: Joshua L. Chamberlain
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 622-625 read; the ED-28 precondition fetch, closing the CA-J2P verification item). Dated July 6, 1863, 'Field near Emmitsburg'. Keys: reached 'the heights southeasterly of Gettysburg at about 7 a.m., July 2'; 'about 4 p.m.' a cannonade signaled the rapid movement left (his artillery-opening clock, chain-agreeing with CA-J2A-2's window); the move to Little Round Top under artillery fire; Company B deployed as skirmishers; the left-flank refuse when the enemy's extension threatened; 'brisk fire at close range' opening within a dozen yards; 'One-half of my left wing had fallen, and a third of my regiment lay just behind us, dead or badly wounded'; the ammunition crisis and the order - 'the word was enough. It ran like fire along the line'; the extended right wheel; first-line surrenders; ~400 prisoners (15th + 47th AL chiefly, some 4th/5th TX) with ~150 k+w counted in front; at 9 p.m. ordered by Rice to seize the western slope of Great Round Top, done with ~200 men, fixed bayonets, without firing; Lt. Linscott mw there; 25 more prisoners incl. a staff officer; 83rd PA + 44th NY reinforced the crest; night picket rotation. Return row (or-27-1-union-return p. 179): 29 k / 91 w / 5 m = 125 - carried per ED-52 as the EC6 basis against any larger narrative figure.

clockProfile.assessment: Stated clocks: 07:00 arrival on the heights, ~16:00 cannonade opening (vs CA-J2A-2 adopted 15:45-16:00 and Jacobs's 16:20 reading - roughly chain-agreeing, raw +10 against the window center), 21:00 Great Round Top order (agrees with Rice's before-9 framing). A rare V Corps report whose clock does not ride the early pole.

sources.title: Report of Col. Kenner Garrard, 146th New York, commanding 3rd Brigade, 2nd Division, V Corps after Weed fell (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 1, pp. 651-652)
sources.author: Kenner Garrard
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 651-652 read). Dated July 16, 1863, camp near Berlin, Md. THE Weed-brigade primary: the brigade advanced ~1.5 miles under fire; the leading regiment (140th NY, Col. O'Rorke) 'was directed by General Warren to Round Top ridge' (the detachment order in the brigade's own report, naming Warren); close-range combat; 'Colonel O'Rorke was mortally wounded at the head of his regiment while leading it into action'; other regiments deployed supporting Third Corps and artillery; then the 91st + 155th PA positioned on the ridge; the night stone wall 'about half way down the slope, wherever the rocks offered no protection' (the fortification record); 'General Weed ... was mortally wounded on the summit, near the battery'; 'Lieutenant Hazlett, commanding the battery, while offering his assistance to General Weed, fell, mortally wounded' (the paired summit pins in one primary). No stated clocks (kind none).

sources.title: Report of Maj. Gen. George Sykes, commanding V Corps (OR Series I Vol. XXVII Part 1, pp. 592-595)
sources.author: George Sykes
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 592-593 read). Corps frame for the July 2 southern field: reached the field 'about 8 a.m.' July 2 after the night march; first posted on the right near Rock Creek; 'At 3 p.m.' Meade ordered the whole corps to the left 'and hold it at all hazards' (report-nominal); the Little Round Top reconnaissance ('a rocky ridge, commanding almost an entire view of the plateau'); Vincent 'had seized the rocky height, closely followed by Weed's brigade'; the WEED VACATED incident - Weed's brigade found moving off toward the Peach Orchard 'By order of General Sickles', Sykes ordered it back 'at the double-quick step' (the cross-order conflict that explains why only O'Rorke's regiment made the crisis); Vincent and O'Rorke 'sorely pressed' and fallen, 'Weed again in position, Hazlett working his guns superbly', Ayres's regulars arriving; Crawford's evening advance; 'the key of the battle-field was in our possession intact'.

clockProfile.assessment: One stated clock: Meade's order to move the corps left 'At 3 p.m.' - just before/around Warren's arrival on Little Round Top (CA-J2A-4 adopted 15:30-16:00 for Warren; Norton rules Meade's ride to the left ~15:00-15:25). Raw offset ~+15 against the window center; roughly chain-agreeing. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Evander M. Law, 'The Struggle for Round Top' (Battles and Leaders of the Civil War, vol. 3)
sources.author: Evander M. Law
sources.editionNote: Read 2026-07-11 via two independent reprint routes: Norton 1913 ch. II reprints the Round Top sections (pp. ~115-119 of Norton) and alexander-1907 quotes the Devil's Den passage - carried as reprint-verified, direct B&L pagination open. ~20-year retrospective by the commander who led Law's brigade and succeeded to Hood's division mid-attack. Keys: quotes the Union SIGNAL DISPATCH verbatim - 'To General Meade - four o'clock p.m. The only infantry of the enemy visible is on the extreme (Federal) left; it has been moving toward Emmitsburg' - a timestamped document (16:00) showing the column still deploying at 16:00 ('It was now past four o'clock in the afternoon, and our troops were in position for the attack'); Hood's protest sequence (twice through Hamilton; 'General Longstreet orders that you begin the attack at once'; 'You hear the order?'); the en-echelon order ('the movement should begin on the right, my brigade on that flank leading'); 'It was near 5 o'clock p.m. when we advanced to the attack. The artillery on both sides had been warmly engaged for about fifteen minutes' (his step-off clock - the LATE pole of the July 2 step-off spread, vs Alexander's ~16:15 arithmetic); Hood w by artillery 'as we moved into action'; 'In less than an hour from the time we advanced ... the hill by Devil's Den ... was taken with three pieces' (duration statement, tier-B input); the rough-ground advance description.

clockProfile.assessment: One stated clock: step-off 'near 5 o'clock p.m.' vs CA-J2A-3. RESOLVED 2026-07-11: ED-53 ADOPTED the pass-5 chain revision (CA-J2A-3 ~16:30, envelope 16:15-17:00); against the adopted preferred the raw offset is ~-30, inside the profile envelope, and Law's stated clock sits at the revised envelope's late edge as the ladder's late pole. His quoted 16:00 signal dispatch is a document pin, not a witness clock (carries no profile, anchors at +-0 by document class, per the ED-25 dispatch rule).

sources.title: The War Between the Union and the Confederacy, and Its Lost Opportunities (ch. on Gettysburg second day)
sources.author: William C. Oates
sources.editionNote: Full text fetched 2026-07-11 (Emory scan djvu text; the advancedsearch mirror-hunt lesson from pass 4 applied - title/creator queries failed, the Emory item id served). 42-year retrospective by the 15th AL's colonel; polemical throughout (Longstreet-hostile), facts used with the standard memoir caution. Keys: ordered ready at 3 a.m., 'near 4 o'clock when the brigade was put in motion'; 25 miles; 'arrived on the field within sight of Gettysburg at about 2 o'clock P.M.' (vs Stine's 'a little before twelve' and Scruggs's 3:30 deployment-area clock - the three-way arrival spread carried); Lee and Longstreet observed together on Seminary Ridge; the concealed circuitous flank march; Law's brigade single-line order 44th+48th right of the 15th, 47th+4th left; 'about 3.30 o'clock P.M. both battalions of artillery' [opened]; THE WATER DETAIL - 2 men per company (22 men) sent with all canteens to a well 100 yards rear, the advance ordered before they returned, the detail walked into Union lines and was captured entire (a primary detachment-loss record feeding EC2/EC6); his sharpshooter-pursuit ascent of Great Round Top and the argument with Terrell about holding it; effectives 'about five hundred officers and men' going in, 223 enlisted + 19 officers at the after-dark roll (with stragglers returning through the night - the roll is not a casualty count); 'When the battle commenced, four hours previously' from the dark bivouac (~20:00 - 4h = ~16:00, a retrospective arithmetic consistent with the mid window); the five-charges fight and the withdrawal 'like a herd of wild cattle' (his phrase); Vincent's-brigade-was-there-first framing.

clockProfile.assessment: Two usable statements: artillery opening 'about 3.30 P.M.' (vs CA-J2A-2 15:45-16:00 adopted, Jacobs 16:20 counter-reading: raw +15 to +50) and the dark-minus-four-hours commencement arithmetic (~16:00, agreeing). 42-year distance caps confidence regardless; envelope held wide.

sources.title: Lafayette McLaws, 'Gettysburg' (address before the Georgia Historical Society; Southern Historical Society Papers vol. 7, pp. 64-90)
sources.author: Lafayette McLaws
sources.editionNote: Full text fetched 2026-07-11 (the southernhistoric7187sout mirror - the pass-4 Trimble advancedsearch lesson applied). The DIVISION commander's only substantial account (McLaws filed no OR report for Gettysburg - standing verified-negative). Keys: July 1 - in march order by 8 a.m., held for Ewell's 14-mile train (he timed it over a known distance - a rare quantified logistics datum), 'not until after four o'clock that it had passed'; heard the guns about five o'clock rising the hills; met Longstreet ~10 p.m.; camped a little after midnight; the sunrise countermand of the 4 a.m. march order. July 2 - Lee's map order verbatim ('General, I wish you to place your division across this road ... perpendicular to the Emmettsburg road ... without being seen'); the deployment under short-range artillery fire cutting tree limbs (killed/wounded in line BEFORE the advance - an EC6 pre-step-off window); BARKSDALE'S IMPATIENCE verbatim ('General, let me go; General, let me charge!' two or three times; told to wait on Longstreet's will); Hood's guides/scouts confusion blamed for the incomplete envelopment; the charge order via Capt. G. B. Lamar Jr.; Kershaw leading, Semmes, then Barksdale, Wofford last; Barksdale and Wofford 'mixed up with the batteries ... temporarily delayed in extricating themselves' (the interval Alexander's ladder times at 20+ minutes); 'one Brigadier-General (Barksdale) killed'. No July 2 clock statements (July 1 clocks only).

sources.title: History of the One Hundred and Fiftieth Regiment Pennsylvania Volunteers, Second Regiment, Bucktail Brigade (ch. XV, 'Gettysburg, To a Finish')
sources.author: Thomas Chamberlin
sources.editionNote: Full text fetched 2026-07-11 (the pass-4 standing fetch item - the Stone/Dana upgrade route, CLOSED). Keys for the July 2-3 record the OR lacks (no July 2-3 report exists for Stone's brigade): morning of the 2d 'the aggregate for duty was increased to one hundred and nine' (a stated regimental strength after July 1's wreck - primary); posted supporting batteries on Cemetery Hill, men passing ammunition for the short-handed artillery; 'About six p.m. the brigade was double-quicked to the left, down the Taneytown road' to the right of Third Corps during the Humphreys collapse; line formed in rear of Humphreys, bayonets fixed, charge withheld; the 149th+150th evening advance to the Emmitsburg road recovering TWO GUNS (Doubleday's report quoted concurring: 'sent in two additional guns taken from the enemy, after a short and spirited engagement'); the night picket at the road 'a little to the left of the Codori House'; dawn July 3 shelled by two guns in an orchard left-front; relieved 'between seven and eight o'clock' with Company A losing 2 k + 1 mw to artillery on the way in; the cannonade ridden out 'back of the brow of the hill'; Adjutant Ashhurst at the Bushman farmhouse; compiled 1895 from participant materials (regimental-history tier).

sources.title: Harry W. Pfanz, Gettysburg: Culp's Hill and Cemetery Hill (UNC Press)
sources.author: Harry W. Pfanz
sources.editionNote: ACCESS FAILED 2026-07-11 (pass 5, one-attempt item per the pass commission): the archive.org item exists but is lending-restricted (in-copyright, no anonymous text channel) and the fulltext inside-search endpoint returns 'Item not available' anonymously. The CA-J3M-3 precondition (pp. ~340-355, the Spangler's Meadow ~10:00 ruling) therefore STANDS OPEN; ED-30 remains provisional with the precondition carried. Acquisition routes: archive.org controlled digital lending (account), or library copy. Pass-6 cheap retry (2026-07-11): search confirms the text exists behind JSTOR/Project MUSE/Perlego auth walls and archive.org lending only - no anonymous channel. ED-30's precondition STANDS; CDL/library route unchanged.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. John C. Caldwell, commanding 1st Division, II Corps (OR 27/1 No. 77, pp. 379-381)
sources.author: John C. Caldwell
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, id = 188622 + page; pp. 379-381 read). July 2 keys: division positioned by Hancock in the morning; ordered to report to Sykes with his command (stated 'around 4-5 p.m.' class); ordered Cross 'to advance in line of battle through a wheat-field'; Kelly committed on Cross's right, Zook farther right to connect with the Third Corps, Brooke in reserve; Cross's brigade 'had been longest engaged, had expended all its ammunition' - called Brooke to relieve; the sweep 'over difficult and rocky ground ... desperately contested by the slowly retreating foe' to 'a strong defensive position'; requested support from Barnes, who ordered Sweitzer's brigade into the wheat-field; the collapse - 'all the troops on my right had broken and were fleeing to the rear in great confusion', flanked, withdrawal; 'Brigadier-General Zook and Colonel Cross, of the Fifth New Hampshire Volunteers, fell, mortally wounded'; 'Colonel Roberts, One hundred and fortieth Pennsylvania Volunteers, and Lieutenant-Colonel Merwin, Twenty-seventh Connecticut Volunteers, were instantly killed.' July 3: reformed on a slight crest, breastworks before noon, bombardment with minimal casualties. Casualty lists 'already forwarded' (no totals in-report - the return carries them).

clockProfile.assessment: One stated clock class (ordered to Sykes 'around 4-5 p.m.') against the division's committal ~18:00-18:15 (CA-J2A-8) allowing a ~45-60 min approach: raw +60 class. The southern-field early-report cluster (ED-58 candidate). Rule 4: never moves an anchor.

sources.title: Report of Col. H. Boyd McKeen, 81st Pennsylvania, commanding 1st Brigade, 1st Division, II Corps (OR 27/1 No. 78, pp. 381-383)
sources.author: H. Boyd McKeen
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 381-383). Dated Aug. 11, 1863, 'Action at Gettysburg, Pa., July 2 and 3'. Keys: advanced from the Sugar Loaf Hill area through the wheat-field, 'steadily drove the enemy back to the far end of the wheat-field, a distance of over 400 yards', prisoners taken; Cross's mortal wounding and command devolution; the 5th NH and 148th PA held 'until ammunition exhaustion'; relieved by a brigade of Barnes's division; retired to a stone wall 'in splendid order'; July 3 bombardment and the assault not on their front. CASUALTY STATEMENT: 59 k / 259 w / 12 m = 330 'total from 780 muskets' - the batch's cleanest strength+loss pairing; the official return prints the brigade block at the same 330 aggregate (composition drift 57/260/13 carried).

clockProfile.assessment: No usable stated clocks in the fetched text; sequence evidence throughout.

sources.title: Report of Col. Patrick Kelly, 88th New York, commanding 2d Brigade (Irish Brigade), 1st Division, II Corps (OR 27/1 No. 82, pp. 386-387)
sources.author: Patrick Kelly
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 386). Keys: moved 'by the left flank' about 5 p.m. 'preceded by the First Brigade'; advanced 'in line of battle through a wheat-field into a wood'; the enemy 'posted behind very large rocks' (the stony hill); drove them back capturing prisoners; engaged 'about three-quarters of an hour' (duration primary); withdrew when 'troops on our left had retired' under 'terrific fire' with near-capture risk; STRENGTH PRIMARY '530 men brought into action'; his casualty statement 202 (30/112/60) vs the return's 198 block - the pass-6 ED-52 exemplar. No regiment-grain action detail (negative).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 5 p.m.' for the move south vs the CA-J2A-8 committal 18:00-18:15 minus the short approach: raw ~+70. Union limb of the southern-field early class (ED-58 candidate); the brigade tablet's 'between 5 and 6 P.M.' is a partial correction - the exact Union mirror of the ED-55 report-vs-tablet structure. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Report of Lt. Col. John Fraser, 140th Pennsylvania, commanding 3d Brigade (Zook's), 1st Division, II Corps (OR 27/1, pp. 394-395)
sources.author: John Fraser
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 394). Keys: brigade line-order stated - 140th PA right, 66th NY center, 52nd NY left, 57th NY reserve (the batch's only stated brigade order); 'advanced steadily, driving the enemy before it, until it reached its most advanced position on the crest of a small hill' (the stony hill north crest); held 'until it became necessary to retreat, the enemy having brought forward fresh troops, who succeeded in flanking us on the right and left' (the wave-4 Wofford axis from the receiving side); 'the brigade lost its highly esteemed general' (Zook mw). No strength or casualty figures in the fetched text (the return block 358 carries EC6); Col. Roberts (140th PA) k. Page also carries the 140th PA regimental report (Roberts/Fraser).

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks in the fetched text.

sources.title: Report of Col. John R. Brooke, 53d Pennsylvania, commanding 4th Brigade, 1st Division, II Corps (OR 27/1, pp. 400-402)
sources.author: John R. Brooke
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 400-401). Dated Aug. 15, 1863. Keys: committed through Cross's spent line; the sweep to the Rose-ravine crest; 'I now found my flanks threatened by a strong force of the enemy, and immediately sent an officer to the general commanding the division for assistance, and finding also a part of the Third Brigade close [by]'; 'finding that unless I retired all would be killed or captured, I reluctantly gave the order to retire' - wounded brought off in order; himself 'severely bruised'; officer losses left 'companies without officers and first sergeants'; Lt. Col. Merwin (27th CT) k 'a national loss'; July 3 rifle pits on Caldwell's order, the assault not on their front; July 4-11 march itinerary. No strength/casualty totals in-report ('revised statement, p. 175' footnote - the return block 389).

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks; sequence evidence.

sources.title: Report of Col. P. Regis de Trobriand, 55th New York, commanding 3d Brigade, 1st Division, III Corps (OR 27/1, pp. 519-521)
sources.author: P. Regis de Trobriand
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 519-521). July 2 keys: deployment at 'the apex of the angle' - 3rd MI forward toward the Peach Orchard, 17th ME 'behind a stone wall' at the Wheatfield's south, 40th NY detached left to Ward, 5th MI + 110th PA on the ravine line; 'large masses of troops' rushing from woodland cover repulsed; the 5th MI 'sustained the loss of more than one-half of its number without yielding a foot of ground'; THE WITHDRAWAL ACCUSATION - supporting V Corps regiments 'had fallen back without engaging the enemy' (the ED-59 candidate conflict vs or-27-1-tilton); 'our ammunition was just exhausted' when Second Corps reinforcements relieved (the wave-2/3 seam trigger); retired to an adjoining wood by Birney's order. July 3: second line supporting batteries, casualties under artillery without engagement; evening advance-line command. CASUALTY STATEMENT 81/391/18 = 490 - the return block prints 490 EXACT (75/394/21, p. 178; composition drift carried). Officer pins: Pierce (3rd MI) w-amputation, Jones (110th PA) w, Pulford (5th MI) w. One stated clock survives only as a garbled fetch ('About @ p.m.') - unusable, re-read flagged.

clockProfile.assessment: The one stated clock came through the fetch channel garbled; carried unusable pending a clean re-read.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. J. H. Hobart Ward, commanding 2d Brigade, 1st Division, III Corps (OR 27/1, pp. 493-495)
sources.author: J. H. Hobart Ward
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 493-494). Keys: arrived July 1 'about dark by a forced march'; July 2 line 'at right angles with the Emmitsburg road, the left resting on a rocky eminence near Round Top' (Houck's Ridge); fire discipline - 'My command did not fire a shot until the enemy came within [200 yards], when the whole command fired a volley'; the oscillating defense 'for the space of one and a half hours did we advance and retire, both parties endeavoring to gain possession of the stone wall' (two-sided duration agreement with the 48th AL's hour-and-a-half); 'at least 10,000 of the enemy, in line and en masse' for 'nearly two hours' (impression, never strength data); STRENGTH ~1,500 effectives with 'nearly 800' lost and 8 of 14 field officers; Col. Wheeler (20th IN) k 'shot through the head'; Col. Van Horne Ellis (124th NY) k 'shot through the head' in the charge; Col. Walker (4th ME) w severely, stayed. Return block 781 adopted (ED-52). No stated clocks.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks in the fetched text; the brigade TABLET's 'forced back between 4 and 5 P.M.' is ~60-90 early vs CA-J2A-5 - recorded on the ED-58 candidate discussion, not on this report.

sources.title: Report of Capt. James E. Smith, 4th New York Independent Battery (OR 27/1 No. 184, pp. 588-589)
sources.author: James E. Smith
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 588-589). Keys: 'two sections of my battery on a hill (near the Devil's Cave) on the left of General Birney's line', one section 150 yards to the rear; enemy artillery opened 'around 2:30 p.m.' (stated - the +90 early class), twenty-minute duel; munition ladder 'case shot upon the advancing column until it entered the woods, when I fired shell', then canister; THE GUNS: three 10-pdr Parrotts + carriages abandoned on the hill (kept firing rather than signal retreat; terrain made removal difficult; believed supports would retake them); the rear section 'opened ... firing obliquely through the gully, doing good execution'; ROUNDS EXPENDED 140; casualties 2 k (Cpl. Thompson, Pvt. Isaiah Smith named) + 10 w + 11 horses (return prints 13 with one pooled missing); credits NO capturing unit (the three-claimant conflict's Union base); his belief the XII Corps 'retrieved' the guns is contradicted by Benning's holding record - carried as the loser's honest misinformation.

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 2:30 p.m.' for the artillery opening vs CA-J2A-2's ~15:45-16:20 (ED-53 two-pole): raw +75..+110. The III Corps limb of the southern-field early class (ED-58 candidate) - the skew is field-wide, not a CSA peculiarity. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Report of Col. William S. Tilton, 22d Massachusetts, commanding 1st Brigade, 1st Division, V Corps (OR 27/1 No. 192, pp. 607-608)
sources.author: William S. Tilton
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 607-608). Keys: 'at 4:30 p.m.' (stated) posted 'in order of battle in a piece of woods at the south of Mr. Roses house', Sweitzer on the left, right wing of the 118th PA refused for want of support; 'twice repulsed the assailants'; personal reconnaissance found 'the enemy in large force coming from the direction of Roses house, with the evident design of outflanking me' (the Barksdale-axis cause - the ED-59 candidate's core); withdrew to a two-line position, then across the open field to Sykes's 2nd Division ground after a battery retired; July 3 'relieved Third Brigade on Little Round Top' (interlocks with ED-54); July 4 skirmish reconnaissance. CASUALTY STATEMENT 12/80/17 = 109 vs the return's 125 block (ED-52: return adopted); strength context 'only 474 men' post-fight vs Barnes's 654 into action (two-scope, flagged).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 4:30 p.m. positioning against Kershaw's ~17:30 step-off + ~15-30 min to contact (CA-J2A-7, downstream-confirmed by ED-53): raw ~+75. V Corps limb of the field-wide early class beside Rice +45 / Sykes +15 (pass-5 profiles). Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Report of Col. Jacob B. Sweitzer, 62d Pennsylvania, commanding 2d Brigade, 1st Division, V Corps (OR 27/1, pp. 610-612)
sources.author: Jacob B. Sweitzer
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 610-612). Keys: first position 'in wood fronting an open field' (stony hill, Tilton's left), 9th MA detached on picket; the first fight (62nd PA + 4th MI changed front left; Maj. Lowry k); Col. Prescott's conditional-order exchange - 'I don't want to retire; I am not ready to retire; I can hold this place' (evidence the first withdrawal was ordered/conditional, not panic - ED-59 candidate input); reformed north of the Wheatfield Road; THE SECOND ADVANCE - a general officer's request confirmed by Caldwell in person, advanced across the wheat to the stone fence; the crisis - color-bearer Ed. Martin's warning, 'considerable firing diagonally toward our rear' first mistaken for friendly fire, 4th MI and 62nd PA changed front to rear, Lt. Seitz's ride (horse killed; Barnes unlocatable); the fighting extraction 'halting and firing as it retired'; Col. Jeffords (4th MI) mw defending the colors at the fence; 9th MA rejoined after. No strength/casualty totals in-report (return block 427: 4th MI 165, 62nd PA 175 - the capture-pocket shape).

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks in the report body (the brigade tablet's 'after 2 P.M.' move is the tablet-class early reading, recorded on the ED-58 discussion).

sources.title: Report of Capt. Henry C. Coates, 1st Minnesota Infantry (OR 27/1, pp. 424-425)
sources.author: Henry C. Coates
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 424-425). Dated Aug. 3, 1863, near Ellis' Ford. July 2 keys: in position ~3 a.m. near the center-left; 'About 5 p. m., when we were moved to support Battery C, Fourth U. S. Artillery' (the support anchor; stated clock early class); companies F and L detached as skirmishers/sharpshooters (named EC2 subtraction - the charge body is eight companies); THE CHARGE 'at double-quick down the slope of the hill right upon the rebel line' (Wilcox's; the receiving side is or-27-2-wilcox p. 618's third-line-at-the-double-quick); 'more than two-thirds of our men and officers' k+w; Colvill, Lt. Col. Adams, Maj. Downie w severely; Capt. Muller k, Lt. Farrar k, Capt. Periam mw. July 3: the ~3 p.m. assault, Capt. Messick k early, Capt. Farrell mw; the 28th Virginia colors taken (Pvt. Marshall Sherman, MoH) and ~500 prisoners claimed on the front. No numeric totals in the fetched text (the return row 50/174/0 = 224 is the ED-52 basis; the monument bronze's 262/215/47 + '232 out of 330' ledger carried as the veterans' count).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 5 p.m.' for the battery-support move; the charge itself sits at CA-J2A-10 (19:00-19:15, sunset-adjacent). If the stated clock keys the move (plausibly ~17:30-18:00 real), raw +30..+60; envelope widened for the single weak statement. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Report of Col. W. W. White, 7th Georgia, commanding G. T. Anderson's Brigade, Hood's Division (OR 27/2, pp. 396-398)
sources.author: W. W. White
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 396-398; part-ii route). A CONSOLIDATION 'from consolidated regimental commander reports' (his own note - one hop above the regimental layer). July 2 keys: the brigade on 'the extreme left of Hood's division'; reached the action 'by a march of several miles under a broiling sun'; 7th GA detached south to watch Union cavalry; THREE ADVANCES - first took the stone fence in the woods south of the Wheatfield, outflanked left, retired to Rose Hill crest; SECOND - Gen. G. T. Anderson WOUNDED, 'temporary confusion before the brigade regrouped'; THIRD - 'half an hour in the ravine' then the opposing line routed (wave-4 duration input). July 3: 7th/8th/9th GA detached per Law; 11th + 59th under Maj. McDaniel drove dismounted cavalry; the 9th repulsed cavalry trying to spike artillery (the Farnsworth front). Casualty composition read at the ~105 k / ~515 w / ~51 m class = 671 total, agreeing with the return block and the tablet's 671 exact. Closes 'W. W. WHITE, Colonel, Commanding Brigade'; the next report is G. T. Anderson's own FUNKSTOWN (July 10) report - Anderson filed nothing for Gettysburg itself (negative, verified).

clockProfile.assessment: No usable stated clocks in the fetched text.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Henry L. Benning, commanding brigade, Hood's Division (OR 27/2, pp. 414-417)
sources.author: Henry L. Benning
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 414-416). Dated Aug. 3, 1863. July 2 keys: 'About 2 or 3 p. m. ... informed by Major-General Hood that his division, as the right of Lieutenant-General Longstreet's corps, was about to attack the left of the enemy's line' with orders to 'follow Law's brigade at the distance of about 400 yards'; the objective read - 'an oblong mountain peak, or spur, presenting to us a steep face, much roughened by rocks' at 600-800 yards, with the enemy guns enumerated by shelf (three on the peak = Smith's forward guns, five on the summit = the Hazlett class, two in the gorge = the rear-section class); THE WRONG-BRIGADE FOLLOW - 'The part of it in our front I took to be Law's brigade, and so I followed it. In truth, it was Robertson's, Law's being farther to the right'; found the 1st TX engaged at the base, ascended 'the rugged steep' through rock faces 'insurmountable' in places; CAPTURED 'three front guns ... 10-pounder Parrotts' while 'the enemy managed to withdraw three rear guns' (count and type agree with or-27-1-smith-4ny across the lines); prisoners; Cols. John A. Jones (20th GA) and William T. Harris (2nd GA) k; loss 'not less than 400 killed, wounded, and missing' (floor by wording; return 497, tablet 509 - all three carried). July 3: held the Den under 'relentless' fire; the Union double breastworks observed overnight; 'the troops of General McLaws had for some hours been withdrawn from my left, leaving my flank entirely exposed' (the wing-extraction cross-bearing); Du Bose's 15th GA 'repulsed repeated attacks ... taking over 100 prisoners'; the vague Law crest order; the 15th GA's mistaken-orders withdrawal disaster (tablet concurs).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 2 or 3 p.m.' for Hood's briefing, which preceded the ~16:30 step-off (CA-J2A-3, ED-53) by less than an hour on the deployment evidence: raw +60..+90 against a ~15:30-16:00 real briefing. The CSA limb of the field-wide early class beside the McLaws exemplar (ED-55). Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Report of Maj. Gen. David B. Birney, First Division, Third Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 482-486)
sources.author: David B. Birney
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, pp. 482-485 read). Dated August 7, 1863. July 2 keys: relieved Geary 7 a.m., line 'resting its left on the Sugar Loaf Mountain'; the noon Berdan/3rd ME Pitzer Woods reconnaissance ('about 60, killed and wounded'); 'I now opened [say at 3.30 p.m.] with Clark's and Smith's batteries'; 'At 4 o'clock the enemy returned the artillery fire'; battery frame (Smith at the gorge/Sugar Loaf, Winslow right of Ward, Randolph/Seeley/Turnbull near the Emmitsburg road); Graham at the Peach Orchard with his right on the Emmitsburg road; THE SICKLES PIN: 'At 6 o'clock I found Major-General Sickles seriously wounded, and, at his request, took command of the troops'; Graham's brigade 'held the peach orchard until nearly dusk, when, finding the right unsupported, it fell back to the next ridge'; 'Major-General Hancock reached me about 7.30 o'clock'; GRAHAM PIN: 'wounded and fell into the hands of the enemy, with Lieutenant-Colonel Cavada'; the 141st PA 'lost, out of 200 taken into the fight, 149' (EXACT match to the p. 177 return row); 'hurled on my small division for three hours'; Ellis and Wheeler k 'with their crowns to the foe'; July 3 division under Ward in reserve; casualties '+Embodied in revised statement, p. 177'.

clockProfile.assessment: Three stated clocks: his batteries open 'say at 3.30 p.m.' and the enemy 'returned' at 4:00 (vs CA-J2A-2's ~15:45-16:20 two-pole: near-agreeing to ~+30 early), and the Sickles wounding at '6 o'clock' vs the CA-J2A-9 climax ~18:15-18:30 (+15..+30 early). MILD early skew - notably shallower than the southern-field brigade class (ED-58), consistent with a division/corps-command clock. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Col. Andrew H. Tippin, 68th Pennsylvania Infantry (commanding regiment; senior at the Peach Orchard after Graham fell) (OR 27/1 pp. 498-499)
sources.author: Andrew H. Tippin
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 498-499 read). Keys: brigade 'deployed in line of battle by battalions, doubled on the center, my regiment being on the left', in rear of Clark's battery; 'ordered to lie down. We remained in this position nearly two hours, suffering severely from the destructive fire' (the pre-assault artillery endurance window, EC5); 'ordered to move my regiment forward into a peach orchard, and fronting a road running parallel with the enemy's front'; THE GRAHAM PIN (attacking-side of the brigade seam): 'Here I met General Graham, who ordered me at once to engage the enemy ... the gallant general was severely wounded'; officer-casualty list (McLearn k, Black and Reynolds k, Lt.-Col. Reynolds, Maj. Winslow w et al.). NO stated clock anywhere in the read - the brigade's clock rides Birney and the tablet.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clock in the report (durations only: 'nearly two hours' under fire). Sequence-rich, clock-poor - tier D/B material.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Andrew A. Humphreys, Second Division, Third Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 530-534)
sources.author: Andrew A. Humphreys
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 530-534 read). July 2 keys: bivouacked 1 a.m.; 9 a.m. Third Brigade + Smith's battery rejoined; 'shortly after midday, I was ordered to form my division in line of battle, my left joining the right of the First Division', right opposite Caldwell's left; the ~16:00 advance: moved forward so 'the first line ran along the Emmitsburg road a short distance behind the crest upon which that road lies' (Carr's first line, Brewster 'in line of battalions in mass 200 yards in rear', Burling 200 more); Seeley's K/4th US at the log house then moved left, 'soon silenced the battery in our front'; Turnbull's F&K/3rd US into the vacated position; his front 'merely made demonstrations' while the left was engaged; Sewell's report of two attacking lines forming; 'Major Burns' Fourth Excelsior to General Graham's brigade'; Christiancy sent to Hancock for support; after Sickles 'dangerously wounded and carried from the field', Birney's order to 'throw back my left, and form a line oblique to and in rear of the one I then held' - executed 'under a heavy fire of artillery and infantry'; THE FIGHTING WITHDRAWAL: withdrew to the ridge crest 'slowly, continuing the contest with the enemy, whose fire of artillery and infantry was destructive in the extreme'; 'formed on the left of General Hancock's troops' and joined the counterattack that 'drove [the enemy] from the field'; battery losses itemized (Seeley 2k/20w, Turnbull 9k/14w); STRENGTH/LOSS PRIMARY: into action ~5,000, loss 2,088 (171 officers + 1,917 men; missing 3 off + 263 men 'the greater part of whom were probably wounded; some were killed' - his own missing-scope caveat); Burling's detachment loss 430 k+w stated at division grain.

clockProfile.assessment: One stated afternoon clock: the advance to the Emmitsburg road 'about 4 p.m.' - the movement that put the division on the road as/just before Longstreet's artillery opened (CA-J2A-2 ~15:45-16:20 two-pole; CA-J2A-1's salient complete ~15:30). Reads near-agreeing to slightly early - the division-grain clocks on this front do NOT share the southern-field brigade skew (recorded as an ED-58 counter-class finding beside Carr's 4.08). Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Joseph B. Carr, First Brigade, Second Division, Third Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 542-544)
sources.author: Joseph B. Carr
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 542-544 read). July 2 keys: arrived '1.30 a.m. on Thursday, July 2'; ordered to the front 12.30 p.m., formed on the 'prolongation of a line composed of the First Division'; THE MINUTE-PRECISION CLOCK: 'at 4.08 p.m.' advanced the line 300 yards (to the Emmitsburg road - the tablet's 'between 3 and 4 P.M.' is its rounding); the flanking attack and the withdrawal under Birney's order; the counterattack retaking the ground. July 3: 6 a.m. to the rear for ammunition; '3.22 p.m.' ordered to the center to support the Second Corps (the repulse hour); dusk return. Casualty basis: the p. 178 return row 790 (report defers to the revised statement).

clockProfile.assessment: Two minute-precision stated clocks: 4.08 p.m. for the 300-yard advance (against the CA-J2A-2 window and Humphreys's 'about 4 p.m.' - agreeing) and 3.22 p.m. July 3 for the move to the center (against the ~15:25 repulse climax CA-J3A-9 - agreeing). The Buford-dispatch-style precision suggests a kept headquarters clock; the cleanest brigade-grain agreeing profile on the July 2 field, and the strongest counter-exemplar to the ED-58 early class. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Col. William R. Brewster, Second (Excelsior) Brigade, Second Division, Third Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 558-560)
sources.author: William R. Brewster
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 558-559 read). Dated August 15, 1863. July 2 keys: 'At about 1 o'clock ... move the brigade to the front, and form line of battle in rear of the First Brigade'; 73rd NY advanced to the hill crest 250 yards ahead; 72nd/71st NY to left/right of the First Brigade, 70th in reserve; 'At 4 p.m.' advanced - 'exposed to a most terrible fire from the enemy's artillery on our left, which was most destructive'; 'about 5.30 p.m.' the 4th Excelsior sent 'to the support of General Graham's brigade' (the Humphreys/Burns order, both ends now primary); the collapse: 'the enemy advanced upon us in great force, pouring into us a most terrific fire of artillery and musketry, both upon our front and left flank'; withdrew 'in good order, but with terrible loss'; THE COLORS EVENT: Brewster with 'about 150 men, from the Second, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and One hundred and twentieth Regiments' recharged, 'recapturing them, taking from one the colors of the Eighth Florida Regiment' - Sgt. Thomas Hogan (3rd Excelsior) the captor (BOTH-SIDED with Lang's report and the Perry advanced tablet's 'the color bearer of the 8th Florida fell and its flag was lost'). July 3: battery support under severe artillery fire. CASUALTY STATEMENT 778 (12+118 k / 50+523 w / 75 m) - EXACT triple with the tablet 778 and the p. 178 return row 778.

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 5.30 p.m.' for the 4th Excelsior's dispatch to Graham, an event tied to the Peach Orchard crisis onset (~18:00-18:15, CA-J2A-9): raw ~+45. His 4 p.m. advance clock agrees with Carr/Humphreys. A mixed profile - the crisis clock carries the early class, the routine clocks do not. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Col. George C. Burling, Third Brigade, Second Division, Third Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 570-571)
sources.author: George C. Burling
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 570-571 read). July 2 keys: 'At 1.30 a.m., July 2, I received orders from General Meade to immediately rejoin the corps' (from the Emmitsburg detachment); massed in column of regiments, then reserve behind the First and Second Brigades; enemy artillery 'on our left flank, at a distance of not more than 1,000 yards'; THE DISPERSAL LEDGER (EC4 as detachments): 2nd NH + 7th NJ to Graham at the Peach Orchard; 5th NJ to the picket/Seeley support; the strongest regiment (6th NJ) to Ward's front; 'The Eighth New Jersey Volunteers was taken from me without my knowledge'; left with the 115th PA '(about 140 soldiers)' and 'reported to General Humphreys for instructions, remaining with him for some time'; collected retiring colors; 'we lay on our arms until daylight of the 3d'. July 3: rear, then right 'to support batteries' under severe artillery fire. CASUALTY STATEMENT 513 - EXACT triple with the tablet 513 and the p. 178 return row 513; 2nd NH heaviest at 193; Lt. Henry R. Clark (ambulance officer) mw 'died on the field'.

clockProfile.assessment: One stated clock (the 1.30 a.m. Meade order - night march, no chain anchor); the afternoon record is triggers and detachments without clocks. Tier D material by construction.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Romeyn B. Ayres, Second Division, Fifth Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 634-635)
sources.author: Romeyn B. Ayres
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 634-635 read). Dated July 28, 1863, camp near Warrenton. July 2 keys: marched forward the night of July 1-2 on the Gettysburg-Hanover road, formed 'in rear of and facing that road'; Third Brigade (Weed) detached to 'a rocky hill (usually called Round Top hill) of great importance'; the First and Second Brigades (Day/Burbank) 'ordered to advance and carry a point some 250 yards in advance' - 'crossing the intermediate swampy ground rapidly, and forming on the left of a division of the Second Corps [Caldwell's]'; 'immediately the troops became actively engaged with the enemy in the woods'; THE WITHDRAWAL DECISION: on the collapse to his right 'I immediately ordered the two brigades to face about and move to the right and rear, and form on the general line of battle'; 'the fight was virtually over, the enemy having failed to break the general line of battle'. No stated clocks. Casualty basis: the p. 179 return rows (Day 382, Burbank 447).

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clock in the Gettysburg narrative; the division's timing rides the Wheatfield wave record (ED-57 W3-W4) and Burbank's stated 5 p.m.

sources.title: Report (forwarding letter) of Col. Hannibal Day, First Brigade, Second Division, Fifth Corps (OR 27/1 p. 636)
sources.author: Hannibal Day
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 636 read). Dated July 28, 1863. A FORWARDING COVER, not a narrative: 'In forwarding the report of regimental commanders ... although not called on myself for a report, I have the honor to say that among casualties not included in the reports of battalion commanders my acting assistant adjutant-general, Captain S. Van Rensselaer [was wounded]'. The brigade's narrative record lives in Ayres's division report and the regular regimentals (No. 205 Sheridan/3rd US - commander absent, Freedley w commanding in action, 4+8k/60w/3m; No. 206 Adams/4th US 'broke camp at 4 o'clock' July 2). Verified-negative class at the brigade echelon for EC5 narrative.

clockProfile.assessment: No narrative, no clock - a forwarding cover. The brigade clock rides Ayres/Burbank and the wave record.

sources.title: Report of Col. Sidney Burbank, Second Brigade, Second Division, Fifth Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 644-645)
sources.author: Sidney Burbank
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 644-645 read). Dated July 21, 1863. July 2 keys: arrived morning July 2, formed 'left resting on the right of the Twelfth Army Corps', advanced slowly through thick woods behind skirmishers, then withdrawn to a rear position; 'about 5 p.m., when, during a heavy cannonading, I received orders to advance, and the brigade took a route to the left' (the stated clock, +75..+100 early against the W3-W4 window); deployed 'left resting on a battery then in position and its right extending back into the woods'; THE ADVANCE: 'to the foot of the hill and out of the woods to the edge of a wide marsh', crossed 'at double-quick', ascended the opposite crest to 'a fence and stone wall on the edge of the woods'; THE FLANK CRISIS: 'the enemy was seen ... moving through a wheat-field to our rear'; ordered to retire - the withdrawal exposed the line 'to a heavy fire on both flanks', losses 'very severe'; STRENGTH/OFFICER PRIMARY: went in 'with less than 900 muskets'; of 80 officers, 40 casualties ('7 killed and 33 wounded, 1 mortally ... since dead'); July 3 held in readiness, 'was not engaged again'. Return row 447 (p. 179) = tablet 447 EXACT.

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 5 p.m.' for the advance order that put the regulars into the Wheatfield ground during waves 3-4 (~18:15-18:40 by the ED-57 record, on CA-J2A-8's confirmed start): raw ~+80. The V Corps regulars join the field-wide ED-58 early class beside Tilton +75 and Kershaw +90. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Samuel W. Crawford, Third Division (Pennsylvania Reserves), Fifth Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 652-656)
sources.author: Samuel W. Crawford
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 653-655 read). July 2 keys: position 'rocky ridge, wooded at the top' fronting the marshy Plum Run ground with 'a low stone wall' beyond; Fisher's Third Brigade detached leftward; THE CHARGE: 'Uncovering our front, I ordered an immediate advance. The command advanced gallantly with loud cheers. Two well-directed volleys were delivered upon the advancing masses of the enemy, when the whole column charged at a run down the slope, driving the enemy back across the space beyond' and 'across the stone wall, for the possession of which there was a short but determined struggle'; 'I directed Colonel McCandless to hold the line of the stone wall and the woods on the right'; Fisher + 20th ME to Big Round Top. July 3: 'At 5 o'clock on the 3d, I received orders from General Sykes ... to advance' - the sweep that hit 'one brigade of the enemy, commanded by brigadier-General George T. Anderson' [tablet/report class attributes the collision to Benning's 15th GA - the McCandless dossier carries the misattribution note], 'completely routing them, taking over 200 prisoners, one stand of colors belonging to the Fifteenth Georgia'; recovered 'one 12-pounder Napoleon gun', three caissons, 'upward of 7,000 stand of arms', and 'all of the ground lost the previous day'; Col. Charles Fred. Taylor (Bucktails) k. DIVISION LOSS STATED: '20 officers and 190 men, 3 only missing' (= the return's McCandless 155 + Fisher 55).

clockProfile.assessment: The July 2 charge is unclocked in the read pages (trigger: the retiring V Corps units cleared his front - the ED-57 W5 seam); the one stated clock is July 3's 5 a.m. Sykes order, which no chain anchor yet covers. Rule 4 would apply regardless.

sources.title: Report of Col. William McCandless, First Brigade, Third Division, Fifth Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 657-658)
sources.author: William McCandless
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 657 read). No. 219, dated July 9, 1863. July 2 keys: 'the command arrived on the field about 1 p.m. of the 2nd instant, and at 5 p.m. was assigned a position near the left' (stated clock, early class); 'Our first position was naturally strong, being a rocky, wooded hillside, with good cover, sloping steeply down to a plain, which extended from the base about 700 yards to a stone wall' (the charge geometry primary - 700 yards to the wall); THE CHARGE: 'the brigade advanced in gallant style, the first line delivering one volley; then the whole brigade charged at a full run down the hillside and across the plain, driving the advancing masses of the enemy back upon the stone wall'; Col. Charles F. Taylor k leading the Bucktails. July 3: the sweep - 'capturing nearly 200 prisoners (among whom was a lieutenant-colonel), also a stand of colors' [15th GA]. Return row 155 (p. 180) = tablet 155 EXACT.

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'at 5 p.m. was assigned a position near the left' for a posting that preceded the W5 charge (~19:15-19:30, bracketed by CA-J2A-8 downstream and the CA-J2A-11 sunset pin); the assignment itself is plausibly ~18:00-18:30 canonical: raw ~+60..+90. Joins the ED-58 field-wide class. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Col. David J. Nevin, Third Brigade, Third Division, Sixth Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 684-685)
sources.author: David J. Nevin
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 684-685 read). No. 234, dated July 5, 1863 (a FIVE-DAY-LATENCY report - the freshest brigade report on this field). Keys: the forced march primary - left Manchester 9 p.m. July 1, 'passed through Littlestown, Pa.' 8 a.m., halted 2 p.m. 'within 2 miles of Cemetery Hill, having marched nearly 34 miles within seventeen hours'; 'At 4.30 p.m. we were hastily marched forward and to the left of Rocky Hill {Round Top} the extreme left of our line, to support the lines of the Second and Fifth Corps'; THE W5 RECEIVING STATE: 'had barely gotten into position when all the troops in front, excepting two regiments of the Pennsylvania Reserves, were driven back and up the hill, retreating irregularly through and past our line'; three regiments advanced supported by the 98th PA, 'delivered two volleys into the ranks of the advancing rebels' then 'charged their column, breaking the same and driving them in disorder down the hill, recovering in the charge two light 12-pounder brass pieces' (the gun-recovery cross-credit - see the dossier); crossed 'a narrow swamp', halted 100 yards beyond, 'left resting at the foot of a small hill'. July 3: 10 a.m. reconnaissance repulsed; 2 p.m. shelled; 6 p.m. supported Crawford's advance, 'recapturing artillery'. July 4: 9 a.m. Sykes reconnaissance support.

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'At 4.30 p.m. hastily marched forward' for the move onto the LRT north slope that ended 'barely gotten into position' when the W4 collapse swept past - canonical ~18:45-19:15 against the CA-J2A-11-pinned W5 charge: raw ~+75 (up to +120 at the envelope edge). VI Corps joins the ED-58 field-wide class - the third corps on the Union side to carry it. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Lieut. Richard S. Milton, Ninth Massachusetts Battery (OR 27/1 pp. 886-887, No. 320)
sources.author: Richard S. Milton
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, pp. 886-887 read). Dated July 17, Camp near Berlin, Md. THE 9th MA/BIGELOW SPINE (Bigelow wounded; the battery's only OR report is Milton's): July 2 - arrived 10 a.m., park left of the Taneytown road; 'Remained in park until 4 p. m., when ordered by Lieutenant-Colonel McGilvery to move to the left, to the support of General Sickles' corps'; 'After an hour's slow firing, the batteries fell back, and the enemy advanced in two lines of battle directly across our front, the Third Corps having fallen back, leaving us without support'; 'prolonges were fixed, and firing at skirmishers on our left and the approaching line of battle on our right, we retired 300 yards, until we reached a position near which two stone walls met at an obtuse angle' (THE TROSTLE ANGLE, primary); 'This position, without infantry support and under an enfilading artillery fire, we were ordered to hold at all hazards. The rebel line of battle could not be checked, although its center was badly broken by our canister fire. Its flanks closing in on either side of us, obtained a cross-fire, which silenced the four pieces on my right, and prevented their withdrawal from loss of officers, men, and horses'; 'Having succeeded in retiring my section, I found myself in command of the battery'; withdrew 1.5 miles, 'it being then 9 p. m. Our four guns were recovered the same day' (THE RECOVERY, agreeing with McGilvery's 8 p.m. detail); itemized loss: k 1 officer + 1 sergeant + 6 men, w 2 officers + 6 NCOs + 9 men, 2 prisoners, '45 horses were killed and 15 wounded'. July 3: shelled out of park ~12 o'clock (1 w, 5 horses); with two pieces of K/3rd US under Baxter near Cemetery Hill; fired 15 rounds; under Whitworth fire.
