Wounded-commander note: Bigelow w at the limbers during the Trostle stand (McGilvery/return context).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 4 p.m. for the move to Sickles vs McGilvery's stated 3.30 order (internal 30-min spread) and the canonical Peach Orchard collapse ~18:30-19:00 for the fights his 'After an hour's slow firing' precedes: the July 2 sequence reads ~+45 early at the move (ED-58 class, the Artillery Reserve limb), but his 9 p.m. park arrival is consistent with the post-sunset withdrawal. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Capt. George B. Winslow, Battery D, First New York Light Artillery (OR 27/1 pp. 586-588, No. 183)
sources.author: George B. Winslow
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, pp. 586-588 read). THE WHEATFIELD BATTERY's own record: July 1 left with a 1st Division brigade at Emmitsburg; marched 3 a.m. July 2, rejoined ~11 a.m.; position 'in a small wheat-field near the base of Round Top hill'; the CSA battery opened 'between 3 and 4 p. m.'; THE BLIND-FIRE DISCIPLINE VERBATIM: 'unable from my obscure position to observe the movements of the troops, and was compelled to estimate distances and regulate my fire by the reports of our own and the enemy's musketry' - by Birney's direction solid shot over friendly troops, 'lessening the range as our troops fell back'; shell/case at 'about one degree elevation, and from 1 to 1 1/2 second fuse' kept the enemy at the woods' edge (~400 yards); the reconnaissance discovering the perpendicular line ('I rode through the woods on my left... found our line there formed perpendicular to my own... facing from me and closely pressed'); the withdrawal 'by piece in succession from the left, continuing to fire until the right and last piece was limbered' with the enemy 'within 25 yards of my left'; impressed '2 passing horses of Captain Smith's'; met Sickles and Randolph immediately after leaving the field; 'obliged to leave one caisson and one caisson body on the field for the want of horses... but subsequently recovered them' (a small self-recovery record on the same ground-class as ED-61's); loss '1 man killed, 16 men wounded, and 3 missing, 2 of whom are known to be prisoners... 17 horses killed' (CONFLICT vs the return's 10 w + 8 m = 18; both carried); July 3 not engaged.

clockProfile.assessment: Sparse clocks: 'between 3 and 4 p. m.' for the CSA artillery opening on his front (canonical opening of the southern-field bombardment ~15:45-16:15) reads ~+30 early at midpoint; his withdrawal is unclocked (sequence-tied to the Wheatfield collapse, W2->W4 seam). ED-58 class, weakly attested. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Adelbert Ames, commanding 2d Brigade and 1st Division, XI Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 712-713, No. 244)
sources.author: Adelbert Ames
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, pp. 712-713 read). A THIN division-level record (one paragraph on the evening): July 1 - brigade pushed through town toward the Harrisburg pike, 'From this position we were driven, the men of the First Brigade... running through lines of the regiments of my brigade... creating considerable confusion. At this time General Barlow was wounded, and the command of the division devolved upon me'; the retreat through town ('many of our men were taken prisoners'); 'The hill in rear of the town was occupied... and in this position the division remained during the two following days'; THE EVENING, whole: 'On the evening of the 2d, an attempt was made to carry the position we held, but the enemy was repulsed with loss. Colonel Carroll, with a brigade from the Second Corps, rendered timely assistance. The batteries behaved admirably'; Harris named to brigade command 'soon after I assumed command of the division'. No clocks anywhere - the division command record leans on its brigades' and batteries' reports.

clockProfile.assessment: No stated clocks in the report (verified); the evening assault is narrated in two sentences. Reason recorded per the kind-none convention.

sources.title: Reports of Col. Andrew L. Harris, 75th Ohio, commanding regiment and 2d Brigade, 1st Division, XI Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 715-716, No. 246)
sources.author: Andrew L. Harris
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-i route, pp. 715-716 read; two reports - regiment then brigade). 75th OH report: 'Marched from Emmitsburg with about 160 officers and men' (100 + 3 officers detached scouting); after July 1 'rallied again the few men left... the number increased to 91 officers and men' (THE July-1-wreckage strength primary); July 2 'about 4 p. m... the Seventy-fifth was placed at the stone wall'; 'About dusk the enemy attacked the regiment in front and on the flank and rear at nearly the same time, having come through the space which had been vacated by the removal of the Seventeenth Connecticut... From this attack but few escaped, and those only in the darkness and smoke; the greater portion were no doubt made prisoners.' BRIGADE report: assumed command late afternoon July 1; the line at the stone wall nearly at right angles with the Baltimore road; all-day skirmishing July 2 from 'the fences and a brick-kiln on the right, and from the houses on the left'; THE GAP CONFESSION: 'In moving the Seventeenth Connecticut Volunteers to the extreme right and front of my line, their place at the wall was left vacant, thus endangering the left flank of the Seventy-fifth Ohio... and the right flank of the Twenty-fifth Ohio. Before I could make any arrangements to remedy this breach in the line, the attack of the enemy on Cemetery Hill was made, and I was forced back by superiority of numbers, with heavy loss'; re-formed 'at the stone wall in the rear of and parallel with the one occupied the previous day'; July 4 the brigade first into the town.

clockProfile.assessment: 'About dusk' for the assault's arrival (sunset 19:29 LMT, ED-31) sits on CA-J2E-3's adopted 19:45-20:00 - agreeing within the dusk window; his one hard clock ('about 4 p. m.' for the line change prep) is unanchored. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Lieut. Col. Detleo von Einsiedel, 41st New York (von Gilsa's brigade), OR 27/1 pp. 713-714 (No. 245)
sources.author: Detleo von Einsiedel
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 713-714 read). The von-Gilsa-brigade frame regimental (the brigade filed no brigade-echelon report through this fetch - the 41st NY report is its best OR record): strength primary '14 commissioned officers, 187 file, and 17 musicians'; arrived ~10 p.m. July 1 at the cemetery; July 2 positions (six companies at the 'square stone fence'); 2 p.m. moved by von Gilsa's order 'to the front of the two batteries... posted on a little hill, on the right of the Baltimore and Gettysburg road, near the cemetery' (the Wiedrich-ground support assignment, drawn-frame corroboration); the 1,000-steps-backward refused line; THE ASSAULT: 'An attack was made by the enemy at 6.30 p. m.' - right wing repulsed it, but 'On the left wing, the enemy came so far as to break through the line, which was kept either by the Sixty-eighth... or Fifty-fourth New York... and move on toward the batteries. In this critical moment, Captain Alfred Theinhardt... placed... some men of other regiments from the left wing behind a stone wall... succeeded in holding the enemy in check until a regiment of the Second Corps arrived, which was sent from the cemetery to support him' (the Carroll-relief seam at regiment grain).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'attack... at 6.30 p. m.' vs CA-J2E-3's adopted 19:45-20:00 = ~+75 early - THE FIRST EVENING-PHASE member of the ED-58 early-stated class (which pass 6 documented for the afternoon field); his 5 p.m. reposition clock carries the same skew against the sequence. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Capt. Michael Wiedrich, Battery I, 1st New York Light Artillery (OR 27/1 pp. 751-752, No. 265)
sources.author: Michael Wiedrich
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 751-752 read). Undated report. July 1: posted 'on a hill on the north side of the Baltimore turnpike, and near the cemetery and east of the city'; ~5 p.m. turned his pieces on the columns pursuing the XI Corps, 'received him with canister, which checked his progress, and gave our troops time to rally in rear of the battery'; July 2: morning-to-3 p.m. duels with two batteries planted directly in front; one piece dismounted by a shell; THE ASSAULT: 'About 8 p. m. the enemy charged on the battery with a brigade of infantry, which succeeded in turning our infantry, and got into the intrenchments of my battery. After they were repulsed by our forces, I opened on them again with canister with good effect'; July 3: forenoon duels, ~1 p.m. the general cannonade cross-fire ('without sustaining any loss whatever from those guns'), town sharpshooters 'molested us continually for two days'; 'During the three days' fighting we held the position first taken'; losses '3 men killed, 2 severely and 9 slightly wounded' (enumerates 14; the return's revised statement p. 183 gives 13 - one-man scope conflict flagged, ED-43 low-risk).

clockProfile.assessment: 'About 8 p. m.' for the charge on the battery sits exactly on CA-J2E-3 (19:45-20:00) beside Hays's 'a little before 8' and Ricketts's 'about 8' - the evening's rare three-source, both-armies clock agreement. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Capt. R. Bruce Ricketts, Batteries F and G, 1st Pennsylvania Light Artillery (OR 27/1 pp. 894-895, No. 328)
sources.author: R. Bruce Ricketts
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 894-895 read). July 2: moved with Huntington's brigade from Taneytown, on the field ~noon; 'At 4 p. m. I was ordered by Captain Huntington to report to Colonel C. S. Wainwright... who placed me in position on Cemetery Hill, to the right of the turnpike leading into Gettysburg' (the ECH east-front slot; Wainwright-frame corroboration); afternoon: engaged the batteries on the enemy's left and 'shelling a column of the enemy that charged into the woods on my right, which was occupied by the Twelfth Army Corps' (a RECEIVING-side glimpse of the Culp's Hill assault from ECH); THE HAND-TO-HAND VERBATIM: 'At about 8 p. m. a heavy column of the enemy charged on my battery, and succeeded in capturing and spiking my left piece. The cannoneers fought them hand to hand with handspikes, rammers, and pistols, and succeeded in checking them for a moment, when a part of the Second Army Corps charged in and drove them back. During the charge I expended every round of canister in the battery, and then fired case shot without the fuses. The enemy suffered severely'; July 3 counter-battery; loss 'Killed, 6; wounded, 14; missing, 3' (= the return's 23 EXACT), 20 horses; 1,200 rounds expended over the two days (the ordnance-return class); Brockway/Spence section pins.

clockProfile.assessment: 'At about 8 p. m.' for the charge = CA-J2E-3 agreeing (with Hays and Wiedrich); the II Corps counter-charge ('a part of the Second Army Corps charged in') is CA-J2E-4's receiving-side sequence pin (tier D - he gives it no clock). Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Reports of Maj. Gen. Henry W. Slocum, commanding XII Corps / right wing (OR 27/1 pp. 758-760, No. 271)
sources.author: Henry W. Slocum
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 758-759 read). The corps/right-wing command frame: July 1 arrival and the extreme-left night position; July 2 morning redisposition (2d Division to the right of center 'between Rock Creek and the crest of the hill held by... Wadsworth'; 1st Division across the creek on its right); the breastworks decision credited in terms: 'a strong force was detailed for the construction of breastworks and abatis, which subsequently proved of great value, as they enabled us at a critical moment to detach portions of the command to other points of the line'; the V Corps massing note and its move to the left 'About half an hour before the attack on our left'. The evening detachment order (CA-J2E-1) appears at corps grain; Greene's/Williams's/Geary's reports carry its clocks.

clockProfile.assessment: No evening-phase clock in the fetched pages (the corps report time-stamps little; its value is the order chain). Reason recorded per the kind-none convention.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Alpheus S. Williams, commanding 1st Division / XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 773-778)
sources.author: Alpheus S. Williams
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 773-775 read). THE ABSENT-THEN-RETURNING FRAME's best narrative (Williams commanded the corps July 1-3 while Slocum commanded the right wing; Ruger the 1st Division): July 1 command cascade verbatim; the Hanover-road probe; July 2 the detachment: 'to detach the First Division (Ruger's) and Lockwood's brigade to support the left wing of the army, then heavily attacked. Geary's division was at the same time ordered to cover and defend the intrenched line of the whole corps'; THE McGILVERY MEETING (an ED-61 ledger primary at command grain): 'Major McGilvery, of the Maine artillery... reported to me that his battery was without support, and threatened by the enemy's infantry in the woods in front, to which it had just retired, carrying several pieces of our guns. I ordered General Lockwood... to deploy his line and occupy the woods, which he did in gallant style, pushing a considerable distance to the front, and recapturing three pieces of artillery abandoned by the enemy in his retreat'; 'It was now quite dark. I therefore ordered both commands to halt' (the darkness pin on the left-wing round trip); the return order; THE WRONG-ROAD RECORD: Geary 'directed to follow with two brigades, leaving Greene's brigade... By some mistake' (the two brigades ended 'on the open fields between the breastworks and the Baltimore pike'); 'I did not learn this state of affairs until nearly 12 o'clock at night, when I reported them to Major-General Slocum, and received his orders to drive the enemy from our intrenchments at daylight'; the July 3 daylight plan (15-minute artillery preparation at 600-800 yards; the seven-hour combat; Lockwood + Shaler reinforcements).

clockProfile.assessment: Sparse but consistent: the detachment ties to the attack on the left (CA-J2E-1 window); 'quite dark' on the halt (physics-adjacent, ED-31: after ~19:59 civil twilight end); 'nearly 12 o'clock at night' for learning Geary's position. No skew measurable; unanchored nominal near zero. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. John W. Geary, 2d Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 825-834)
sources.author: John W. Geary
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 825-828 read). The division command record over Greene: July 1 Two Taverns 11 a.m., ~2 p.m. advance, night on the extreme left (Little Round Top ground) by Hancock's direction; July 2 line layout verbatim (Greene's 3d Bde on the left, 'The Second Brigade (Kane's) extended from the right of Greene's brigade at an angle of about 45 degrees', Candy in double column support; breastworks 'immediately thrown up... unusual facilities being afforded by the wood and rock'); THE LATIMER DUEL BOTH-SIDED: 'At 4 p. m. the enemy opened... Finding that a battery of the enemy posted on a hill across Rock Creek was enfilading the lines of the First and Eleventh Corps, I ordered a section of Knap's (Pennsylvania) battery and one of Battery K, Fifth U. S. Artillery, to take position on the eminence at the left of Greene's brigade and to silence the enemy's battery. This they did after a hot artillery duel of about thirty minutes' (vs Johnson's 4 p.m. Latimer order - cross-army clock agreement); 'at 7 p. m. I received orders to move the division by the right flank, and follow the First Division, leaving one brigade' (CA-J2E-1 at division grain, -30 vs Greene's 6.30); GREENE'S FIGHT AT DIVISION GRAIN: the 71st PA withdrawal 'by its colonel, who stated that he was ordered so to do by his general'; 'the noble brigade repelled four separate and furious charges... during an incessant attack of two and a half hours'; the same 355/400 reinforcement ledger as Greene's; 'The enemy... discontinued their attack at about 10 p. m.'; the 137th NY perpendicular line with 'its right near a stone wall... about 200 yards in rear' held by the enemy; Kane's 9 p.m. return order and the two volleys in the darkness that proved the works occupied.

clockProfile.assessment: His 4 p.m. Latimer-duel clock agrees with Johnson's across the lines; his 7 p.m. move order sits -30 against Greene's 6.30 (order-propagation lag as plausible as skew); 'about 10 p. m.' attack end agrees with Greene/Hays. Near-zero, division-grain. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Maj. Gen. Jubal A. Early, Early's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 465-478; Gettysburg pp. 469-471)
sources.author: Jubal A. Early
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com part-ii route, pp. 469-471 read). July 1 (p. 469): Gordon vs Barlow ('capturing... General Barlow himself'); Hays and Avery advanced on Gordon's left, 'driving back into the town in great confusion the second line'; THE JULY-1 TWO-NAPOLEON CONVERGENT CAPTURE IN THE PRIMARY: 'Two pieces of artillery (Napoleons) were also captured outside of the town, the capture being claimed by both brigades; but it is unnecessary to decide which reached these pieces first, as the capture was unquestionably due to the joint valor of both brigades' (the ED-56/ED-61 pattern ruled by the commander himself); the evening-of-July-1 Cemetery/Culp's Hill deliberation (Smith's York-road alarm; 'Johnson's division was coming up... no effort to get possession of the wooded hill... was made that night'); July 2 (p. 470): Hays moved under cover of night to the field; the 4 o'clock artillery opening; THE TRIGGER CHAIN VERBATIM: 'I was ordered by General Ewell to advance upon Cemetery Hill with my two brigades... as soon as General Johnson's division, which was on my left, should become engaged at the wooded hill... Accordingly, as soon as Johnson became warmly engaged, which was a little before dusk, I ordered Hays and Avery to advance and carry the works on the heights in front' (CA-J2E-2 -> CA-J2E-3 expressed as cause, primary); the ascent ('at least two lines of infantry posted behind stone and plank fences... they reached the crest of the hill, and entered the enemy's breastworks... getting possession of one or two batteries'); 'no attack was made on the immediate right, as was expected' - the unsupported withdrawal 'with comparatively slight loss'; 'Hays' brigade brought off four stand of captured colors'; Gordon halted in the field ('it was evident that the crest... could not be held'); p. 471: 'In this attack, Colonel Avery, of the Sixth North Carolina Regiment, commanding Hoke's brigade, was mortally wounded' (THE PIN); Godwin succession named; July 3 dispositions (Hays/Hoke to the town street before light; Smith's brigade to Johnson); division loss '158 killed, 796 wounded, and 227 missing' (vs the return's 1,188 - conflict carried). PASS-9 EXTENSION (2026-07-11, p. 471 re-read): the Smith-to-Johnson detachment primary, verbatim: 'After night, I was ordered by General Ewell to send Smith's brigade to report to General Johnson, on the left, by daylight, and General Smith was ordered to do so, and did report to General Johnson, and his three regiments were engaged on the 3rd on the extreme left, under General Johnson's directions'; details deferred to 'the report of Colonel [John S.] Hoffman, the present brigade commander, herewith forwarded' (Hoffman report not located this pass); also 'Before light on the morning of the 3d' Hays'/Hoke's brigades withdrawn to the town street line, Gordon left in their old position; brigades 'quietly withdrawn' at 2 a.m. July 4.

clockProfile.assessment: 'A little before dusk' for Johnson becoming warmly engaged (sunset 19:29 LMT) brackets CA-J2E-2's ~19:00 within the dusk window, and his 4 o'clock artillery clock agrees with Johnson/Geary across the lines. The trigger construction (advance WHEN Johnson engages) is tier-D structure better than any watch. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Report of Maj. Gen. Edward Johnson, Johnson's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 499-506; Gettysburg pp. 503-506)
sources.author: Edward Johnson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 503-506 read). Division roster verbatim with all regimental commanders (p. 503); the 25-mile July 1 march ('obstructions caused by wagons of Longstreet's corps'), night line NE of town along the York railroad (Nicholls right, Jones, Steuart, Walker left); JULY 2 (p. 504): skirmishing until '4 p. m., at which hour I ordered Major Latimer to open fire with all of his pieces from the only eligible hill within range, Jones' brigade being properly disposed as a support' (Benner's Hill; the Ewell-wing dossier's Latimer record now division-corroborated; Geary's 4 p.m. agrees opposite); 'The unequal contest was maintained for two hours'; Latimer's untenable report and the four-guns-left cover order; THE ASSAULT ORDER: 'In obedience to an order from the lieutenant-general commanding, I then advanced my infantry to the assault... Jones' brigade in advance, followed by Nicholls' and Steuart's'; THE WALKER DETENTION: 'General Walker was directed to follow, but reporting to me that the enemy were advancing upon him from their right, he was ordered to repulse them and follow on as soon as possible... General Walker did not arrive in time to participate in the assault that night' (the Stonewall-brigade evening negative, division-attested); THE DARKNESS PIN: 'By the time my other brigades had crossed Rock Creek and reached the base of the mountain, it was dark'; Steuart 'carried a line of breastworks which ran perpendicular to the enemy's main line, captured a number of prisoners and a stand of colors'; Jones and Higginbotham w -> Dungan; July 3: Stonewall up early, the Union dawn counter-effort 'repulsed with great slaughter', Daniel's + O'Neal's brigades, 'two other assaults were made; both failed'; withdrawal 10 p.m. July 3 to the hill NW of town; officer pins p. 505: Latimer mw ('the boy major'), Maj. B. W. Leigh chief of staff k 'within a short distance of the enemy's line' (= Greene's found-body record, both-sided), Maj. H. K. Douglas w&c; ADDENDA p. 506: the Betterton flag forwarding ('a United States flag captured in the enemy's breastworks... by Sergt. Thomas J. Betterton, Company A, Thirty-seventh Virginia, Steuart's brigade') and the October 7 divisional casualty table (Gettysburg: Stonewall 35/208/87 = 330; Jones 58/302/61 = 421; Steuart 83/409/190 = 682; Nicholls 43/309/36 = 388; stated division aggregate 219/1,229/375 = 1,823). PASS-9 EXTENSION (p. 504 re-read, the July 3 paragraph verbatim): 'Early next morning, the Stonewall Brigade was ordered to the support of the others, and the assault was renewed with great determination'; 'Shortly after, the enemy moved forward to recapture the line of breastworks which had been taken the night previous, but was repulsed with great slaughter'; 'Daniel's and Rodes' brigades [Colonel E. A. O'Neal commanding], of Rodes' division, having reported to me, two other assaults were made; both failed'; 'The enemy were too securely intrenched and in too great numbers to be dislodged by the force at my command' — time-silent throughout (the division report's known shape; the clocks live with Muhlenberg/Kane/O'Neal/Daniel).

clockProfile.assessment: His 4 p.m. Latimer clock agrees with Geary across the lines; the assault itself he times only by sequence (order after the two-hour duel) and darkness ('it was dark' at the Rock Creek base - after ~19:59 civil twilight end, ED-31), consistent with contact ~19:00 at Greene's front and the escalation to ~20:00. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Reports of Brig. Gen. George H. Steuart, Steuart's Brigade, Johnson's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 507-512; Gettysburg pp. 509-512)
sources.author: George H. Steuart
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 507-512 read; the Gettysburg report follows the Winchester one). July 2 (p. 509): pickets 'relieved at about 5 o'clock by four companies of skirmishers from the Twenty-third Virginia, and shortly afterward the brigade was formed in line of battle and moved forward'; THE APPROACH GEOMETRY VERBATIM: the objective hill 'lay in a southwesterly direction... our left wing was obliged to swing around by a right half-wheel'; the staggered Rock Creek crossing ('The right wing... crossed the creek considerably in advance of the center and left wing' - the wheel order misunderstood on the left + 'the greater number of natural obstacles'); the slope beginning '50 feet from the bank'; p. 510 THE WORKS TAKEN: the perpendicular breastwork 'not occupied in force' - 'The Twenty-third Virginia accordingly... immediately charged the work, and scattered the enemy'; the 23d's file-right into the flank/rear fire; 37th VA + 10th VA + 1st MD Bn occupying the works; the 3d NC's exposed stand with ammunition 'nearly exhausted' (replenished 'from those of the wounded and dead'); the 1st NC reserve led up by Lt. McKim; THE FRIENDLY-FIRE HESITATION: 'the impression at this time prevailed that we were firing upon our friends, and the fire been discontinued at intervals'; the 10th VA stone-wall probe and the repulsed bayonet charge from the woods; 'The whole command rested from about 11 p. m. till about daylight'; p. 511 JULY 3: the dawn artillery storm; ~10 a.m. the perpendicular charge ('Third North Carolina, First Maryland Battalion, Thirty-seventh Virginia, Twenty-third Virginia, and First North Carolina') toward the second breastworks - 'The enemy's position was impregnable... the whole line fell back, but in good order'; the hour behind the stone wall under 'a fire... more terrific than any experienced during the day'; the fall-back to the creek and the midnight withdrawal; officer pins: Herbert (1st MD Bn) 'dangerously wounded the evening of the 2d', Goldsborough 'severely wounded next morning', Crane succession; Wood/Warren/Walton/Brown/Parsley command list; Betterton (37th VA) named among the commended 'who took a stand of colors and was severely wounded' (p. 512, = Johnson's addenda, both-echelon).

clockProfile.assessment: Stated 'about 5 o'clock' picket relief with the advance 'shortly afterward' vs the adopted ~19:00 contact (and his own tablet's 6 P.M. crossing) = ~+75 early on the assault clocks - the CSA limb of the evening early-stated class (with Einsiedel's +75 opposite); but his 'about 11 p. m.' rest clock sits near zero - the skew is on the action clocks, noted in-profile. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Reports of Col. J. M. Williams, 2d Louisiana, commanding Nicholls' Brigade (OR 27/2 pp. 512-514; Gettysburg pp. 513-514, No. 483)
sources.author: Jesse M. Williams
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 512-513 read). THE CSA CLOCK THAT AGREES WITH GREENE: the brigade 'remained until 7 p. m. of July 2, when, after changing position to the left of Jones' brigade, it was ordered forward in line of battle toward the heights in front, about three-quarters of a mile'; 'engaged the enemy near the base of these heights, and, having quickly driven his front line into the intrenchments on their crest, continued forward until it reached a line about 100 yards from the enemy's works, when it again engaged him with an almost incessant fire for FOUR HOURS, pending which several attempts to carry the works by assault, being entirely unsupported on the right [Jones' brigade having failed to hold its line on the right], were attended with more loss than success' (the inter-brigade accusation - carried as a named conflict with Jones's own record); 'The command remained in this position during the night, and at early light opened on the enemy again... for four hours almost without cessation, and at intervals until 12 m.'; the noon withdrawal to the creek ravine and the 3 a.m. July 4 march to the rear of Gettysburg.

clockProfile.assessment: '7 p. m.' for the forward order = CA-J2E-2's ~19:00 exactly - the first CSA report clock AGREEING on the evening chain (opposite Greene's 'a few minutes before 7'); his four-hours duration runs the fight to ~23:00, inside CA-J2E-5's envelope edge. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Reports of Brig. Gen. John M. Jones, Jones's Brigade, Johnson's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 531-533, No. 495)
sources.author: John M. Jones
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 531-532 read). Regimental roster with commanders (21st/25th/42d/44th/48th/50th VA); July 1 night line 'in a ravine in an open field northeast of the town, and to the left and front of the enemy's artillery on Cemetery Hill'; July 2: Lilley's 25th-VA skirmishers out from daylight; 'about 4 p. m... it moved to the front to support Andrews' battalion of artillery [Major Latimer], which was moving into position on a hill opposite to Cemetery Hill. The brigade was halted under cover of a range of low hills, about 300 yards in rear and to the left of the battalion' (the Benner's Hill support anchor, agreeing with Johnson/Geary at 4 p.m.); the 50th VA at the guns' left; THE ASSAULT: Johnson 'directed me to form my brigade in line, to move forward when Nicholls' brigade had formed on my left, and to attack the enemy in his position on the opposite hill'; the advance 'down the slope... across the bottom {Rock Creek}, and up the hill occupied by the enemy. The hill was steep, heavily timbered, rocky, and difficult of ascent'; the left-wing confusion 'perhaps, unavoidable from THE LATENESS OF THE HOUR at which the advance was made, THE DARKNESS IN THE WOODS, and the nature of the hill' (physics-adjacent, ED-31); THE WOUNDING PIN (registers' July-3 note CORRECTED): 'When near the first line of intrenchments... I received a flesh wound through the thigh, the excessive hemorrhage from which rendered it necessary for me to be borne from the field, and the command of the brigade devolved upon Lieutenant-Colonel Dungan, Colonel J. C. Higginbotham having been previously wounded' - JULY 2 EVENING, in the first assault; further pins: Cobb (44th) w -> Buckner; Lt. F. Pendleton Jones aide 'badly wounded, and since dead'; the report's closing note on its own latency ('My absence from the brigade... have caused a delay in this report').

clockProfile.assessment: 'About 4 p. m.' for the Latimer-support move agrees with Johnson and Geary; the assault carries no watch clock - only the lateness/darkness statements (after ~19:59 civil twilight end, ED-31), consistent with the ~19:00 contact + climb. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Reports of Brig. Gen. James A. Walker, Stonewall Brigade, Johnson's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 517-519, No. 488)
sources.author: James A. Walker
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 518-519 read; distinguish from or-27-2-walker = R. L. Walker, Third Corps artillery). THE EVENING NEGATIVE, self-attested: July 2 on 'Culp's farm' near the Hanover road; all-day long-range skirmishing; 'About 6 p. m. our line was advanced in a northerly direction, and took position immediately on the north side of the Hanover road'; Nadenbousch's 2d VA sent to clear the wheat-field/wood - 'the enemy opened on his line with two pieces of artillery... he reported that the enemy had a large force of cavalry [supposed to be two brigades], two regiments of infantry, and a battery of artillery' (the Brinkerhoff's Ridge action vs Gregg's cavalry, CSA-side primary); THE DISCRETION ORDER: 'received information from Major [H. K.] Douglas, of his staff, that the line was about to advance, with instructions from General Johnson to remain on the flank, if I thought it necessary. As our flank and rear would have been entirely uncovered... I deemed it prudent to hold my position until after dark, which I did. After dark, I withdrew... and joined the rest of the division in rear of the enemy's breastworks' (the brigade MISSED the evening assault - matching Johnson's 'did not arrive in time' division-side record); JULY 3: from daylight supporting Steuart on request, 'warmly engaged along my whole line, and my right, extending beyond the breastworks, suffered very heavily'; the five-hours firing, the rest-and-refit withdrawal, the renewed attack 'with equally bad success', the third advance keeping 'a desultory fire until dark'; Terry's 4th VA 'gallantly led his regiment almost to the breastworks... and only retired after losing three-fourths of his command' (vs the return's 4th VA 86 - scope conflict flagged, three-fourths reads as of the engaged fraction at the works).

clockProfile.assessment: 'About 6 p. m.' for the Hanover-road advance and 'after dark' for the withdrawal bracket the evening consistently with Johnson's sequence (the division stepped off while Walker held the flank); no chain anchor is defined by his clocks. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: N.C. Dept. of Natural & Cultural Resources: 'I.E. Avery's Words for His Father' (the Avery note, State Archives of North Carolina)
sources.author: NC DNCR
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11. The archival record of the Avery death note: Col. Isaac E. Avery (6th NC, commanding Hoke's brigade) 'shot in the neck and partially paralyzed' leading the July 2 attack on (East) Cemetery Hill; 'Though right handed, Avery was forced to write with his left because of paralysis'; the note verbatim: 'Major, tell my father that I died with my face to the enemy. I E Avery', addressed to Maj. Samuel McDowell Tate (6th NC), who stayed with him; died July 3; the original bloodstained note held by the State Archives, Raleigh. HONESTY NOTE: the popular 'written in the dark' framing is NOT attested on this archival page (the left-hand/paralysis detail is); carried accordingly.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Thomas H. Ruger, First Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 777-782)
sources.author: Thomas H. Ruger
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 778, 780-782 read). July 1: ordered 'about 9 a. m.' toward Gettysburg via the Baltimore turnpike; Culp's Hill line laid out (works of 'logs, rocks, and earth'; Rock Creek in front). July 2 evening: 'Around 6 p.m.' the division ordered left 'to support the general line, then hotly engaged'; ordered back before engagement (Dechert reported Sedgwick/Sykes said the attack was repulsed). JULY 3, THE DIVISION SPINE: 'At 4.30 o'clock on the morning of July 3, fire was opened on the position held by the enemy from a battery placed in position during the night in rear of the left of the then position of the division; also from a battery still farther to the right, on a commanding hill' (= CA-J3M-1 at division grain, agreeing with Muhlenberg's 4.30 program). THE CA-J3M-3 LATE POLE, verbatim: 'This state of things continued until about 10 a. m., the enemy maintaining the attack with great constancy... At this time I received orders to try the enemy on the right of the line of breastworks, to the left of the swale, with two regiments, and, if practicable, to force him out. I sent orders by a staff officer to Colonel Colgrove... to advance skirmishers against the enemy at that point, and, if not found in too great force, to advance two regiments, and dislodge him from the breastworks. From mistake of the staff officer, or misunderstanding on the part of Colonel Colgrove, it was attempted to carry the position without first ascertaining the force of the enemy' — the order-chain primary clocking the Spangler's Meadow order ~10 a.m. AND attesting the order corruption. The charge narrative: 'moved forward gallantly, crossed the swale in line under a severe fire, gained the woods on the opposite side, forced the enemy back part way up the slope to breastworks, but could not dislodge him'; withdrawal covered by supports; 'About 100 prisoners'; then the 123rd NY gained the stone wall, 'the enemy fell back over the breastworks', the division line 'at once advanced, and the breastworks reoccupied, the enemy retiring from under fire' — the charge is welded into the fight-ending sequence (CA-J3M-4). Afternoon: 1st Bde (Lockwood) sent to the left center 'At about 2 p. m.'; 13th NJ + 107th NY to Gregg's cavalry 'At about 5 p. m.' (returned 'about 9 a. m.' July 4).

clockProfile.assessment: His 4.30 artillery opening sits on Muhlenberg's stated program to the minute, and his fight-shape (10 a.m. escalation-end, works reoccupied) matches the quadruply-attested 10.30 close; treated as agreeing near-zero. Rule 4: never defines an anchor.

sources.title: Report of Col. Archibald L. McDougall, 1st Brigade, 1st Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 782-785)
sources.author: Archibald L. McDougall
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 783-784 read). July 2 evening: ordered left 'Late in the afternoon', 'proceeded about 1 1/2 miles to the left of the general line'; the NIGHT RETURN primary — skirmishers of the 123rd NY and 5th CT sent forward cautiously discovered 'the enemy had not only obtained possession of our works, and were occupying them in force, but had advanced into the woods south of them'; on retiring the brigade 'were fired upon by the enemy from the woods south of the works and immediately in front of our line' (temporary right-flank confusion, order restored); withdrew 'a short distance to the rear, where they were, in a measure, concealed by a rise of ground in front, and remained upon their arms until morning'. JULY 3: the 20th CT 'advanced into the woods in front of our troops, where the enemy had posted himself', fighting under friendly artillery fire passing overhead (men 'severely wounded by our artillery fire' — a friendly-fire record); the 123rd NY relieved it 'About 2 p. m.'(sic — the relief clock) and 'not finding any enemy... entered and then held the breastworks'. No brigade strength or casualty table in the fetched pages (return p. 184: 1/11 k, 4/56 w, 1/7 m = 80).

clockProfile.assessment: Few usable clocks in the fetched pages (the July 3 morning narrative is sequence-told); reason recorded per the kind-none convention.

sources.title: Report No. 282 of Brig. Gen. Henry H. Lockwood, 2nd Brigade, 1st Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 804-805)
sources.author: Henry H. Lockwood
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 804-805 read). The unattached-brigade arrival: 1st MD Potomac Home Brigade (Maulsby) + 150th NY (Ketcham) 'arrived, and reported to the First Division, Twelfth Army Corps, at 8 a. m. July 2' after 'a long and painful march from Baltimore, via Frederick City' (1st MD Eastern Shore joined July 3). July 2 evening: posted variously 'until about 5 p. m.', then marched left 'to support the left wing... deployed near a battery, then firing on the enemy' (the McGilvery-line episode ED-61 credits: A. S. Williams's 'recapturing three pieces of artillery' claim rides Lockwood's command grain); 'The command withdrew from the field by special order after full darkness set in.' JULY 3 MORNING: 'Early on the morning of the 3d, these regiments supported a battery placed to shell the woods in front of the rifle-pits on our right. At about 6 a. m. I received orders to deploy a regiment and engage the enemy within these woods' — the 6 a.m. woods-deployment order (Maulsby's 1st MD PHB; return p. 184 gives the brigade 174, the 1st MD PHB 104 of it — the morning woods fight's cost). Staff horses 'killed by shot and shell on the 3d'. Maulsby's own No. 283 begins p. 805 (double-quick ~1 mile leg, July 2).

clockProfile.assessment: Two round clocks (8 a.m. arrival July 2; about 6 a.m. deploy order July 3) with no anchor tension; the 6 a.m. order follows the 4.30-5.30 artillery program naturally. Single-source derivation.

sources.title: Report No. 286 of Col. Silas Colgrove, 3rd Brigade, 1st Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 812-814)
sources.author: Silas Colgrove
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 812-814 read; dated Aug 8 near Kelly's Ford). Took brigade command morning July 2 (Ruger up to the division). The Rock Creek terrain primary ('a small creek (Rocky Run) [Rock Creek], some 60 to 80 feet in width and from 6 to 8 feet deep'); July 2 evening left-wing move 'Shortly before sundown... about 1.5 miles'; return to the works 'about 10 p. m., as near as I can judge' with 2nd MA skirmishers taking 23 prisoners. JULY 3 / THE SPANGLER'S MEADOW EXECUTOR PRIMARY: 'Early on the morning of the 3d, before it was fairly light, the battle commenced on our left' (= the CA-J3M-1/2 opening, not the charge); prisoner intelligence 'that [John M.] Jones' and [George H.] Steuart's brigades occupied our works'; THE ORDER AS RECEIVED, verbatim: 'The general directs that you advance your line immediately' — delivered VERBALLY by Lieutenant Snow ('About this time', no clock) — vs Ruger's stated skirmishers-first intent (or-27-1-ruger): the order-corruption pair at both echelons. The no-skirmish decision: 'It was impossible to send forward skirmishers. The enemy's advantages were such that a line of skirmishers would be cut down before they could fairly gain the open ground... The only possible chance I had to advance was to carry his position by storming it.' Frontage: 'impossible... to advance more than two regiments in line', the meadow 'about 100 yards in width'; picked the 2nd MA (charge the works in front) + 27th IN (oblique right); 'Forward, double-quick!'; the 27th had 'nearly double the distance to traverse', 'at every volley of the enemy, gaps were being cut through its ranks'. Losses stated: 2nd MA 'about 130 killed and wounded' incl. 'Lieutenant-Colonel Mudge, who fell while gallantly leading his men in the charge'; 27th IN '112 in killed and wounded' incl. 8 officers (vs return 110 and Fesler's 111 — three-component conflict carried). Afternoon: under the cannonade 'shell, shot, and missiles bursting over them, around them, and among them for hours'. NOTE: the report attaches NO clock to the charge — the early-morning placement is narrative-positional only.

clockProfile.assessment: No clock on the charge (the report's one July 3 time-shape is 'before it was fairly light' for the general opening); the charge's time is the CA-J3M-3 dispute itself and is carried on the anchor's conflict record, not as a source skew.

sources.title: Report No. 287 of Lieut. Col. John R. Fesler, 27th Indiana (OR 27/1 pp. 815-816)
sources.author: John R. Fesler
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 815-816 read; dated Aug 8, Kelly's Ford). Strength primary: 339 officers and men carried into action. July 2: 8 a.m. into brigade position 'behind rock cliff'; 'About 6 p. m.' to the left; ~20 minutes there; back to former position. JULY 3, THE EARLY-POLE SEQUENCE: 'remained there unmolested until between 5 and 6 o'clock on the morning of the 3d, and then received orders from you to occupy the breastworks' (the 3rd WI's works); on arrival the sharpshooters opened; 'I was then ordered by you to charge their works. I immediately moved the regiment forward, but, on arriving within about 100 yards of their position, their fire was so deadly that I was compelled to fall back' — the regiment's own narrative places occupation-then-charge in one motion from the 5-6 a.m. order ('Remained there until about 8 a. m.' follows, cut in channel); 'kept up occasional firing on the 3d until about 4 p. m.; then, in accordance with your orders, made a reconnaissance to the right of Gettysburg' (the Gregg-support move). CASUALTY PRIMARY, itemized by episode: 'In the charge on the enemy on the 3d, loss in killed, 15; loss in wounded, including 7 commissioned officers, 83. Loss in killed in works, 3 enlisted men; loss in wounded, 10, including 1 commissioned officer. Total loss: Killed, 18; wounded, 93; total 111' — an EC6 charge-vs-works split primary (vs return 110 agg and Colgrove's '112 killed and wounded': components carried).

clockProfile.assessment: Its 'between 5 and 6 o'clock' occupation-then-charge sequence is the CA-J3M-3 dispute's early pole (with Morse's 5.30 and the 27th IN advance-marker 6 a.m.); profiling an offset would prejudge the anchor ruling, so the reading is carried on the anchor's conflict record instead (ED-64 candidate).

sources.title: Report No. 288 of Lieut. Col. Charles F. Morse, 2nd Massachusetts (OR 27/1 pp. 816-818)
sources.author: Charles F. Morse
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 816-818 read). July 1: Company F skirmish 'through the woods into an open meadow, and to within about 300 yards of the crest of a hill... occupied by the enemy's cavalry'. July 2: 'About 6 p. m. we left our position to go to the support of the left wing'; night: 'During the night breastworks were constructed along our new line.' JULY 3, THE EARLY-POLE CLOCK, verbatim: 'At daylight, July 3, our skirmishers, Company E, Captain Robeson, became engaged. Firing was kept up until 5.30 o'clock, when the regiment was ordered to charge the woods in front of us.' The charge: 'Colonel Mudge gave the order, "Forward!"... The men jumped over the breastworks with a cheer, and went forward on the double-quick.' THE MUDGE PIN: 'I now took command of the regiment, Colonel Mudge having been killed.' Withdrawal: '[the general] ordered me to bring the regiment back to the rear of its former position.' Casualties enumerated: '2 officers killed, 2 severely wounded, who have since died, and 6 other officers badly wounded, 21 men killed, 102 wounded, and 3 missing' (vs return 2/21 k, 8/101 w, 0/4 m = 136; monument 4+41 k&mw / 6+84 w; Colgrove 'about 130' — the four-component cluster carried). Supplementary one-liner by Col. William Cogswell p. 818: 294 enlisted men taken into action July 3 (+22 officers per the monument).

clockProfile.assessment: The 5.30 charge clock is the CA-J3M-3 dispute's sharpest early-pole primary (vs Ruger's about-10-a.m. order clock and Bachelder's 8-11 a.m. drawn adjudication); assigning an offset would resolve the dispute by fiat — carried on the anchor's conflict record instead (ED-64 candidate).

sources.title: Report of Col. Charles Candy, 1st Brigade, 2nd Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 835-838)
sources.author: Charles Candy
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 836-837 read). July 2 evening: 'The brigade remained in this position until about 7 p. m. the 2nd instant, when the brigade was ordered to the right, for the purpose of supporting a corps, supposed to be General Hancock's' (the wrong-road/wrong-object confusion at brigade grain — Geary's column); 'Remained in position until about 12 p. m.; ordered up to the former position on the right of the First Corps' (the midnight return; his 28th PA states 12.30 a.m. forward move, p. 845). JULY 3: 'Remained in the latter position until 3.45 a. m., when the enemy were opened upon by a battery placed for the purpose of shelling them' (an opening clock 45 min ahead of Muhlenberg's stated 4.30 program — the reveille-vs-program noise carried); the brigade's right regiments 'exposed to a harassing and heavy enfilading fire' (p. 837, the 66th OH outside-the-works episode at brigade grain). Regimental color: Powell's 66th OH over-the-works flanking deployment; Flynn's 28th PA night clock 'Remained in that position until 12.30 a. m. July 3, when the regiment moved forward to retake the position left the morning before.'

clockProfile.assessment: His 3.45 opening runs ~45 min ahead of the Muhlenberg/Ruger 4.30 program (plausibly the pre-program skirmish fire Cobham clocks at 3-4 a.m.); envelope reflects it. Weak single-page derivation.

sources.title: Report No. 302 of Brig. Gen. Thomas L. Kane, 2nd Brigade, 2nd Division, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 846-848)
sources.author: Thomas L. Kane
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 847-848 read). THE NIGHT-RETURN DISCOVERY PRIMARY (July 2, ~22:00, the CA-J2E-5-adjacent event Greene logs as Kane's two-volley return): 'On entering the wood, within 200 paces of our breastwork, we were met by a sharp fire, which we supposed to come from the First Brigade, misled by the darkness'; 'We moved directly to the position of the Third Brigade... and, making our way past Greene's right, were again fired upon, thus discovering that the enemy had entire possession of our works.' JULY 3: 'The attack in force upon us commenced at 3.30 a. m., July 3. The Confederate Major-General Johnson's division led, followed by Rodes'' — the receiving-side identification of the Rodes-division reinforcements; THE SEVEN-HOUR SPINE + THE 10.30 LAST EFFORT, verbatim: 'kept up a fire of unintermitting strength for seven hours, until about 10.30 o'clock, when the enemy made their last determined effort by charging in column of regiments' (= the Steuart/Daniel charge received; CA-J3M-2 wave 3 + CA-J3M-4's edge in one sentence). STRENGTH PRIMARY: 'The whole number of men of my brigade who were in this action was 652.' Loss stated: 'Our own loss was but 23 killed and 73 wounded' (= 96; vs Cobham's 23 k / 66 w / 9 m = 98 and return p. 184 — the brigade's two reports carry a component conflict, both kept).

clockProfile.assessment: His 3.30 attack-commencement matches Cobham's 3-to-4 skirmish escalation (the infantry fire preceding the 4.30 artillery program) and his 10.30 last-effort sits exactly on the quadruply-attested close; the seven-hours duration matches the Greene tablet.

sources.title: Report No. 303 of Col. George A. Cobham, Jr., 111th Pennsylvania / 2nd Brigade command (OR 27/1 pp. 848-849)
sources.author: George A. Cobham Jr.
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 848-849 read). The command-passage pin: 'About 6 o'clock on the morning of July 2, Brigadier-General Kane arrived on the field in an ambulance of the Second Army Corps, and assumed command of the brigade' (Kane's sick-arrival; Cobham re-assumed brigade command during July 3 per the OOB dual listing). JULY 3 OPENING LADDER: 'At 3 o'clock next morning, July 3, the enemy's skirmishers commenced firing on us, and by 4 o'clock the firing had become general along the whole line' — the skirmish-to-general escalation that precedes Muhlenberg's 4.30 artillery program (with Kane's 3.30 inside it). Loss stated: 'Our loss was 23 killed, 66 wounded, and 9 missing' (vs Kane's 23/73; component conflict carried).

clockProfile.assessment: The 3-then-4 o'clock escalation brackets Kane's 3.30 and lands the general engagement at the artillery program's eve; agreeing class, thin single-report derivation.

sources.title: Report of Lieut. Edward D. Muhlenberg, Artillery Brigade, XII Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 870-871)
sources.author: Edward D. Muhlenberg
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 870-871 read). THE CA-J3M-1 TABLET'S AUTHOR RECORD — the XII Corps artillery tablet's daylight-open / 15-min / 5.30-resume / 10.30-end ladder traces to this report: 'At 4.30 a. m. the two rifle batteries (ten guns) and the two light 12-pounder batteries (ten guns) opened', 'fired for fifteen minutes without intermission at a range of from 600 to 800 yards' (A. S. Williams's corps report repeats the 15-minutes-by-arrangement and 600-800 yards VERBATIM-CLASS), then 'Commenced at 5.30 a. m., and continued firing at intervals until 10 a. m.' — NOTE: the report's stated END is 10 a.m. vs the tablet's 10.30 A.M. (a tablet-vs-report 30-min end conflict, carried; Jacobs and the Johnson tablet side with 10.30). GUN-COUNT CONFLICT: the report's program is 20 guns (10 rifles + 10 Napoleons) vs the tablet's 26 — the tablet plausibly folds in Rigby's A 1st MD (Arty Reserve, Powers Hill, 6 guns); carried, not reconciled. Batteries: Knap's PA (Atwell; Lt. Geary section forward July 2 — 'one gun (10-pounder Parrott), and at 5 p. m. two more'), Battery M 1st NY (Winegar, McAllister's Hill ground), K 5th US (Kinzie; Van Reed's Napoleon section), F 4th US (Rugg) — 'Battery F, Fourth U. S. Artillery, and Battery K, Fifth U. S. Artillery, remained in park' July 2 (the July 2 park negative). July 2 counter-battery (the Latimer duel, receiving side): 'the enemy opened with eight guns; continued an incessant fire for some thirty minutes'; losses Geary 1 k 2 w, Van Reed 2 mw 3 w. NO rounds-expended figures in the report (verified negative; casualties 'Embodied in revised statement, p. 185' — 9 w aggregate).

clockProfile.assessment: The anchor's basis text: 4.30 open + 15-min program + 5.30 resume, corroborated verbatim-class by the corps commander's report and inherited by the tablet; his 10 a.m. end is the one 30-min divergence from the adopted 10.30 close (carried as conflict, envelope covers it).

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Junius Daniel, Daniel's Brigade, Rodes's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 566-569)
sources.author: Junius Daniel
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 566, 568-569 read). July 1 (context): the railroad-cut fight; 45th NC/2nd Bn/32nd NC 'to meet the heaviest efforts'; 'losing nearly one-third of their number' (p. 569) — the return's 916 is July-1-dominant. July 2: crest right of the railroad cut, O'Neal reporting to him; 'About 3.30 p. m. the enemy's artillery opened in reply to our own'; after sunset formed to support Doles/Iverson/Ramseur on Cemetery Hill — halted by Ramseur's impracticability call, back into town 'about 10 p. m.'. THE JULY 3 REINFORCEMENT MARCH, verbatim: 'Some two hours afterward, I received orders to move with my own and Rodes' brigade [O'Neal's] to the left of the town, a distance of about 4 miles, and report to General Johnson... I moved off at about 1.30 a. m., and reported to General Johnson at about 4 a. m., by whom I was immediately ordered into action, to the support of Jones' brigade, Colonel [R. H.] Dungan commanding' (the 1.30-4.00 night leg — physics-checkable; O'Neal to take orders from Johnson). THE ASSESSMENT PRIMARY: 'The hill in front of this position was, in my opinion, so strong that it could not have been carried by any force.' After 'some two or three hours' supporting Jones: moved by the left flank under staff guidance (troops 'much exposed, and many were killed and wounded'), then 'I received orders from General Johnson to charge the enemy's works, in conjunction with General Steuart. This charge was made in a most gallant manner, and the enemy driven from a portion of their works in front of my center and right, and near the works captured the evening before by Jones' brigade. Owing to the heavy fire brought upon General Steuart, he was unable to advance farther, and I was, therefore, unable to occupy the works of the enemy; but from a sheltered position, within less than [100 yards, cut in channel]' — the Steuart charge BOTH-SIDED from the co-charging brigade. p. 569: 'entered the fight again at 5 a. m.' (recap paragraph; vs his own 4 a.m. — intra-report 60-min noise carried) and 'not withdrawn until between 3 and 4 o'clock in the afternoon', skirmishers 'engaged until nearly 12 o'clock at night' — the east-of-Rock-Creek holding phase after CA-J3M-4. Officer pins: Bond and Green 'severely wounded on the first day'; Hammond commended.

clockProfile.assessment: His 1.30-4.00 a.m. march brackets arrival at the artillery program's eve (Kane's receiving-side 'followed by Rodes'' concurs); the 2-3-hours-then-charge sequence lands the Steuart/Daniel effort in the 8-10 window. The p. 569 recap's 5 a.m. is intra-report noise (envelope covers).

sources.title: Report of Col. Edward A. O'Neal, O'Neal's (Rodes's old) Brigade, Rodes's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 591-594)
sources.author: Edward A. O'Neal
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (pp. 592-593 read). July 1: the Oak Hill attack (fell back when the left regiment was flanked; the return's 696 is July-1-heavy). July 2 evening: 'About dusk on the evening of July 2, I was ordered to advance, and, after moving forward some distance, was recalled and placed in the town to bivouac for the night' (the aborted Cemetery Hill support — matching Daniel/Ramseur). THE JULY 3 REINFORCEMENT PRIMARY, verbatim: 'About 2 a. m. on July 3, I was ordered to move to the left of our lines, to re-enforce General Edward Johnson, and arrived there at daylight, and was soon under a severe fire of artillery and infantry, but did not actively engage the foe until 8 a. m., when I was ordered to attack the works of the enemy, strongly posted in a long fort on the spur of the mountain' — THE WAVE-2 EXECUTOR CLOCK (CA-J3M-2's second assault, the skeleton's softest clock, now carried by its executor at 8 a.m.). The attack: 'made with great spirit by the Sixth, Twelfth, Twenty-sixth, and Third Alabama Regiments... The brigade moved forward in fine style, under a terrific fire of grape and small-arms, and gained a hill near the enemy's works, which it held for three hours, exposed to a murderous fire' (8-11: the hold runs to the withdrawal). Withdrawal: 'held their ground until ordered to fall back with the entire line. We retired behind the hill, where we remained, under an incessant fire of artillery and musketry, till 12 o'clock at night, when I was ordered to withdraw and join my division' — the east-of-creek holding phase, released midnight.

clockProfile.assessment: His 2 a.m. start agrees with Daniel's 1.30; 'arrived at daylight' sits on the program's opening; the 8 a.m. attack is the wave-2 clock itself and his three-hour hold ends on the 10.30-11.00 close. The agreeing-cluster CSA morning source.

sources.title: Reports of Col. William Gamble, 1st Brigade, 1st Cavalry Division (OR 27/1 pp. 933-936, No. 338)
sources.author: William Gamble
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com part-i route p. 934 + IA full text warofrebellionco27unit, OCR-grade, pp. 933-936 read in full; the two channels agree). Campaign report dated August 24 with per-action sections. GETTYSBURG JULY 1 section verbatim: 'About 8 o'clock on the morning of the 1st instant, while in camp at the seminary building, the officer commanding the squadron on picket in front gave me notice that the enemy, consisting of infantry and artillery, in column, were approaching his pickets from the direction of Cashtown, with deployed skirmishers in strong force, about 3 miles distant' - THE CA-J1M-2 EXECUTOR CLOCK at brigade grain. Strength primary: 'about 1,600 strong, with Tidball's battery, Second U.S. Artillery' (8th NY, 8th Ill, three squadrons 3d Ind, two squadrons 12th Ill). The delay line drawn: 'placed in line of battle about 1 mile in front of the seminary, the right resting on the railroad track and the left near the Middletown or Fairfax [Fairfield] road, the Cashtown road being a little to the right of the center, at right angles with the line' (the OR print itself carries the [Fairfield] correction). The fight: fell back 'only about 200 yards from the first line of battle' before 'our infantry advance of the First Corps arrived, and relieved the cavalry brigade in its unequal contest' - 'After checking and retarding the advance of the enemy several hours'; 'opposed by three divisions of Hill's corps' (an overcount, carried as stated). THE AFTERNOON LEFT-FLANK STAND: ordered 'forward at a trot, and deployed in line on the ridge of woods, with the seminary on our right', half the command dismounted 'behind a portion of a stone wall and under cover of trees'; the carbine fire threw the first CSA line back on its second; mounted withdrawal 'to the next ridge, on the left of the town'; his claim: 'prevented our left flank from being turned, and saved a division of our infantry.' Named officer pins: Maj. Lemon (3d Ind) mw; Lt. Conroe (12th Ill) k; Capts. Fisher/Voss/Follett/Martin/Morris w. Calef credit ('worked faithfully'). The closer: 'This brigade had the honor to commence the fight in the morning and close it in the evening.'

clockProfile.assessment: One clock: 'About 8 o'clock' picket warning sits at the front edge of Buford's 'between 8 and 9 a.m.' report window and inside CA-J1M-2's adopted 08:00-09:00; agreeing, single-statement envelope held at class default minus the corroboration.

sources.title: Report of Col. Thomas C. Devin, 2nd Brigade, 1st Cavalry Division (OR 27/1 pp. 938-941, No. 339)
sources.author: Thomas C. Devin
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 938-939 + IA full text, channels agree). Dated August 6. June 30: 'marched by Emmitsburg to Gettysburg, Pa., and encamped.' July 1 morning: First Brigade pickets on the Cashtown road 'driven in by a heavy force advancing from that direction'; formed 'on the crest of the hill on the right of the First Brigade... my right resting on the road to Mummasburg', connecting 'by skirmishers and vedettes with my pickets on the three roads on the right leading toward Carlisle, thus establishing a continuous line from the York road, on the extreme right, to the left of the First Brigade, on the Cashtown road' - THE FOUR-ROAD SCREEN GEOMETRY PRIMARY. The Heidlersburg front: skirmishers on the right 'forced back by the advance of the enemy's line of battle, coming from the direction of Heidlersburg' (Rodes/Early approach axis); 'succeeded in holding the rebel line in check for two hours, until relieved by the arrival of the Eleventh Corps' - THE CA-J1M-7-ADJACENT DURATION at executor grain (sequence-clocked, no watch time anywhere in the July 1 text). The withdrawal ladder: successive formations in line to the rear by regiment; the York-road post; THE FRIENDLY-FIRE RECORD: 'a heavy fire of shells was opened on us from one of our own batteries on Cemetery Hill, immediately in my rear... I immediately removed my command into the town, the column being shelled the whole distance'; the York-road CSA entry and the 9th NY dismounted counter-charge ('drove them some distance into the town, punishing them severely'); 'then ordered to the extreme left, where it bivouacked for the night.' July 2 morning: reconnoitering 'in rear of the enemy's right' when sharpshooters engaged 'a division of the enemy advancing to feel our lines'; two squadrons dismounted supporting Berdan; relieved by III Corps arrival, ordered to Taneytown - the Pitzer Woods both-sided counterpart (see us-iii-1-1-graham/berdan frame, CA-J2A-1 vicinity).

clockProfile.assessment: No clock statement in the entire Gettysburg narrative (durations and sequence only - 'for two hours', 'the next morning'); kind-none per the pass-9 convention, reason recorded.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. James S. Wadsworth, 1st Division, I Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 265-267, No. 29)
sources.author: James S. Wadsworth
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 265-266 + IA full text pp. 265-267 in full). THE UNION MORNING CLOCK LADDER: 'On the morning of July 1, at 8 a.m., the division moved from Marsh Creek on Gettysburg'; 'struck the Cashtown road about three quarters of a mile west of Gettysburg at about 10 a.m.'; deployment ('Three regiments of the Second Brigade were ordered to deploy on the right of the road...'); 'The right became sharply engaged before the line was formed, and at this time (about 10.15 a.m.) our gallant leader fell, mortally wounded' - THE REYNOLDS PIN IN A COMMANDER PRIMARY, agreeing with the WD 1st-Corps-tablet 10.15 to the minute (CA-J1M-5). The railroad-cut credit (14th NYSM Fowler / 6th Wis Dawes / 95th NY, 'captured a large number of prisoners, including two entire regiments with their flags'). Afternoon (as quoted in Doubleday's report p. 251): Stewart's battery up 'about 3 p.m.'; 'At about 2.30 p.m. Major-General Schurz... fell back after partially engaging the enemy, and left our right exposed' (the EARLY counter-reading against the ~16:00 XI-collapse frame - a recorded conflict, not adopted); 'I ordered the command to retire at 3.45 o'clock' - THE I CORPS RIGHT-WING RETIREMENT CLOCK (sits inside CA-J1P-5's 15:45-16:45 envelope at the front edge); 'at least half of the officers and men who went into the engagement were killed or wounded.' Evening: 'ordered to occupy a hill on the right of the cemetery' (Culp's Hill) 'which we held on the 2d and 3d.' Also the Howard direct-order note: 'I received orders direct from Major-General Howard to hold Seminary Ridge as long as possible.'

clockProfile.assessment: His 10.15 Reynolds statement IS the tablet's adopted minute (offset 0 by agreement); his 3.45 retirement sits at the front edge of CA-J1P-5 and his 2.30 Schurz-fell-back reading runs ~60-90 early against the XI-collapse frame - the envelope reflects that early pull; report-nominal caps tier per ED-25 rule 4.

sources.title: Report of Brig. Gen. Lysander Cutler, 2nd Brigade, 1st Division, I Corps (OR 27/1 pp. 281-285, No. 37)
sources.author: Lysander Cutler
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 281-282 + IA full text pp. 281-285 in full). Dated July 9 (early, low-litigation class). March: 'moved from camp early on the 1st instant (being the leading brigade of the corps)'; deployed across the railroad with 76th NY / 147th NY / 56th Pa 'immediately formed in line of battle, and found myself engaged with a vastly superior force of the enemy, advancing in two lines, at short range, in front and on my right flank' (95th NY + 14th Brooklyn detached left BY REYNOLDS's own order to support Hall - the general's last tactical act in a brigade primary). THE 147TH NY RECORD: Miller w at the moment of the order, 'Major Harney held the regiment to its position until the enemy were in possession of the railroad cut on his left'; loss '2 officers killed and 10 wounded, 42 men killed and 153 wounded - 207 out of 380 men and officers within half an hour' (RATE-CLASS loss primary). 76th NY: 'went in with 348 men and 27 officers', lost 169 'within thirty minutes' (Maj. Grover k). 56th Pa: 17 officers + 235 men, lost 78+ 'at that point.' THE AFTERNOON FRAME: reoccupied the old ground 'from half to three-quarters of an hour'; changed front right 'having no support on either my right or left until 2 o'clock, when a brigade from the Second Division formed on my right, and the Eleventh Corps came in on the right of them' - the 2-o'clock Baxter/XI-arrival sync; the flank volley that made 'one regiment [throw] down their arms and surrender' (Iverson's front, both-sided); the railroad-embankment retreat under fire on both flanks; 'having been in action from 10 a.m. until 4 p.m.' - THE BRIGADE'S OWN WINDOW STATEMENT bracketing CA-J1M-4 to CA-J1P-5. July 2-3: 'between the First Brigade and General Greene's brigade of the Twelfth Corps'; 'the fighting on those days was mostly in the trenches.' Colors: 147th NY color-sergeant k, saved by Sgt. Wybourn (w).

clockProfile.assessment: His 10 a.m. action-start agrees with Wadsworth's 10 a.m. arrival and the 10.15 pin's preamble; his 2 o'clock right-support statement is the Rodes-front opening read from the receiving side (CA-J1P-1 adopted 14:00); his 4 p.m. end agrees with the corps frame. Three agreeing statements, tight envelope, tier capped by class.

sources.title: Reports of Lieut. Col. Rufus R. Dawes, 6th Wisconsin (OR 27/1 pp. 275-278, No. 35)
sources.author: Rufus R. Dawes
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 275-276 + IA full text pp. 275-278 in full; ED-26's 'Dawes VERIFIED time-silent' now page-verified). Dated July 17. The reserve detachment: halted by Doubleday's order 'and detached from the brigade as a general reserve to the line of the division' with the 100-man brigade guard attached. THE RAILROAD-CUT CHARGE PRIMARY: the fence fire-by-file 'checked the advance of the rebels, who took refuge in a railroad cut'; the charge 'upon a double-quick, well closed, in face of a terribly destructive fire'; 'When our line reached the edge of the cut, the rebels began throwing down their arms in token of surrender'; Adjt. Brooks's 20-man enfilade detachment; 'Maj. John A. Blair, commanding the regiment (Second Mississippi Volunteers), upon my demand, surrendered his sword and regiment to me' - the surrender scene BOTH-SIDED with Davis's report; '7 officers and about 225 men' to the cavalry guard; the 2d Miss flag taken pre-surrender by Corpl. F. Asbury Waller (Medal of Honor, compiler's note). Loss: 'not less than 160 men killed or wounded' in the charge; July 1 total 2+5 officers / 27 k, 100 w, 24 m men. The 2d Maine gun recaptured in the charge (Capt. Converse credit). The retreat: faced by the rear rank past the railroad embankment, the through-town crush ('streets were crowded with retiring troops, batteries, and ambulance trains'), 'hearty cheers for the old Sixth'; to Culp's Hill reporting to Col. W. W. Robinson 'now commanding the brigade' (the Meredith succession pin). NO clock anywhere - the morning's physics rides Wadsworth/Doubleday pins.

clockProfile.assessment: Time-silent throughout (the ED-26 verified negative, now page-verified); sequence evidence only.

sources.title: Report of Col. Henry A. Morrow, 24th Michigan, Meredith's brigade (OR 27/1 pp. 267-271, No. 33)
sources.author: Henry A. Morrow
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 267-268 + IA full text pp. 267-271 in full). Written February 22, 1864 from Culpeper (Morrow was wounded and captured in the town; late-report caveat). March clock: 'At an early hour on July 1, we marched in the direction of Gettysburg, distant 6 or 7 miles... about 9 a.m. we came near the town of Gettysburg, and filed off to the left' - THE I CORPS APPROACH CLOCK from a regiment primary. The unloaded charge: ordered forward 'no order being given or time allowed for loading our guns' (staff-officer override recorded); 'the brigade dashed up and over the hill and down into the ravine, through which flows Willoughby's Run, where we captured a large number of prisoners, being a part of General Archer's brigade' (24th Mich extreme left, 19th Ind on its right). Color-bearer Abel G. Peck k at the run. The McPherson's-woods line: 'changed front forward on first battalion', 19th Ind left / 7th Wis right, the curved right wing; the hold order: 'the position was ordered to be held, and must be held at all hazards.' The retreat: 'forced back, step by step, contesting every foot of ground, to the barricade' west of the seminary, where Morrow w; 'orders were received to fall back, given, I believe, by Major-General Doubleday.' STRENGTH PRIMARY: 496 (3 field + 1 staff + 24 line officers + 468 NCOs/privates). LOSS: his table 316 (with 'about 80... missing' additional, many 'undoubtedly killed'), print note 'But see revised statement, p. 173' -> return row 363 aggregate; 'exceeding, perhaps, the losses sustained by any one regiment of equal size in a single engagement of this or any other war.' FOUR color-bearers k (Peck, Ballare, Ernest, Kelly), flag carried by NINE men, all color guard k/w; officer ledger: 8 officers k, 14 w (Flanigan leg, Wight eye...). Culp's Hill July 2-4 'little or no loss.'

clockProfile.assessment: One clock ('about 9 a.m.' near the town) - consistent with Wadsworth's 10 a.m. Cashtown-road strike given the approach leg; single statement, class default envelope; 7.5-month retrospective distance noted.

sources.title: Reports of Maj. Gen. Carl Schurz, 3rd Division / XI Corps command July 1 (OR 27/1 pp. 727-731, No. 253)
sources.author: Carl Schurz
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com p. 727 + IA full text pp. 727-731 in full). Dated August 20, addressed to Howard ('you'). THE XI CORPS AFTERNOON CLOCK LADDER: left Emmitsburg 7 a.m.; 'About 10.30 o'clock... I received, through one of your aides, the order to hurry forward my command as fast as possible'; 'I hastened to the front, where I arrived about 11.30 o'clock, finding you upon an eminence east of the cemetery... You informed me that General Reynolds had just been killed; that you were in command of the whole, and that you had to turn over the Eleventh Corps to me' - THE HOWARD-ASSUMES-COMMAND SCENE WITH A CLOCK, agreeing with Howard's own 'about 11.30 a.m.' (the sub-anchor's second primary; it also RESOLVES the XI-tablet's '10.30' as the hurry-forward ORDER, not the command handoff - conflict-collapse recorded); 'It was about 2 p.m. when the deployment of the two divisions was accomplished' (Schimmelfennig right of I Corps in two lines; Barlow First Division right of Third, 2d Bde ordered en echelon east of the Mummasburg road); 'Soon afterward, however, about 3 o'clock... the enemy appeared in our front with heavy masses of infantry, his line extending far beyond our right' - THE RECEIVING CLOCK FOR CA-J1P-3, pairing with Gordon's 'About 3 p.m.' order clock ACROSS THE LINES; 'It was after 5 o'clock when the Eleventh Corps occupied the position on Cemetery Hill.' EC2: 'the two small divisions under my command, numbering hardly over 6,000 effective men when going into battle.' The Barlow record: Barlow 'had moved forward his whole line, thus losing on his left the connection with the Third Division', the en-echelon brigade taken out of position, the two-battery enfilade from the Harrisburg road, Barlow w ('had to be carried off'), Ames succeeds. The Coster deployment ('ten minutes before' regret), the through-town retreat ('streets being filled with vehicles... overrun with men of the First Corps'; 'A considerable number of men, who became entangled in cross streets and alleys, were taken prisoners'; Schimmelfennig's hiding recorded), and the Cemetery Hill posting (2d Div behind the west stone walls, 3d opposite the town, 1st right).

clockProfile.assessment: Four-clock ladder: 11.30 agrees with Howard's assumption statement; ~3 o'clock attack-received pairs with Gordon's 3 p.m. order clock (cross-line agreement); after-5 Cemetery-Hill completion agrees with Howard's 4.30-columns + reform interval. Agreeing cluster, envelope tightened below class default.

sources.title: Report of Maj. Gen. R. E. Rodes, Rodes's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 549-563, No. 506)
sources.author: Robert E. Rodes
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 552-553 + IA full text warofrebellionco272unit pp. 549-563; the ED-27 'NO July 1 clocks' negative now page-verified across the whole July 1 narrative). THE OAK HILL COMMAND PRIMARY: the Middletown turn by Ewell's order on hearing Hill was moving on Gettysburg; 'When within 4 miles of the town, to my surprise, the presence of the enemy there in force was announced by the sound of a sharp cannonade'; the ridge approach ('moved along the summit of the ridge... finally, with three brigades deployed; Doles on the left, Rodes' (old) brigade, Colonel O'Neal commanding, in the center, and Iverson on the right'); the surprise read ('He had apparently been surprised... no troops facing me at all') collapsing as 'the enemy began to show large bodies of men in front of the town'; the attack decision ('Being thus threatened from two directions, I determined to attack with my center and right'), Doles holding the plain for Early's arrival ('which I knew would be soon'); THE O'NEAL FAILURE ANATOMY: the temporary line shift, the 5th Ala retained under Rodes himself, the 3d Ala released to Daniel's line by O'Neal, 'went into action in some confusion, and with only three of its regiments... (but not in accordance with my orders as to direction)'; 'Colonel O'Neal, instead of personally superintending the movements of his brigade, had chosen to remain with his reserve regiment. The result was that the whole brigade... was repulsed quickly, and with loss'; THE IVERSON DESTRUCTION SENTENCE: 'Iverson's left being thus exposed, heavy loss was inflicted upon his brigade. His men fought and died like heroes. His dead lay in a distinctly marked line of battle. His left was overpowered, and many of his men, being surrounded, were captured'; Daniel's railroad-cut fight and 'most desperate, gallant, and entirely successful charge'; Ramseur 'hurled by its commander... with irresistible force, upon the enemy just where he had repulsed O'Neal and checked Iverson's advance'; Doles relieved by Early's arrival attacking 'without waiting for orders'; the Halsey rally of Iverson's remnant; Fry's battery pushed after Ramseur; the through-town pursuit (Doles 'penetrated the heart of the town first of all'; Ramseur's Lt. F. M. Harney took the 150th Pa colors 'falling almost immediately thereafter, mortally wounded' - the pass-4 Stone-brigade colors record now both-sided); 'In the pursuit, the division captured about 8,500 [print/OCR reads 8,500 - IA text; verify digit at owner review; his own division loss stated 2,500 below] prisoners - so many as to embarrass its movements materially'; THE EVENING NON-ATTACK RATIONALE, verbatim reasons 1st/2d (the do-not-bring-on-a-general-engagement order relayed mid-fight; the heights 'quite a formidable line of infantry and artillery' by the time his line could renew; 'To have attacked this line with my division alone, diminished as it had been by a loss of 2,500 men, would have been absurd'; Early met 'in the streets of the town within thirty minutes after its occupation'; no orders though 'my superiors were upon the ground') - THE CA-J1P-9 (Ewell declines) COMPANION RECORD at division grain; the two-line evening posture (Doles-Iverson-Ramseur front line, Daniel-O'Neal on the railroad 200 yards rear); July 2: the aborted dark co-attack with Early (1,200-1,400 yards traverse vs Early's half; Ramseur's halt-and-report; 'a recall was ordered'), the old-roadbed forward line, the Johnson reinforcement order (pass-9 frame confirmed).

clockProfile.assessment: NO clock statement in the entire July 1 narrative (page-verified; distances and sequence only) - the ED-27 verified negative carried onto the source record; the 14:00 CA-J1P-1 rides the WD 1st-Corps tablet, not this report.

sources.title: Reports of Brig. Gen. Alfred Iverson, Iverson's Brigade, Rodes's Division (OR 27/2 pp. 578-581, No. 513)
sources.author: Alfred Iverson
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-12 (civilwar.com pp. 578-579 + IA full text pp. 578-581 in full; two reports, July 17 from Darkesville). THE DESTRUCTION PRIMARY, his own words: brigade led the division's advance; Rodes 'took upon himself the direction of the brigade'; the advance order chain (don't move 'until my skirmishers became hotly engaged', then the battery-rescue order with O'Neal to advance on his left and Daniel in support); 'I advanced at once, and soon came in contact with the enemy, strongly posted in woods and behind a concealed stone wall. My brigade advanced to within 100 yards, and a most desperate fight took place'; the unfilled left gap; the Daniel support miscarriage ledger (the promised regiment formed on the 3d Ala's right instead, 'could not be used in time to save my brigade'); O'Neal's brigade 'almost instantaneously driven back, upon which the enemy... charged in overwhelming force upon and captured nearly all that were left unhurt in three regiments of my brigade'; THE SURRENDER-AND-EXONERATION RECORD: 'When I saw white handkerchiefs raised, and my line of battle still lying down in position, I characterized the surrender as disgraceful; but when I found afterward that 500 of my men were left lying dead and wounded on a line as straight as a dress parade, I exonerated, with one or two disgraceful individual exceptions, the survivors, and claim for the brigade that they nobly fought and died without a man running to the rear. No greater gallantry and heroism has been displayed during this war' - THE EC6 SHAPE PRIMARY (one episode, minutes-scale, line-static: the exemplar of a casualty distribution with a single spike); the failed rally ('in the noise and excitement I presume my voice could not be heard'); the 12th NC intact under Lt. Col. Davis; Capt. D. P.
